papers.title: Elevated indoor CO2 degrades cognitive performance — and ventilation rate is the design lever
papers.excerpt: The air in your office may be making you dumber. This 2021 study in *Building and Environment* measured the effects of elevated indoor CO2 concentrations on cognitive performance, adding to a growing evidence base that ventilation rates in buildings have cognitive consequences that current standards do not adequately address. Indoor CO2 concentration serves as both a marker for overall indoor air quality (it correlates with occupant density and ventilation adequacy) and a direct physiological variable — CO2 itself affects cerebral blood flow, acid-base balance, and neural function at concentrations commonly found in occupied buildings. The study exposed participants to controlled CO2 levels while measuring performance on standardized cognitive tasks. The results confirmed a dose-response relationship. At CO2 concentrations of 1000 ppm — a level routinely exceeded in conference rooms, classrooms, and densely occupied offices — cognitive performance on decision-making and complex reasoning tasks was measurably impaired compared to performance at outdoor baseline levels (~400 ppm). At 2500 ppm — a level reached in poorly ventilated meeting rooms within 30–60 minutes — the impairment w

papers.title: An architectural intervention reduces the stress of high-density dormitory living — corridor bisection restores perceived control
papers.excerpt: Can you fix crowding stress by bisecting a corridor? Andrew Baum and Glenn Davis showed in a landmark 1980 study in the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology that a simple architectural modification — dividing a long dormitory corridor into two shorter segments with walls and doors — reduced crowding stress, restored perceived control, and normalized social behavior among residents. The intervention cost nothing in density, nothing in building area, and nothing in bed count. It cost only the price of some walls and doors. Yet it eliminated the psychological damage that characterized the unmodified long corridor. The study compared residents across three dormitory floor configurations at Stony Brook University: a long corridor (approximately 40 residents sharing a single corridor with bedrooms on both sides), a short corridor (approximately 20 residents in a naturally shorter wing), and a long-corridor-intervention floor where the long corridor had been physically bisected by walls and doors, creating two semi-independent groups of approximately 20 residents each. papers.title: The density paradox: when walkable neighborhoods become too crowded to walk
papers.excerpt: Pack more people into a neighborhood and they walk more — until they walk less. Koohsari, Yasunaga, Veitch, Kaczynski, and Oka name this the density paradox: higher physical density simultaneously promotes walking and provokes psychological stress, and beyond some threshold the stress wins. Their 2024 commentary in *Cities & Health* synthesizes evidence from urban design, public health, and environmental psychology to frame the problem. Physical density — the number of dwelling units or residents in a given area — is measurable, politically charged, and newly urgent after COVID-19 forced cities to rethink crowding, social distancing, and the allocation of public space. The evidence for density's benefits is well-established. Neighborhoods with higher population density support more transport-related walking and cycling (Omura et al. 2020; Kärmeniemi et al. 2018). But this relationship is not linear. A study of the Seoul Metropolitan area found that walking behavior dropped sharply once density reached 9,132–16,101 persons per km² (Eom and Cho 2015). A California study identified a threshold near 101 housing units per acre, above which walking for transport no longer increased (Wali

papers.title: Acoustic design in architecture: Balancing aesthetics, open-plan challenges, and soundscape quality
papers.excerpt: Architecture shapes sound as thoroughly as it shapes light — but acoustic design rarely receives the same attention as visual design in the architectural process. This review examined how architectural design decisions affect acoustic outcomes in buildings, focusing on three themes that define the contemporary acoustic challenge: the tension between aesthetic and acoustic goals, the specific acoustic problems created by open-plan layouts, and the emerging paradigm of soundscape quality as a design target beyond noise control. Achieving optimal acoustic performance requires a delicate balance between aesthetic ambitions and functional acoustic requirements. Hard, visually striking materials — exposed concrete, glass, polished stone, metal cladding — that define contemporary architectural aesthetics are also the materials that reflect sound most efficiently, creating reverberant, noisy interiors. Soft, acoustically absorptive materials — carpet, textile panels, perforated ceilings, upholstered furniture — that provide acoustic comfort are often perceived by architects as visually inferior, domestically coded, or stylistically limiting. papers.title: Light transitions between warm-dim and cool-bright affect alertness, comfort, and mood — temporal dynamics matter
papers.excerpt: A light switch is not just a light switch — it is a physiological transition with temporal dynamics that architects and lighting designers rarely consider. Imagine arriving at your office at 8:00 AM. The automated circadian lighting system ramps from warm, dim light (2700 K, 100 lux) to cool, bright light (6500 K, 1000 lux) over ten seconds. For a moment you squint, the room feels harsh, and your comfort plummets. Then, within fifteen minutes, you adapt — and you feel more alert than you did in the warm-dim setting. Kompier, Smolders, van Marken Lichtenbelt, and de Kort demonstrated in a 2020 study in Physiology & Behavior that these asymmetric temporal patterns are not random. Stepping up in light level is not the mirror image of stepping down, and the timing of adaptation determines whether a lighting transition helps or harms the occupant. The study used a controlled within-subjects design. Thirty-eight healthy participants (later reduced to 30 in some analyses due to data quality) each completed four 90-minute sessions in a climate-controlled laboratory: constant warm-dim (WD: ~2700 K, ~100 lux), constant cool-bright (CB: ~6500 K, ~1000 lux), a warm-to-cool transition (WD→CB),
papers.authors: Kompier, M., Smolders, K., van Marken Lichtenbelt, W. & de Kort, Y.

papers.title: Daylight through windows benefits health, mood, and productivity — but the mechanisms are multiple and hard to separate
papers.excerpt: People prefer daylight. Ask building occupants what they value most about their workspace and windows consistently rank at or near the top — above temperature, noise control, and spatial layout. Peter Boyce, Claudia Hunter, and Owen Howlett, writing for the Lighting Research Center at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute in 2003, reviewed the evidence for why this preference exists and whether it reflects genuine health and performance benefits or merely aesthetic taste. The review, commissioned by the US Department of Energy and several state energy agencies, examined the full spectrum of daylight's effects: visual performance, circadian entrainment, psychological wellbeing, and potential health risks (UV exposure through glazing). Its most important conclusion is that daylight's benefits cannot be justified by energy savings alone — the economic case for daylighting depends on demonstrating productivity and health gains. The circadian pathway provides the strongest mechanistic evidence. Daylight is the primary Zeitgeber (time-giver) for the human circadian system. papers.title: The human perceptual system needs organic geometry in buildings — and modernism denied it
papers.excerpt: Buildings that ignore how human perception works make their occupants feel disconnected, uncomfortable, and psychologically adrift. Nikos Salingaros and Kenneth Masden argue in this 2006 chapter — presented at Yale's Bringing Buildings to Life Conference and published in *Biophilic Design: The Theory, Science and Practice of Bringing Buildings to Life* — that the key mechanism is informational connectivity: the degree to which a building's surfaces provide the kind of structured visual information that the human nervous system evolved to process. The argument begins with a contrast between three conceptions of the human being in architecture. The mechanical human of 20th-century modernism is an abstraction: a body that requires shelter, light, and a certain floor area, but has no biological need for particular visual patterns. The biological human — the one that neuroscience reveals — requires specific kinds of sensory input to feel at ease: fractal geometry, hierarchical scaling, symmetries at multiple scales, and organic curvature. The transcendental human — reaching for meaning through architectural experience — emerges only when the biological requirements are met. Salingaros a

papers.title: Cognitive wayfinding principles should constrain architectural design — Le Corbusier ignored them
papers.excerpt: Human beings navigate buildings using cognitive maps — internal spatial representations that encode landmarks, routes, and the survey-level geometry of the environment. When a building's spatial structure aligns with how these maps are constructed, navigation is effortless. When it conflicts, people get lost, feel anxious, and waste time. Steffen Werner and Paul Long argue in this 2003 chapter in *Spatial Cognition III* that architectural design has largely ignored what cognitive science knows about wayfinding — and that Le Corbusier's influential design principles may be among the worst offenders. The cognitive science of wayfinding rests on a well-established hierarchy. People first learn landmarks — distinctive, recognizable features at decision points. Then they learn routes — sequences of landmark-to-landmark movements. Finally, some people construct survey knowledge — a map-like representation of the overall spatial layout that allows flexible navigation through novel shortcuts. Not everyone reaches the survey stage; many people navigate by route knowledge alone, which fails catastrophically when a familiar route is blocked. papers.title: Coherence, fascination, and hominess — three dimensions of psychological response to architectural interiors with distinct neural signatures
papers.excerpt: People reduce the visual complexity of architectural interiors to three psychological dimensions — and each dimension activates a different brain region. Alexander Coburn, Oshin Vartanian, and colleagues demonstrated in a 2020 study in *Cortex* that when 798 participants rated 200 photographs of real architectural interiors on 16 aesthetic response measures, three principal components emerged: coherence (the ease of understanding a scene), fascination (informational richness that generates interest), and hominess (the sense that a scene could be a personal space). These three dimensions explained 90% of the variance in aesthetic response. The three-component structure replicated in an independent sample of 614 participants, confirming its robustness. The components map onto established landscape preference theory — coherence and fascination parallel Kaplan and Kaplan's coherence-complexity framework — but hominess is a new dimension specific to architectural interiors, reflecting the uniquely personal relationship humans have with built spaces. The architectural variables that drive each dimension differ systematically. Ceiling height increases fascination, F(1, 192) = 13.56, p < .

papers.title: Biophilic design and the savanna hypothesis: Evolutionary landscape preference and its built-environment applications
papers.excerpt: The landscape that feels most right to most humans is one that most humans have never lived in. The savanna hypothesis proposes that the landscape preferences of modern Homo sapiens were shaped during the Pleistocene on the East African savanna — a landscape characterized by scattered trees, open grassland, water visibility, and long-distance prospect — and that these preferences persist as evolved dispositions that influence how people respond to designed environments. This review traces the evidence for savanna-based landscape preference and its implications for biophilic design in the built environment. The foundational claim is straightforward. Exposure to natural landscape elements in the built environment produces positive affective and cognitive responses — improved mood, reduced stress, faster psychological recovery, and enhanced attention. These responses are not culturally constructed but biologically grounded: they reflect evolutionary adaptations that favored organisms who preferred environments with the resource and safety characteristics that natural landscapes signaled. The savanna hypothesis provides the specific evolutionary context: early humans spent millions of

papers.title: Maritime environments and seafarer psychology: Confinement, isolation, and design implications for extreme settings
papers.excerpt: Maritime environments present unique psychological challenges including confinement, isolation, and extreme conditions that affect organizational behavior and health outcomes of seafarers. Ships are among the most spatially constrained inhabited environments on Earth — workers live and work in the same compressed footprint for weeks to months, with no control over when they can leave, no access to green space, limited natural light in interior compartments, and constant low-frequency vibration and machinery noise. This edited volume examines the psychological, ergonomic, and design dimensions of life at sea, with implications that extend to any confined, isolated, and controlled (ICC) environment: submarines, offshore platforms, space stations, Antarctic research bases, and — more relevantly for most architects — hospitals, prisons, dormitories, and long-term care facilities. The volume documents programmatic approaches to building psychological resilience in confined environments. The Shell Health Resilience Programme, consisting of 12 modules on positive psychology, demonstrated that structured psychological training can support well-being in extreme occupational settings. papers.title: Blue light from electronic devices: Circadian disruption, melatonin suppression, and health risks for adolescents
papers.excerpt: The light that keeps adolescents awake is the light from the device in their hands. This review examined the health consequences of exposure to blue light from electronic equipment — LEDs, laptops, smartphones, and tablets — focusing on circadian disruption, melatonin suppression, and the downstream metabolic, cardiovascular, and cognitive risks that chronic light exposure at the wrong time of day produces. The photobiological mechanism is now well established. Short-wavelength light near 480 nm activates melanopsin-expressing intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) in the inner retina. These cells project to the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), the brain's master circadian clock, signaling "daytime" regardless of the actual time. When this signal arrives in the evening — from a smartphone screen viewed in bed, from bright LED room lighting, from a laptop used for late-night study — it suppresses or delays the nocturnal melatonin rise that prepares the body for sleep. The melatonin suppression finding is acute and dramatic. Intense blue light exposure cancelling the melatonin night peak requires only 10–20 minutes of exposure at sufficient intensity and spectral

papers.title: Attitudes, intentions, and energy-saving behavior: A systematic review of the psychological determinants
papers.excerpt: Positive attitudes toward saving energy predict intentions to save energy — but intentions do not reliably predict actual behavior. This systematic review examined the psychological determinants of energy-saving behavior in buildings, revealing a gap between what people think they should do and what they actually do, with architectural design positioned as a critical moderator that can either close or widen that gap. The attitude-intention link is well established. People who believe that energy conservation is important, who feel personally responsible for energy use, and who perceive that their actions can make a difference report higher intentions to save energy. These attitudes are shaped by environmental awareness, social norms, personal values, economic motivation, and perceived efficacy. The Theory of Planned Behavior and the Norm Activation Model — the dominant psychological frameworks in this literature — predict that attitudes and subjective norms combine to produce behavioral intention, which in turn drives behavior. The models are elegant, extensively tested, and moderately predictive of intention. Where they fail is at the intention-behavior junction. The intention-beh

papers.title: PDF-0023
papers.excerpt: Sit a student next to a window on a sunny morning and she will score higher on her exam than the student under fluorescent tubes in the interior corridor. Shishegar and Boubekri at the University of Illinois reviewed the evidence for this claim in this 2016 review paper and argued that natural light improves health, alertness, and cognitive performance through two pathways: the visual pathway (better illumination of tasks) and the non-visual pathway (circadian entrainment through the melanopsin system). For most of the twentieth century, architects designed lighting to a single metric: how many lux reached the work surface. Electric lighting delivered those lux cheaply and reliably. But over the past two decades, chronobiology and circadian science showed that light does far more than illuminate. Photosensitive retinal ganglion cells containing melanopsin send signals to the suprachiasmatic nucleus, which synchronizes the body's circadian clock. The spectral composition and timing of light regulate melatonin, cortisol, alertness, and mood. The review covers three domains. First, health: daylight exposure through windows correlates with lower rates of seasonal affective disorder, be

papers.title: Color temperature and gender interact to shape mood and cognition: Warm light is not universally better
papers.excerpt: The assumption that warm light is universally calming and cool light is universally alerting — the foundation of most circadian lighting recommendations — breaks down when you add a variable that lighting research has rarely considered: gender. This study investigated the interaction between correlated color temperature (CCT) and gender on mood, cognitive performance, and memory, finding that the color temperature that benefits one gender may harm the other. The experiment exposed male and female participants to two white light conditions: warm light at 3000 K (the amber-tinged light associated with incandescent bulbs and domestic coziness) and cool light at 4000 K (the bluer-tinged light associated with daylight-simulation panels and modern offices). Under each lighting condition, participants completed mood assessments, cognitive problem-solving tasks (embedded figures tests requiring spatial reasoning), and memory tasks. The design was fully crossed: every participant experienced both color temperatures, allowing within-subjects comparison. The colour temperature × gender interaction on mood revealed a striking dissociation. The colour temperature that induced the least negative

papers.title: Restorative environments reduce directed-attention fatigue — but the mechanism depends on environment type
papers.excerpt: Mental fatigue from sustained attention is not just a feeling — it is a measurable depletion of a cognitive resource. This 2022 study in the *Journal of Environmental Psychology* investigated how different restorative environments replenish directed attention, testing the predictions of Kaplan's Attention Restoration Theory (ART) against empirical measures of cognitive recovery. ART proposes that natural environments restore directed attention through four properties: being away (psychological distance from routine demands), extent (sufficient scope to occupy the mind), fascination (holding attention without effort), and compatibility (fitting the person's inclinations). The theory predicts that environments rich in these properties — typically natural settings — should restore cognitive function more effectively than environments that lack them. The study exposed participants to different environmental conditions after a standardized attention-depleting task and then measured recovery using cognitive tests. The environments varied systematically in the ART properties, allowing the researchers to assess which properties contributed most to restoration. Natural settings consistently

papers.title: Buildings, beauty, and the brain — a neuroscience of architectural experience
papers.excerpt: Can neuroscience help architects design better buildings? Coburn, Vartanian, and Chatterjee argued in a 2017 review in the *Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience* that it can — but only if the field moves beyond borrowing neuroscientific findings for post-hoc justification toward conducting experiments in which architectural experiences are themselves the targets of neuroscientific investigation. The review traces the historical separation between architecture and science. For two thousand years since Vitruvius codified the triad of firmitas, utilitas, and venustas, architects have treated beauty as a legitimate design objective. The twentieth century relegated venustas to the periphery, subordinating aesthetic experience to functional and structural optimization. The result: modern buildings that perform well on measurable criteria (energy, cost, fire safety) but produce anemic experiences. Recent neuroscience offers a path back. The parahippocampal place area (PPA) encodes spatial layout — it responds preferentially to scenes that contain navigable space. The reward circuitry (medial prefrontal cortex, ventral striatum) activates when people judge buildings as beautiful. The amygdala

papers.title: Biophilic environments and stress recovery: A randomized trial of four environmental conditions
papers.excerpt: Does a biophilic environment actually reduce stress, or does it just look like it should? This study randomized participants to four environmental conditions — non-biophilic indoor, indoor biophilic, outdoor urban, and outdoor nature — to test whether biophilic design elements produce measurable physiological and psychological stress reduction compared to conventional indoor environments. The randomization to four conditions with no statistically significant differences in baseline demographic characteristics means that any observed differences in stress outcomes can be attributed to the environmental condition rather than to pre-existing differences between participant groups. This is the gold standard of environmental psychology methodology: random assignment eliminates the self-selection bias that plagues observational studies (where people who choose to sit in nature may be less stressed to begin with). The four conditions create a systematic comparison matrix. The non-biophilic indoor condition represents the default built environment: standard room without natural elements, views, living materials, or daylight quality beyond minimum functional illumination. The indoor biophil

papers.title: Designing non-stressed psychological public spaces — environmental stressors and architectural countermeasures
papers.excerpt: Public spaces can be incubators of psychological stress or antidotes to it — and the difference is architectural. Elsamahy and Abdelfattah reviewed in a 2018 conference paper the environmental psychology literature on stress in public spaces, synthesizing the environmental stressors that make public spaces psychologically aversive and the architectural countermeasures that can make them restorative. The identified stressors form a familiar litany. Noise — particularly unpredictable, uncontrollable traffic and construction noise — elevates cortisol and reduces people's willingness to linger. Crowding — too many people in too little space, with insufficient escape options — triggers the loss-of-control response that Baum and colleagues documented in residential settings. Visual complexity without coherence — chaotic signage, incompatible architectural languages, visual clutter — exhausts attentional resources. Thermal extremes — unsheltered plazas under summer sun, wind-tunneled corridors in winter — make spaces inhospitable. The architectural countermeasures are organized by sensory modality. Acoustic design: vegetated barriers, spatial separation from traffic, water features for ma

papers.title: PDF-0037
papers.excerpt: The human body runs on light. Not just for seeing — for keeping time. Anna Wirz-Justice and Colin Fournier wrote this 2010 review in World Health Design to bridge two worlds that rarely talk: chronobiologists who study the body clock and architects who design the buildings where that clock runs. Chronobiology is the science of 24-hour biological rhythms. The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) in the hypothalamus acts as the master clock, synchronizing sleep, hormone release, temperature, and cognitive performance to the solar cycle. The clock depends on light entering the eye — not through the rods and cones of vision but through melanopsin-containing retinal ganglion cells that project directly to the SCN. The spectral sensitivity of these cells peaks in the blue range (around 480 nm), meaning that the biological clock responds most strongly to blue-enriched daylight. The review summarizes what chronobiologists know with high confidence. Morning light advances the circadian phase, raising alertness earlier and promoting better sleep that night. Evening light delays the phase and suppresses melatonin, disrupting sleep. Intensity matters: the melanopsin system needs hundreds of lux, far

papers.title: Thigmotaxis and edge-seeking: A 3.6-billion-year trajectory from bacteria to building occupants
papers.excerpt: Humans seek the edges of rooms for the same reason bacteria seek the edges of petri dishes — because edges are safe. Thigmotaxis, the tendency to maintain contact with surfaces and boundaries, is observable from bacteria (3.6 billion years ago) through invertebrates, fish, rodents, and humans, making it one of the oldest behavioral traits in the living world. This chapter examines the evolutionary trajectory of edge-seeking behavior and its implications for how people occupy architectural spaces. The thigmotactic response in its simplest form is a movement bias: organisms move along boundaries rather than across open spaces. Single-celled organisms accumulate at container edges. Cockroaches run along walls. Mice in open-field tests spend most of their time at the perimeter. Fish in novel tanks swim along the glass. In each case, the behavior serves a survival function: boundaries provide tactile orientation (the organism knows where it is relative to the edge), protective cover (predators can approach from fewer directions along a wall than in open space), and navigational structure (following a boundary eventually leads somewhere). The human version of thigmotaxis is architectural

papers.title: Built environment as cultural information: How social processes encode meaning into architectural form
papers.excerpt: Buildings are documents. Not in the metaphorical sense that critics read facades like texts, but in the anthropological sense that built forms become important repositories of cultural information, encoding social hierarchies, cosmological beliefs, gender roles, economic structures, and collective identity into physical configurations of walls, roofs, doors, and spaces. This review examines the built environment as a product of social processes, tracing how cultures inscribe their values into architectural form and how those forms, in turn, shape the social behavior of their inhabitants. The encoding process operates through design conventions that become invisible through familiarity. A Western suburban house with a front lawn, a porch, and a setback from the street encodes specific cultural values: property ownership (the lawn marks territory), social display (the porch provides a stage for neighborly interaction), privacy gradient (the setback creates distance between public street and private interior), and nuclear family domesticity (the single-family form assumes a particular household structure). These encodings are not arbitrary — they reflect historically specific social a

papers.title: PDF-0042
papers.excerpt: Does the built environment make people depressed? Annette Chu, Alice Thorne, and Hilary Guite at Greenwich TPCT and King's College London asked this in a 2004 review in the Journal of Mental Health Promotion. They scanned the health, social science, and architecture literatures and built a conceptual model linking the urban physical environment to mental well-being through five key domains. The review was prompted by a practical need: Greenwich's primary care trust wanted to include the physical environment in its mental health strategy. But no existing model pulled together the scattered evidence. The team identified five domains that link the built environment to mental health. First, control over the internal home environment: residents who can adjust heating, ventilation, and lighting report better mental health than those in rigid systems. Second, quality of housing design and maintenance: damp, mold, noise, and poor layout correlate with anxiety and depression. Third, presence of valued escape facilities: access to parks, gardens, and green spaces buffers stress. Fourth, crime and fear of crime: dilapidated environments signal danger and erode well-being even when actual crim

papers.title: Chinese observers share Western preferences for high ceilings and open space but not curvilinear contours — cultural universality has limits
papers.excerpt: Do Chinese observers perceive architectural beauty the same way as Western observers? Partially. Dai, Zou, Wang, Ding, and Fukuda demonstrated in a 2022 study in *PLoS ONE* that Chinese observers share the Western preference for high ceilings and open space — but their response to curvilinear versus rectilinear contours depends on the spatial context, unlike the robust curvilinearity preference found in Western samples. The experiment used a factorial design: 29 Chinese observers rated computer-generated architectural images that varied systematically in ceiling height (high vs. low), degree of openness (open vs. closed), and contour type (curvilinear vs. rectilinear). Each participant rated all eight combinations on beauty and pleasantness. The preference for high ceilings was robust and large. The main effect of ceiling height on pleasantness was massive: F(1, 28) = 53.38, p < .001, η²p = .656 — the largest effect size in the study. High-ceiling spaces were rated as more beautiful and pleasant regardless of openness or contour. This replicates the Western finding (Vartanian et al., 2015; Coburn et al., 2020) and suggests that the preference for spatial volume is cross-culturally

papers.title: Timeless architectural patterns and evolved perception: Why Modernism feels wrong and traditional buildings feel right
papers.excerpt: Modernist architecture abandoned something that evolution spent millions of years calibrating humans to prefer. This review argues that Modernists abandoned timeless architectural patterns due to fascination with technology and ideological commitment to abstraction, producing architecture that feels uncomfortable because it violates evolved perceptual and affective processing that the nervous system performs on every built environment it encounters. The "timeless patterns" in question are the spatial, geometric, and ornamental features that appear repeatedly across pre-industrial architectural traditions worldwide: bilateral symmetry, hierarchical scale progression (large elements composed of medium elements composed of small elements), fractal-like self-similarity across scales, natural material textures, warm color palettes, threshold articulation, roof silhouettes that reference organic or landscape forms, and fine-grained ornamental detail at the scale of the human hand. These patterns are not culturally arbitrary — they emerge independently in civilizations that had no contact with each other, suggesting that they are responses to universal human perceptual preferences rather

papers.title: Dynamic lighting in offices: Effects on alertness, mood, sleep, and well-being — a review
papers.excerpt: More light is not always better light. Increased maintained illuminance — simply making the office brighter — can have negative effects on alertness, mood, sleep, and overall perception of the lit environment when the spectral composition and temporal pattern are wrong. This review examined the evidence on dynamic lighting in offices — lighting systems that change their intensity, color temperature, or spectral composition across the day — and found that the way light changes matters as much as how much light there is. The review synthesizes findings across three domains: alertness and cognitive performance, mood and well-being, and sleep quality. The alertness evidence shows that dynamic lighting can improve subjective alertness during afternoon hours when circadian dip produces natural drowsiness. Optimal dynamic lighting control — adjusting both illuminance and correlated color temperature across the workday to track the biological optimum — produced improved subjective alertness and better glare indices compared to static lighting at the same average illuminance. The key variable is not mean light level but temporal alignment: bright, cool-spectrum light in the morning supports

papers.title: Light as a circadian stimulus for architectural lighting — the CS metric brings photobiology to practice
papers.excerpt: Architects need a number for circadian light — not just illuminance, not just color temperature, but a single metric that captures how effectively a lighting condition stimulates the human circadian system. Rea and Figueiro developed in a 2018 paper in *Lighting Research & Technology* the Circadian Stimulus (CS) metric, translating decades of photobiology research into a practical quantity that lighting designers can specify and measure. The circadian system runs on light, but not the kind of light that traditional photometry measures. Standard illuminance (lux) weights light by the photopic sensitivity of cone photoreceptors — the visual system. But the circadian system is driven primarily by intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) containing the photopigment melanopsin, which has peak sensitivity at ~480 nm (short-wavelength blue). The circadian system also receives input from rods and cones, creating a spectral sensitivity that differs substantially from the photopic luminous efficiency function. A warm-white LED at 300 lux provides much less circadian stimulation than a cool-white LED at 300 lux, even though both register the same illuminance. The CS metric

papers.title: Biomimicry principles for healthy building design: A review
papers.excerpt: Americans spend over ninety percent of their time indoors — sleeping, working, commuting, eating, exercising — in environments that were designed by humans for humans, using materials and systems that bear almost no resemblance to the natural world in which the human body evolved. This paper asks whether biomimicry, the practice of borrowing functional strategies from living organisms and ecosystems, can produce buildings that are not only more environmentally sustainable but healthier for they people who occupy them. The review synthesizes literature from three domains — biomimicry in architecture, healthy building science, and biophilic design — to argue that these three fields, when combined, produce a framework for buildings that simultaneously reduce environmental impact, improve indoor environmental quality, and activate the evolved perceptual preferences that influence occupant well-being. From biomimicry, the paper identifies six major principles that guide biological design strategies: resource (material and energy) efficiency, evolved and tested solutions, functional diversity, resilience through redundancy, use of locally available resources, and benign manufacturing pro

papers.title: Indoor plants improve air quality, reduce stress, and boost productivity — but the mechanisms may be more psychological than chemical
papers.excerpt: Plants in offices reduce sick building syndrome symptoms, lower stress, and improve self-reported productivity. But are they cleaning the air or just changing how people feel about the room? Ahu Aydogan and Ryan Cerone, in a comprehensive review published in *Indoor and Built Environment*, examined the evidence for plants' effects on indoor environmental quality, finding that the psychological pathway is likely stronger than the chemical one. The air-quality case is often overstated. Plants can remove volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from enclosed chambers under laboratory conditions (the famous NASA clean air study). But in real buildings with mechanical ventilation, the air exchange rate overwhelms the bioremediation capacity of any realistic planting density. A building's HVAC system moves thousands of cubic feet of air per minute; a few dozen potted plants process a negligible fraction. The review concludes that at typical office plant densities, the measurable impact on VOC concentrations, CO2 levels, or particulate matter is marginal. The humidity contribution is real but modest. Plants release water vapor through transpiration, increasing local relative humidity. In the art

papers.title: Nature heals stress faster than cities — and the body confirms what the mind reports
papers.excerpt: After a stressful experience, where should you go to recover — a tree-lined park or a commercial street? Roger Ulrich and colleagues at Texas A&M answered this question with unusual rigor in a 1991 study in the *Journal of Environmental Psychology*, using a combination of physiological and self-report measures that made their conclusion difficult to dismiss. One hundred and twenty participants first watched a stressful industrial-accident film to induce genuine physiological stress — elevated heart rate, increased skin conductance, rising muscle tension. Then each participant was randomly assigned to watch a 10-minute color/sound videotape of one of six outdoor environments: two natural settings (vegetation-dominated scenes with water or open views) and four urban settings (pedestrian mall, moderate vehicle traffic, heavy vehicle traffic, and mixed commercial). The critical comparison was not preference but recovery — how quickly and completely each group returned to pre-stress physiological baseline. The physiological results converged. Pulse transit time (an inverse measure of blood pressure) recovered significantly faster in the nature groups than in all urban groups (mixed-mode

papers.title: Architectural stimuli affect human psychology and physiology — a systematic review catalogs the evidence
papers.excerpt: What, exactly, do buildings do to us? Not metaphorically or spiritually — what measurable psychological and physiological effects do specific architectural features produce? A 2022 systematic review in *Building and Environment* catalogued the empirical evidence, feature by feature, and found that the field has come further than most architects realize — and still has enormous gaps. The review screened the literature for studies that empirically measured human responses to identifiable architectural parameters. The features examined include ceiling height, room proportionality, spatial openness and enclosure, window presence and size, natural versus artificial lighting, material texture and color, spatial complexity and order, the presence of natural elements (vegetation, water, natural materials), and acoustic properties. For each feature, the review tabulated the evidence for effects on three outcome domains: psychological responses (mood, preference, aesthetic judgment, stress), physiological responses (cortisol, heart rate, EEG, skin conductance), and behavioral responses (spatial behavior, task performance, wayfinding). The strongest evidence clusters around a few features. Ce

papers.title: PDF-0054
papers.excerpt: Walk into a cathedral and a library on the same afternoon. Both are quiet, both are large. Yet the cathedral produces awe while the library produces focus. The difference lies not in square footage but in what Yi-Fu Tuan calls *place* — the layer of meaning, memory, and emotional significance that transforms abstract space into lived experience. Charles Grantham's 1978 review in *Contemporary Sociology* presents Tuan's *Space and Place: The Perspective of Experience* (University of Minnesota Press, 1977, 226 pp.) as a foundational text that bridges geography, philosophy, and environmental psychology. The review identifies the book's central contribution: a systematic argument that perceived physical location shapes attitudes and behavior through an experiential process, not merely through measurable spatial properties. Tuan begins by tracing the development of spatial awareness across the human lifespan — from infants, through children, to adults — drawing on cross-cultural comparisons to establish the universality of the space-to-place transformation. His analysis employs a qualitative, phenomenological method rather than statistical testing, which Grantham notes is 'supplemented

papers.title: PDF-0055
papers.excerpt: Step into a university design studio flooded with warm-toned light and the room feels spacious, collaborative, inviting. Switch the same fixtures to cool-toned light and the space suddenly feels cramped and clinical. Mohammad Kamal Zoubi, Ali Rashidi, Wedad Ali Al-Alawneh, and Mustafa Mohanad at Al-Balqa Applied University showed in this 2023 study that light colour temperature directly affects how interior design students perform and how they perceive the spaces they work in. Architects and educators know that lighting affects mood and productivity. But the specific question of which colour temperature, warm (2700-3000K), neutral (4000K), or cool (5000-6500K), best supports creative design work has received surprisingly little controlled testing. Most studies focus on offices or schools. Design studios, where students sketch, model, and critique visual work, place different demands on perception and cognition. The researchers tested three colour temperature conditions, warm (3000K), neutral (4000K), and cool (6500K), in the same classroom space at Al-Balqa's interior design program. Participants were interior design students who performed standardized design tasks under each light

papers.title: The Perception of Color by Ralph Evans — book review on five-dimensional color space and brilliance
papers.excerpt: Color in buildings is not what the spectrometer says it is — it is what the eye-brain system constructs from the interaction between the spectral power distribution of light, the reflectance of surfaces, and the adaptation state of the observer. This 1975 review in the *Journal of the Optical Society of America* assessed Ralph Evans's posthumous book *The Perception of Color*, published by Wiley in 1974, which argued that the standard colorimetric system used by architects and lighting designers fails to capture central aspects of how humans perceive color. Evans's central contribution was the expansion of color description from three dimensions (hue, saturation, brightness — as in the CIE system) to five. He added lightness (the perceived brightness of a surface relative to its surround) and brilliance (a perceptual dimension related to fluorescence and glow — the quality that makes a yellow surface appear to emit more light than it receives). These two additional dimensions capture phenomena that standard colorimetry treats as measurement artifacts but that perceivers experience as real and salient. The reviewer found Evans's treatment of the G₀ function — the ratio of surround l

papers.title: Light and color affect psychological responses across cultures — but the patterns are not identical
papers.excerpt: Does a warm-lit room feel the same in Stockholm as it does in Buenos Aires? This cross-cultural field study compared the psychological effects of interior lighting and color across four countries, finding that light and color significantly affect mood, perceived spaciousness, and room preference — but that the direction and magnitude of these effects vary with cultural context. The study measured responses to varied lighting conditions (warm vs. cool CCT, high vs. low illuminance) and wall colors (warm vs. cool hues) in standardized interior settings. Participants from four culturally distinct populations evaluated the environments on mood, perceived pleasantness, spaciousness, and preference. The multi-country design allowed the researchers to test which lighting-color effects are universal and which are culturally modulated. The universal effects: higher illuminance increased perceived spaciousness and alertness across all cultures. Lower illuminance increased perceived intimacy and relaxation across all cultures. These effects likely reflect basic photobiological mechanisms (more light = more arousal) and spatial perception processes (brighter rooms appear larger) that are large

papers.title: PDF-0058
papers.excerpt: Do tunable classroom lights make students think better? Nina Stojanovic reviewed the evidence in this 2024 systematic review and found that human-centric lighting — fixtures designed to mimic daylight's spectrum and timing — shows promise for boosting student alertness and cognitive performance, but the evidence base remains thin and fragmented. Traditional classroom lighting maximizes visual acuity at the desk: enough lux to read, minimal glare, uniform coverage. Human-centric lighting (HCL) goes further. It adjusts color temperature and intensity through the day to align with the circadian rhythm. Cool, blue-enriched light in the morning activates the melanopsin pathway and raises alertness. Warm light in the afternoon supports calming and transition to evening. The review searched databases systematically and identified studies that tested HCL interventions in educational settings and measured cognitive outcomes (attention, memory, test scores) or physiological proxies (cortisol, EEG, heart rate). The included studies varied widely in design, sample size, and outcome measures. Several studies showed positive effects: cool-white light (5000-6500K) improved reading speed and susta

papers.title: An immersive multisensory VR method enables ecologically valid study of human-environment interaction across visual, thermal, and auditory modalities
papers.excerpt: Most environmental psychology research studies one sense at a time — but real buildings assault all senses simultaneously. Lyu, Brambilla, Globa, and de Dear developed in a 2023 paper in *Automation in Construction* an immersive multisensory virtual reality method that simultaneously controls visual, thermal, and auditory conditions, enabling researchers to study how people respond to the full sensory complexity of built environments rather than artificially isolated sensory channels. The method integrates three sensory modalities within a single VR experience. The visual channel uses a head-mounted display to present photorealistic architectural environments with high spatial fidelity and freedom of movement. The thermal channel uses radiant heaters, fans, and temperature-controlled air to produce realistic thermal conditions that match the visual scene — a sunny outdoor plaza feels warm, a shaded courtyard feels cooler. The auditory channel uses spatial audio to present environmental soundscapes (traffic, birdsong, fountains, HVAC noise) that are spatially congruent with the visual scene. The innovation lies in the integration. Previous VR-based environmental research typically c

papers.title: Embodied learning using a tangible user interface: Effects of haptic perception and selective pointing on spatial learning
papers.excerpt: Hand a student a physical model of the human skull and ask them to learn the names of its bones. Then hand another student the same anatomical model on a computer screen, navigable by mouse. Who learns more? Alexander Skulmowski, Simon Pradel, Tom Kühnert, and Guido Brünken demonstrated in a 2016 study that the student holding the physical object — the tangible user interface — retained significantly more information, and the reason involves not just touch but the interaction between touch and how labels are displayed. The study used a 2×2 between-subjects factorial design with 96 participants randomly assigned to four conditions: mouse with permanently displayed labels, mouse with selectively pointed labels (labels appear only when the cursor targets the structure), tangible user interface (TUI) with permanent labels, and TUI with selective pointing labels. The learning task required participants to study a three-dimensional anatomical model and learn the names and locations of labeled structures. Pre-test measures confirmed that the four groups did not differ in prior anatomical knowledge or spatial ability, and there was no significant gender difference in either measure — estab
papers.authors: Skulmowski, A., Pradel, S., Kühnert, T. & Brünken, R.

papers.title: Place attachment has two dimensions — identity and dependence — and they predict different outcomes
papers.excerpt: How strongly do you feel attached to your neighborhood? This question contains two distinct psychological constructs that are often conflated. M. Carmen Hidalgo and Bernardo Hernández, in a study published in the *Journal of Environmental Psychology* in 2001, provided some of the clearest empirical evidence that place attachment comprises two separable dimensions — place identity and place dependence — and that these dimensions relate differently to the physical and social features of environments. Place identity captures whether a place has become part of the self: "I am a person from this neighborhood; this place reflects who I am." Place dependence captures whether a place uniquely serves one's functional needs: "This neighborhood is the best place for the things I do; no other place could substitute." Hidalgo and Hernández developed a questionnaire to measure both dimensions and administered it across multiple spatial scales — home, neighborhood, and city — to test whether the two-dimensional structure held consistently. The two dimensions emerged as distinct factors at all three scales. People could feel high identity attachment but low dependence (loving a place that no longe

papers.title: Cognitive load theory and the physical learning environment: A new perspective
papers.excerpt: A student sits in a windowless classroom under flickering fluorescent light, the air warm and stale, the chairs bolted in rows facing a whiteboard. The instructor explains how to solve a system of differential equations. The student's working memory — a system that can hold no more than five to nine information elements for roughly twenty seconds before they decay — is already partially occupied: processing the thermal discomfort, filtering the flickering light, suppressing the urge to fidget in the rigid chair. Before the first equation reaches the board, the cognitive bandwidth available for learning has been quietly taxed by the room itself. Hee Hwan Choi, Jeroen van Merriënboer, and Fred Paas proposed in this 2014 paper that cognitive load theory should be expanded to recognize the physical learning environment as a distinct causal factor, one that interacts with learner and task characteristics to determine how much working memory remains available for instruction. Cognitive Load Theory in its original formulation (Paas and Van Merriënboer, 1994) identifies two main sources of cognitive load: intrinsic load, determined by the inherent complexity of the learning material (the n
papers.authors: Choi, H.H., van Merriënboer, J.J.G. & Paas, F.

papers.title: Psychoacoustics: Perception of normal and impaired hearing with audiology applications
papers.excerpt: A fire alarm in a hospital corridor blasts at 85 decibels. A ventilation system hums at 45. A whispered conversation across an open-plan office registers at 30. These three sounds differ by a factor of more than thirty thousand in physical pressure — the alarm exerts roughly 30,000 times more pressure on the eardrum than the whisper — yet the human auditory system compresses that astronomical range into a perceptual experience that feels merely like "loud," "moderate," and "quiet." This chapter on psychoacoustics explains how the auditory system performs that compression and what happens when built environments push it beyond its operating parameters. The chapter provides the foundational conceptual framework for understanding how sounds in built environments are perceived and how environmental noise affects cognitive performance. It covers the full chain from physical stimulus to perceptual experience: the physics of sound pressure, the mechanics of auditory transduction, the psychophysics of loudness and pitch perception, the principles of spatial hearing, and the cognitive consequences of noise exposure. For architects and environmental designers, the chapter functions as a refe

papers.title: Highlight-shading patterns signal translucency — the visual system uses specular-nonspecular contrast to perceive material transparency
papers.excerpt: The way light scatters beneath a surface tells the brain what that surface is made of — and the visual system reads this information from the relationship between specular highlights and non-specular shading. Isamu Motoyoshi demonstrated in a 2010 study in the *Journal of Vision* that perceived translucency of three-dimensional objects depends on diagnostic image features in the non-specular shading component, providing a computational-level account of how humans perceive material transparency. Translucent materials — skin, wax, porcelain, grapes, marble, frosted glass — involve complex light transport: light enters the surface, scatters internally (sub-surface scattering), and exits at locations distant from the entry point. This blurs the non-specular component of the image (the body-reflected shading) while leaving the specular component (surface-reflected highlights) relatively sharp. The visual system exploits this differential to judge material properties. Through a series of psychophysical experiments using computer-generated 3D objects, Motoyoshi showed that when the non-specular image component is made blurrier, fainter, or partially contrast-reversed relative to the specu

papers.title: Transparent glass barriers are 'there and not there': Perceptual, behavioral, and social dimensions
papers.excerpt: Glass walls are simultaneously there and not there — visually transparent but physically impenetrable, acoustically filtering but not blocking, spatially dividing but perceptually connecting. This paradox makes transparent barriers one of the most psychologically complex architectural elements in the contemporary built environment. This review examined transparent glass barriers from perceptual, behavioral, and social perspectives, arguing that they define spaces while shaping lighting, noise, and climate, yet little systematic research exists on their cognitive and behavioral impacts. The perceptual challenge is fundamental. Visual perception of transparent barriers depends on cues that may be present or absent depending on lighting conditions, viewing angle, and surface treatment. Transparency is conveyed by reflections and refractions of light — highlights, specular reflections, distortions of background imagery, and Fresnel effects at oblique angles. When these cues are strong (bright daylight, reflective coatings, visible mullions), the glass is perceptually present. When they are weak (even lighting on both sides, anti-reflective coatings, frameless mounting), the glass can b

papers.title: PDF-0071
papers.excerpt: What if architects could read the brain scans of their future occupants before drawing a single line? Joyal George and Vishnu Prakash at SCMS School of Architecture reviewed the field of neuroarchitecture in this 2024 paper in IJSREM, asking how neuroscience findings about light, color, scale, acoustics, and spatial layout translate into design principles. The review covers four domains. First, lighting and circadian rhythm: buildings that provide morning daylight and shift to warm light in the evening align with the melanopsin system and support better sleep, mood, and alertness. Second, color and emotion: warm colors raise arousal, cool colors lower it, but the effects interact with context and culture. Third, scale and spatial volume: high ceilings activate abstract thinking (Meyers-Levy and Zhu's freedom hypothesis), while low ceilings focus attention on details. Fourth, acoustics: background noise above 35 dB degrades cognitive performance, and reverberation above 0.6 seconds reduces speech clarity. The authors trace the historical roots to Gaudi's Sagrada Familia and Notre-Dame Cathedral, arguing that architects have always intuited the connection between form and feeling. Wh

papers.title: Gibson saw surfaces, not space — the ecological theory of visual perception begins with texture
papers.excerpt: The visual world is not made of points in empty space — it is made of surfaces. James J. Gibson proposed in this foundational 1950 paper in *The American Journal of Psychology* that the starting unit of visual perception is not the retinal image, not the geometric point, and not the stimulus energy, but the textured surface — the ground beneath our feet, the walls around us, the ceiling overhead. This reframing changed how we think about vision, and it should change how architects think about the surfaces they design. Gibson argued that traditional theories of visual perception began with the wrong problem. They asked how the brain reconstructs a three-dimensional world from a two-dimensional retinal image — the "inverse optics" problem. Gibson proposed instead that the three-dimensional layout of surfaces is directly specified by the optic array — the structured pattern of light arriving at the eye — without the need for unconscious inference or computation. The key: surfaces have texture, and that texture provides a density gradient in the optic array that directly specifies the surface's orientation, distance, and extent. Look at a floor stretching away from you. The texture ele

papers.title: Wayfinding task complexity: A choice-clue model for measuring navigation difficulty in buildings
papers.excerpt: Why do some buildings make you lost and others guide you effortlessly? This study proposed that wayfinding task complexity in built environments can be measured using a choice-clue model consisting of two critical elements: the number of decision points a navigator must pass and the availability of environmental clues at each decision point. The model transforms wayfinding difficulty from a vague experiential quality into a quantifiable design parameter. The choice-clue model is elegant in its simplicity. A wayfinding task becomes more complex when it requires more choices — more intersections where the navigator must decide which direction to turn. And it becomes more complex when fewer clues are available at those choice points — when the corridors all look the same, when signage is absent or ambiguous, when no landmarks distinguish one intersection from another. Complexity is the interaction of choices and clues: many choices with abundant clues (a well-signed hospital with distinctive departments) may be easier than few choices with no clues (a small building with identical corridors). Decision points are architecturally determinable. Every T-intersection, every corridor juncti

papers.title: PDF-0074
papers.excerpt: Children attach not just to people but to places. The tree they climb every afternoon. The corner shop where the owner knows their name. The bedroom where they feel safe. Gordon Jack at Durham University argued in this 2010 article in the British Journal of Social Work that children's place attachments matter for their well-being as much as their human attachments — and that social work has almost entirely ignored them. Bowlby's attachment theory transformed child welfare by showing that secure bonds with caregivers predict healthy development. But Bowlby focused on people. Environmental psychology and human geography have shown that places also anchor identity, security, and belonging. Jack draws on this literature to argue that disrupting a child's place attachments — through forced moves, institutional care, or neighborhood demolition — can damage well-being as deeply as disrupting a human relationship. The paper reviews three bodies of evidence. First, developmental research on how children form place attachments: toddlers explore outward from a secure home base, school-age children claim territories (the fort, the den), and adolescents use neighborhood spaces for identity work

papers.title: Architectural aesthetics through the pleasure-arousal-dominance lens — how buildings make you feel
papers.excerpt: Architecture makes you feel things — but what exactly? Mfon applied in a 2023 narrative review the Mehrabian-Russell PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance) emotional model to architectural aesthetics, mapping specific architectural features onto the three orthogonal emotional dimensions that this model proposes for describing any emotional state. The PAD model, developed in environmental psychology, describes emotional responses along three independent axes. Pleasure captures the valence dimension — how positive or negative the experience feels. Arousal captures the activation dimension — how stimulating or calming the environment is. Dominance captures the control dimension — how much the person feels in command of or subordinate to the environment. The review maps architectural features to each dimension. Pleasure is predicted by visual coherence, naturalness (biophilic elements), harmonious proportions, and material warmth. Spaces that score high on pleasure feel welcoming, comfortable, and aesthetically satisfying. Arousal is predicted by visual complexity, novelty, spatial drama (tall ceilings, unusual geometries), and sensory richness. Spaces that score high on arousal feel excitin

papers.title: Older adults default to geometric spatial cues during real-world navigation — an age-related strategy shift with architectural implications
papers.excerpt: The way you find your way through a building changes as you age — and the change has consequences for architectural design. Bécu, Sheynikhovich, and colleagues demonstrated in a 2020 study in *Nature Human Behaviour* that older adults default to geometric spatial cues (corridor angles, room shapes, spatial structure) when navigating in real environments, while younger adults preferentially use landmark cues (distinctive objects, signs, colors). This age-related strategy shift reflects a specific deficit in landmark coding, not a general cognitive decline. The study used a real ecological environment — not a laboratory or virtual reality — where geometric boundaries and landmark features provided conflicting navigation information. Participants navigated real spaces while their body movements and eye gaze were continuously recorded, providing both behavioral and oculomotor evidence of which spatial cues they were processing. Older adults showed a clear geometric preference: when landmarks and geometry conflicted, they followed the geometry. Their eye movement patterns revealed a different scanning policy — less efficient exploration, longer reorientation times, and an imbalance betw
papers.authors: Bécu, Sheynikhovich, Tatur, Agathos, Bologna, Sahel, Arleo

papers.title: Age changes navigation strategy — older adults rely on geometry, younger adults on landmarks
papers.excerpt: Older adults and younger adults navigate buildings differently — and the difference matters for architectural design. This 2010 study in *Landscape and Urban Planning* found that older adults rely more on environmental geometry (spatial layout, path angles, room shapes) for wayfinding, while younger adults rely more on landmarks (distinctive objects, signs, visual features) — suggesting that buildings designed primarily with landmark-based wayfinding cues may fail the population that needs the most navigational support. The research used a combination of behavioral wayfinding tasks and verbal protocol analysis. Participants of different ages navigated through environments and described their strategies. Older adults consistently referenced the spatial structure — "I turned left at the T-intersection," "I followed the longer corridor" — while younger adults referenced landmarks — "I turned at the red door," "I went past the artwork." The age-related shift in strategy reflects both cognitive changes and accumulated experience. Hippocampal function declines with age, and the hippocampus is critical for landmark-place associations. But the geometric properties of spaces — angles, dista

papers.title: Designing for multi-sensory experiences in the built environment — a framework from the intersection of architecture and sensory design
papers.excerpt: Architecture is usually designed for the eye — but human experience of buildings is irreducibly multi-sensory. Jaakko Ranne developed in a 2019 master's thesis at Aalto University a framework for designing multi-sensory experiences in the built environment, arguing that architectural quality depends on the coherent integration of visual, auditory, haptic, olfactory, and thermal information. The thesis draws from two traditions. Sensory design — practiced in product design, exhibition design, and hospitality — has developed sophisticated methods for engaging multiple senses simultaneously. Architecture — despite producing inherently multi-sensory objects — has primarily theorized and practiced visual design, treating sound, touch, smell, and thermal sensation as engineering concerns separate from design intent. The framework maps the interaction between sensory modalities in architectural experience. Visual-haptic coherence: the material a surface looks like should feel like what it looks like — a warm-looking wood surface should feel warm, and the cognitive dissonance of visual-haptic mismatch reduces perceived quality. Visual-acoustic coherence: the sound a space looks like it sho

papers.title: Towards an analysis of mental representations of attachment to the home — how people construct the meaning of home
papers.excerpt: What does it mean to feel attached to your home — and how is that attachment structured in the mind? Maria Vittoria Giuliani proposed in a highly cited 1991 paper in the *Journal of Architectural and Planning Research* a theoretical analysis of home attachment as a system of mental representations — cognitive, affective, and behavioral — that together constitute the experience of home. The analysis distinguishes home attachment from related concepts (residential satisfaction, place identity, sense of community) and argues that attachment to home is a specific psychological phenomenon with its own structure, developmental trajectory, and architectural determinants. Residential satisfaction is an evaluative judgment; place identity is a self-concept process; home attachment is an emotional bond to a specific physical environment. The cognitive representation encodes knowledge about the home: its spatial layout, material qualities, functional affordances, and the memories associated with specific locations within it. This representation is detailed, multi-sensory, and temporally layered — accumulated through years of daily inhabitation. The architect influences this representation thr

papers.title: Indirect lighting feels cooler and more pleasant — and the EEG shows why
papers.excerpt: Walk into a room where light bounces off the ceiling before reaching your eyes. Something feels different — cooler, more spacious, more pleasant — even though the total illumination is the same as a room lit entirely from above. Yu-Bin Shin and colleagues at Yonsei University wanted to know whether this subjective difference has a measurable neural signature. It does. Twenty-eight participants (12 female, mean age 22.5) sat in a furnished living room measuring 4.7 × 4.4 × 3.1 meters, containing sofas, a coffee table, side table, television, and a rug — a realistic residential setting, not a sterile laboratory. Eight LED panels (600 × 600 mm, 53 W each, 5000 K) were suspended from the ceiling, four facing up and four facing down. The experiment compared two conditions while holding total illuminance constant at desk level: direct/indirect lighting (400 lx downlight plus 300 lx uplight) versus direct-only lighting (700 lx downlight). Daylight was excluded. After a four-minute dark adaptation, each lighting condition was presented for four minutes while 40-channel EEG recorded brain oscillations continuously. Participants then rated their emotional experience on 100-millimeter visual
papers.authors: Shin, Woo, Kim, Kim, Kim, Park

papers.title: PDF-0084
papers.excerpt: Pack people into a small room and make them work. Do they perform worse? Jonathan Freedman, Simon Klevansky, and Paul Ehrlich at Stanford University showed in this 1971 study in the Journal of Applied Social Psychology that spatial density — the amount of floor space per person — had no consistent effect on task performance across three experiments. In the early 1970s, public anxiety about overpopulation drove researchers to ask whether crowding harmed human functioning. Animal studies had shown dramatic effects: crowded rats became aggressive and sick. But did the same apply to humans doing cognitive work? Freedman's team ran three experiments. Groups of people worked for four hours at a time, for two or three consecutive days, under crowded or uncrowded conditions. The tasks ranged from simple (rote memory, crossing out letters) to complex (creativity tests, abstract reasoning). The rooms were real, not simulated, and the durations were long enough to capture adaptation and fatigue. Across all three experiments, density produced no significant effects on any performance measure. People in crowded rooms solved as many problems, made as few errors, and showed as much creativity as

papers.title: Acoustic modeling of sequential spaces — how openings, absorption, and room coupling shape sound fields
papers.excerpt: Sound does not stop at the door — it flows through openings, diffuses along corridors, and couples between rooms in ways that static acoustic models miss. Yang and Kang demonstrated in a 2022 parametric study in Building and Environment how sound propagates through sequential spaces — rooms connected by openings — using finite element modeling to map the relationships between three architectural design parameters (opening dimension, acoustic absorption, and source location) and the resulting sound field. The study modeled five successive rooms connected by openings, systematically varying the opening dimension from 0 percent to 100 percent of the separating partition width. This configuration represents one of the most common architectural conditions in public buildings: enfilade rooms in galleries, sequential spaces in shopping malls, open-plan offices with partial partitions, and hospital corridors opening onto patient rooms. The parametric approach allowed isolation of variables that are confounded in field measurement: opening size was varied while geometry, volume, and absorption were held constant. The primary finding is straightforward but quantitatively precise. As opening

papers.title: Neuroscience and architectural theory: Redirecting from objectification to embodied spatial experience
papers.excerpt: Architecture has spent a century talking about buildings as objects — their form, their composition, their material expression, their stylistic lineage. Neuroscience is now forcing a different conversation: about buildings as experiences. Recent biological and neuroscientific discoveries regarding human spatial perception, embodied cognition, and affective processing demand that architectural theory redirect its focus from the objectification of buildings to the embodied experience of inhabiting them. The objectification tradition treats a building as a composed artifact to be contemplated from outside — evaluated by its formal properties (proportion, rhythm, balance), its stylistic coherence (Modernist, Postmodernist, Deconstructivist), and its material expression (concrete, glass, steel). This tradition produces architectural criticism that reads buildings as texts, architectural photography that frames buildings as sculptures, and architectural education that teaches design as composition. What it does not produce is an account of what it feels like to be inside the building — how the space affects the body, the emotions, the cognitive processes of the person who occupies it. Ne
