papers.title: The experience of crowding is about personal space violation, not density — a two-factor attribution theory
papers.excerpt: Why does a packed subway feel more crowded than a full football stadium — even though the stadium has a higher total density? Worchel and Teddlie proposed in a landmark 1976 study in the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology that crowding is not a direct function of spatial density but a two-step attribution process. First, violation of personal space produces physiological arousal. Then, the individual attributes that arousal to the presence of other people — and only this attribution creates the subjective experience of being crowded. If the arousal can be attributed to something else — wall decorations, an interesting task, environmental novelty — the experience of crowding diminishes even though the physical conditions remain identical. The two-factor theory makes a testable prediction that distinguishes it from every density-based account of crowding. If arousal can be misattributed to a non-social source, then crowding should be reduced even when the same number of people occupy the same amount of space at the same interpersonal distance. This prediction was tested experimentally. Forty subjects were assigned to eight conditions in a 2 × 2 × 2 factorial design crossing

papers.title: A scientometric analysis of spatial cognition research at the intersection of architecture and neuroscience
papers.excerpt: Walk into Boston City Hall Plaza and your eyes search for something to land on — a doorframe, a window edge, a pattern in the stone. They find almost nothing. Eye-tracking data from Sussman and Ward (2019) showed that visitors' gaze wanders aimlessly across that brutalist expanse because the brain needs fixation points within the first three to five seconds of viewing a new scene. The neuroscience of why some buildings feel right and others feel wrong has been accumulating for two decades, but nobody had mapped where all that research actually lives. Sule Tasli Pektas did exactly that in a 2021 scientometric analysis published in Architectural Science Review, charting the full publication landscape of spatial cognition studies that sit at the intersection of neuroscience and architecture. The core finding is structural: the field is not one field but several loosely connected clusters. By analyzing 2,377 publications from 1999 to 2019 using co-citation analysis, keyword co-occurrence, and bibliographic coupling, Pektas identified 13 keyword clusters that organize into four major intellectual territories. The largest cluster (43 keywords) centers on the parietal lobe and spatial pro

papers.title: PDF-0095
papers.excerpt: A well-designed bus stop does not just look good — it smells neutral, sounds calm, feels smooth underhand, and shelters the body from wind. Hendrik Schifferstein at Delft University of Technology described in this 2011 paper at the DESIRE conference how Multi Sensory Design (MSD) integrates all five senses into the product design process so that every sensory channel supports a single coherent experience. Most design disciplines treat vision as the primary sense. Architects draw floor plans and render perspectives; industrial designers shape forms and choose colors. But users touch, smell, hear, and taste what they encounter. A hospital waiting room that looks calming but smells of bleach and echoes with overhead pages delivers a contradictory sensory message. MSD addresses this by systematically exploring every modality during the design process. Schifferstein lays out an eight-step method. First, select a target expression — the emotional experience the design should create (e.g., warmth, welcome, trust). Second, explore the concept: what associations does the target evoke across all senses? Third, conduct a sensory exploration: collect physical samples — materials, sounds, scent

papers.title: Environmental psychology: From stimulus theory to design application
papers.excerpt: Before the 1960s, architects designed buildings and psychologists studied behavior — in different rooms, in different institutions, speaking different languages. Then a discipline emerged that claimed both rooms belonged to the same building. Environmental psychology, which first appeared in the United States in the 1960s and spread to Britain, France, Sweden, and beyond, proposed a radical idea for its time: that the physical environment is not merely a backdrop against which behavior occurs but an active determinant of psychological experience that can be studied with the same rigor that psychologists apply to any other variable. This paper examines the theoretical foundations, core frameworks, and design applications of environmental psychology, tracing its evolution from stimulus-response models to the interactive, transactional perspectives that inform contemporary evidence-based design. Environmental psychology's core theories provide multiple lenses for understanding person-environment interaction. Stimulus theory — the earliest framework — treats the environment as a source of sensory input that the nervous system processes, filters, and responds to. Too much stimulation pr

papers.title: PDF-0100
papers.excerpt: Why do some images please the eye while others leave it cold? Maria Grebenkina, Anselm Brachmann, Marco Bertamini, Ali Kaduhm, and Christoph Redies at the University of Jena showed in this 2018 study in Frontiers in Neuroscience that a single statistical property — edge-orientation entropy — predicts aesthetic ratings across a wide range of man-made visual stimuli, from building facades to album covers. Previous work had shown that artworks have higher edge-orientation entropy than non-art images: the luminance edges in a painting are distributed more evenly across all angles (horizontal, vertical, diagonal). But nobody had tested whether this property extends beyond fine art to the kinds of designed visual stimuli people encounter every day — buildings, ornamental patterns, graphic designs. The team ran two studies. In the exploratory study, participants rated the aesthetic appeal of photographs of simple shapes, ornamental patterns, building facades, and music album covers. Across all categories, images with higher edge-orientation entropy received higher ratings on 'pleasing' and 'interesting' scales. For facades, entropy predicted about half the variance in aesthetic ratings. F

papers.title: The Built Environment and Spatial Form
papers.excerpt: The layout of a room shapes who talks to whom—and by extension, what your culture believes about itself. This landmark review by anthropologists Denise Lawrence and Setha Low synthesizes nearly a century of theory and ethnography on how the built environment and spatial organization reflect and reinforce the social structures they contain. Rather than treating buildings as neutral containers, the authors argue that architecture is culture made concrete, and changing one requires understanding the deep reciprocity between form and behavior. The paper traces a coherent intellectual genealogy. Early anthropologists—Lewis Henry Morgan observing aboriginal house forms, Marcel Mauss studying Eskimo dwellings—recognized that building form correlates with kinship structures and economic organization. The Mauss example is instructive: larger dwellings were required to accommodate collective hunting activities, and dwelling size became a window into social complexity. These observations were not incidental; they were evidence that architecture encodes social rules. As theory matured, scholars moved beyond simple correlation to mechanism. Émile Durkheim and his successors showed that ritual p
papers.authors: Denise L. Lawrence, Setha M. Low

papers.title: PDF-0103
papers.excerpt: Pack twenty people into a room designed for ten and most will report feeling crowded. But pack the same twenty into a room three times larger and some will still feel crowded if they cannot leave. Andrew Baum and Glenn Davis at Trinity College and SUNY Stony Brook showed in this 1976 study in Environment and Behavior that crowding is not about how many people occupy a space — it is about whether the occupant feels spatially restricted. Daniel Stokols (1972) had already distinguished physical density (people per square meter) from the psychological experience of crowding. He argued that feeling spatially restricted or unable to move freely was a necessary precondition for crowding stress. Baum and Davis tested this claim by asking what environmental and social variables make spatial restriction feel worse or better. The study manipulated both physical density and social context. Groups of participants were placed in rooms that varied in size and were given tasks that varied in how much social interaction they required. The researchers measured self-reported crowding, perceived control, physiological stress indicators, and behavioral withdrawal (how often people turned away from othe

papers.title: PDF-0104
papers.excerpt: Hand a design student a piece of bamboo composite and watch their face. They run their fingers along the grain, press the edge, hold it up to the light. Then do the same with recycled PET plastic. The reactions differ instantly. Lung-Chi Lin, Jirawat Vongphantuset, and Veerawat Sirivesmas at Silpakorn University in Thailand showed in this 2024 study that tactile experience with sustainable materials changes how art and design students engage with sustainability, and that texture plays the central role. Design schools increasingly teach sustainability, but most teaching stays abstract: carbon footprints, lifecycle assessments, recycling percentages. Students rarely touch the materials themselves. This study asked whether hands-on tactile workshops would shift both engagement and awareness. The team used a mixed-methods approach. They interviewed three art and design experts in depth. They then ran three participatory workshops with 48 university students. Each workshop focused on a different set of sustainable materials: bamboo composites, recycled plastics, and bio-based textiles. Students touched, bent, stacked, and arranged the materials. After each workshop, students completed a

papers.title: Visual transparency perception: Polarity constraints, X-junctions, and the luminance rules for seeing through surfaces
papers.excerpt: How does the visual system decide whether a surface is transparent? Not by detecting physical transmittance — the eye cannot measure photons per square meter — but by reading geometric and photometric cues in the retinal image that are consistent with one surface lying in front of and being partly visible through another. This study investigated the perceptual rules governing transparency perception, revealing that the visual system applies strict luminance constraints that determine whether overlapping surfaces are seen as transparent or opaque. The polarity constraint is the foundational rule. When the visual system perceives transparency across surfaces, a polarity constraint must be satisfied: at the border between the transparent region (P) and the background (Q), the contrast polarity must be preserved. If region A is lighter than region B without the transparent overlay, then the portions of A and B seen through the overlay must maintain that same lighter/darker relationship. When this polarity rule is violated — when the overlay reverses which region appears lighter — the visual system rejects the transparency interpretation and sees the regions as opaque surfaces with pain

papers.title: Classroom illuminance affects students memory — a neuroarchitecture study using EEG and electrodermal activity across three light levels in VR
papers.excerpt: More light does not always mean better learning — in fact, for memory tasks, it may mean worse. Castilla, Higuera-Trujillo, and Llinares demonstrated in a 2023 study in *Building and Environment* that classroom illuminance significantly affects students' memory performance and neurophysiological activation, with the counter-intuitive finding that lower illuminance produced better memory performance and higher neural engagement than higher illuminance. Forty university students performed a psychological memory task in three immersive virtual environments (IVEs) that simulated identical classrooms differing only in illuminance level: 100 lux, 300 lux, and 500 lux. The VR classrooms were photorealistic simulations of actual classroom geometry and materials, ensuring ecological relevance while maintaining experimental control. The illummiance levels span the range commonly found in educational buildings, from dim (100 lux — below most standards) to bright (500 lux — at or above most standards). Memory test performance decreased systematically as illuminance increased. Students performed best at 100 lux and worst at 500 lux — the reverse of what lighting standards, optimized for visual

papers.title: PDF-0109
papers.excerpt: When Loretta rented a three-bedroom apartment next to the highway interchange, she thought she had found a bargain. A year later, both her children were struggling in school, sleeping poorly, and showing signs of chronic stress. William Sullivan and Chun-Yen Chang open this book chapter from Making Healthy Places (2011, Island Press) with Loretta's story to make a point that most residents never grasp and most architects never design for: the built world shapes mental health through pathways people cannot see or name. The chapter organizes evidence around five pathways. The first is noise. Living near chronic ambient noise above 65 dB correlates with higher blood pressure, worse reading scores in children aged 8 to 11, and more anxiety and depression in adults. The body responds to persistent noise by keeping the sympathetic nervous system running — a stress response built for short-term threats that turns harmful under chronic exposure. The best fix is spatial planning that puts homes far from highways and flight paths. The second pathway is attention recovery. The Kaplans' theory holds that directed attention is a finite resource that wears out under sustained cognitive effort. S

papers.title: Cast shadows and surface perception: How the visual system fails to use shadow shape for 3D inference
papers.excerpt: Shadows tell lies — or rather, the visual system does not listen to what they truthfully say. This review examined how cast shadows are processed by the visual system in the context of three-dimensional surface perception, revealing a surprising limitation: the visual system does not effectively use cast shadow shape to determine surface structure. When cast shadows are present on an ambiguous folded surface like a Mach card, the shadow's geometric deformation — which in principle fully specifies the surface's three-dimensional shape — is largely ignored by the perceptual system that determines how the surface is seen. This finding is counterintuitive. Cast shadows contain rich geometric information. A shadow cast by one surface onto another deforms according to the angle between the surfaces and the direction of the light source. In principle, the shadow's shape is a projective transformation that fully specifies the receiving surface's orientation — you could compute the 3D structure of a folded surface by analyzing the shape distortion of a cast shadow. But the visual system does not perform this computation, or performs it weakly. Instead, it relies on other cues — binocular di

papers.title: Human factors in lighting: visual performance, discomfort, health, and circadian photobiology
papers.excerpt: The human eye does not see what a light meter measures. A photometer registers illuminance — the total luminous flux falling on a surface — and reports a number in lux. But the visual system processes contrast, color, flicker, glare, spectral composition, temporal variation, directionality, and spatial distribution simultaneously, and the perceptual experience of a lit room depends on all of these variables interacting at once. Peter Boyce's comprehensive treatment of human factors in lighting examines how lighting influences visual performance, discomfort, health, and behavior in architectural settings, synthesizing decades of research into an evidence-based framework for design practice. The book begins with the photometric and radiometric foundations. The CIE Standard Photopic Observer defines spectral sensitivity with maximum sensitivity at 555 nm — the yellow-green region — with relative luminous efficiency defined across the visible spectrum from approximately 380 to 780 nm. This is the sensitivity function of the cone-dominated daytime visual system. The CIE Standard Scotopic Observer, operating through rod photoreceptors, peaks at 507 nm — shifted toward blue. papers.title: The brain does not measure physical properties of materials — it builds statistical appearance models
papers.excerpt: Mistaking soap for pâté would be messy. Mistaking ice for dry pavement could be fatal. Yet we almost never make such errors — our visual system recognizes materials and infers their properties with remarkable accuracy across enormous variation in lighting, viewpoint, and shape. Roland Fleming, in this 2014 *Vision Research* paper (Young Investigator Award lecture), proposes a general theory of how the brain accomplishes this: not by estimating the physical parameters of surfaces, but by constructing "statistical generative models" that capture the natural patterns of variation in how materials look. The standard assumption in computational vision is that material recognition works by "inverse optics" — the brain observes an image and works backward to estimate physical quantities like the bidirectional reflectance distribution function (BRDF), surface roughness, or index of refraction. Fleming argues this is wrong on two counts. First, inverse optics is computationally intractable for most real materials because the mapping from physical properties to image features is many-to-one: the same BRDF can produce wildly different images depending on illumination and geometry. Second, it

papers.title: Place attachment in residential environments varies with environmental quality and residential satisfaction
papers.excerpt: People do not attach equally to all places they inhabit — they attach more strongly to residences with higher environmental quality and that produce higher satisfaction. This 2003 study in *Medio Ambiente y Comportamiento Humano* examined the predictors of place attachment in residential environments, testing whether physical environmental characteristics of housing and neighborhood quality predicted the strength of residents' emotional bonds to their homes. The study measured place attachment using established multi-item scales, alongside assessments of residential housing quality (building condition, spatial adequacy, natural light, ventilation, noise levels, maintenance), neighborhood environmental quality (cleanliness, green space, social infrastructure), and residential satisfaction (a global evaluative judgment of how well the home meets the resident's needs). The sample included residents from varied housing contexts, allowing comparisons across quality gradients. Environmental quality predicted attachment strength. Residents in homes with better natural light, lower noise exposure, adequate spatial dimensions, and well-maintained building fabric reported stronger place atta

papers.title: Cognitive Architecture: Designing for how we respond to the built environment
papers.excerpt: Walk down any street in a European city center and notice where your feet take you. You hug the edges. You drift toward building facades and away from the open center of the plaza, even when no cars are present. This is thigmotaxis — a wall-hugging behavior that humans share with rats, mice, and most other terrestrial mammals — and it evolved as a predator-avoidance strategy millions of years before the first building was ever designed. Ann Sussman and Justin Hollander's Cognitive Architecture starts from this observation and builds outward: if human beings are sophisticated mammals with built-in responses to the spatial environment, then architecture that ignores those responses is architecture that fights its own users. The book synthesizes findings from psychology, neuroscience, evolutionary biology, and environmental behavior to offer architects a portable toolkit for understanding why people respond to buildings the way they do. The central argument is that human perceptual systems are products of natural selection, designed to register aspects of the external world that were important for survival — edges, faces, movement, shelter, prospect, and hazard — and that these evolve

papers.title: See JSONL for full title
papers.excerpt: A building's lighting system could one day read your brainwaves and adjust itself — not to a predetermined schedule, but to what your brain actually prefers. Mostafavi, Cruz-Garza, and Kalantari demonstrated in a 2023 study in the *Journal of Building Engineering* that a machine learning classifier can predict whether a person prefers or dislikes a lighting condition from just ten seconds of electroencephalogram (EEG) data, opening a pathway toward brain-computer interfaces for adaptive architectural lighting. Twenty-five participants wore EEG caps while immersed in a virtual reality office environment. Each participant experienced seventeen different lighting conditions created by systematically varying two parameters: illuminance level (the quantity of light) and correlated color temperature (CCT, the warmth or coolness of the light). After each condition, participants rated their arousal, pleasure, and lighting preference. The VR immersion was validated by the Igroup Presence Questionnaire — participants scored 5.2 out of 7 for overall presence and 4.91 for spatial presence, confirming that the virtual office felt sufficiently real for meaningful responses. papers.title: Green space exposure reduces cardiometabolic and mental health risks — a dose-response meta-analysis
papers.excerpt: How much green space is enough? This 2024 study in *Environmental Research* synthesized the evidence from multiple epidemiological studies to estimate the dose-response relationship between green space exposure and health outcomes, finding that even modest increases in neighborhood greenness are associated with measurable reductions in cardiometabolic and mental health risks. The analysis used NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) — a satellite-derived measure of vegetation density — as the exposure metric. NDVI scores range from 0 (no vegetation) to 1 (dense vegetation), with most urban neighborhoods falling between 0.1 and 0.6. The health outcomes examined included all-cause mortality, cardiovascular disease incidence, type 2 diabetes incidence, depression, and anxiety. The dose-response curves showed diminishing returns. The largest health benefit per unit of NDVI increase occurred at low baseline greenness — moving from a nearly barren neighborhood (NDVI = 0.1) to a moderately green one (NDVI = 0.3) produced larger health gains than moving from moderately green to very green. The implication: greening the most deprived neighborhoods delivers the greatest health return o

papers.title: Urban soundscape perception and emotional states: Associations between sound environments and affective responses
papers.excerpt: The sound of a city is not background noise — it is an emotional environment. This doctoral thesis investigated associations between urban soundscape perception and emotional states, examining how sound environments in cities relate to affective responses and psychological experience of place. The research combined field surveys, psychoacoustic measurement, and emotional state assessment across multiple urban locations in Manchester, producing one of the most comprehensive datasets linking specific soundscape characteristics to specific emotional outcomes in real urban environments. The study began with a systematic survey identifying Manchester soundscapes and classifying them along perceptual dimensions. Piccadilly Gardens was classified as Vibrant — a soundscape dominated by human activity, conversation, street performance, and movement. Peel Park was classified as Calm — a soundscape dominated by birdsong, wind, and distant traffic with minimal proximate human noise. A bus stop location was classified as Monotonous — a soundscape dominated by repeating engine noise, air brakes, and idling with little acoustic variation. papers.title: PDF-0140
papers.excerpt: Neuroscience can now watch the brain respond to a room. But few architects know what the scans show, and few neuroscientists know what architects need. Tulay Karakas and Dilek Yildiz at Istanbul Technical University wrote this 2020 systematic review in Frontiers of Architectural Research to map the intersection of neuroscience and architecture as a field and identify what it has found, what it lacks, and where it should go next. The review covers the emerging domain now called neuroarchitecture or cognitive architecture. Over the past two decades, fMRI, EEG, galvanic skin response, and eye-tracking have let researchers measure how people respond to spaces without relying on survey questionnaires. The authors searched databases systematically and identified studies that used neuroscience methods to study how buildings, rooms, and urban spaces affect human experience. The findings cluster around several themes. First, curved versus rectilinear geometry: fMRI studies show that curved rooms activate the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) more strongly than rectangular rooms. Curved rooms receive higher beauty ratings and trigger stronger approach motivation. Second, ceiling height: higher

papers.title: Cross-cultural thermal perception: Swedish and Japanese responses to identical outdoor thermal conditions
papers.excerpt: The same temperature feels different to different cultures — not because bodies differ but because expectations do. This study compared Swedish and Japanese participants exposed to similar comfortable thermal outdoor conditions, finding that Japanese participants estimated the current weather as warmer than Swedish participants did despite experiencing identical physical conditions. The finding demonstrates that thermal perception is culturally calibrated, not merely physiologically determined, with implications for how architects specify thermal comfort in cross-cultural contexts. The experimental conditions were controlled for physical equivalence: both populations experienced outdoor thermal environments within the same temperature, humidity, and wind speed ranges. The thermal stimulus was objectively identical. The perceptual responses were not. Japanese participants rated the environment as warmer, while Swedish participants rated the same environment as cooler. This perceptual divergence cannot be explained by physiological differences (body size, subcutaneous fat, metabolic rate) because the populations were not physiologically extreme — they were healthy adults of similar a

papers.title: See JSONL for full title
papers.excerpt: The best light for your mood depends on how old you are — and what feels warm and comfortable for a twenty-three-year-old may not work for a sixty-five-year-old. Knez and Kers demonstrated in a 2000 study in *Environment and Behavior* that the color temperature of indoor lighting interacts with age to affect mood, producing opposite patterns in young and older adults after ninety minutes of exposure. The experiment used a between-subjects design in which participants worked under one of two lighting conditions for approximately ninety minutes: warm white light (more reddish, lower CCT) or cool white light (more bluish, higher CCT). The lighting was installed in a laboratory room at the University of Gävle, Sweden, maintaining standard illuminance levels used in offices and schools (matching CIE 1986 recommendations). Participants were divided by age — younger adults averaging 23 years and older adults averaging 65 years — and by gender. Three types of dependent measures captured the effects. Mood was assessed using the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS), a validated instrument comprising ten positive and ten negative adjectives rated on a five-point scale. Participants r

papers.title: Building envelope noise abatement and soundscape design: A systematic review
papers.excerpt: A building envelope does two acoustic jobs at once: it blocks noise from entering, and it shapes the soundscape that occupants experience from within. Most acoustic research treats these as separate problems — noise control engineering on one side, environmental psychology on the other. This systematic review bridged the gap, examining 80 published articles on how building envelope elements — facades, balconies, windows, green walls, overhangs — reduce outdoor noise and shape indoor acoustic environments. The central finding: geometric and material building envelope elements can significantly reduce outdoor noise levels, but noise reduction alone does not predict occupant satisfaction. Soundscape quality — the perceptual experience of the acoustic environment, not merely its decibel level — requires an integrated design strategy. The review sample comprised 80 articles written in English and published after 1973, spanning nearly five decades of building acoustic research. Study of outdoor sounds entering through building envelopes began extensively from the 1970s, initially focused on city street noise and balcony insertion loss — the sound level reduction produced by a balcony ove

papers.title: PDF-0148
papers.excerpt: Show 411 people a series of photographs and videos of interior spaces that vary in three ways: windows present or absent, materials natural or artificial, and decor featuring diverse or non-diverse representations of people. Then ask them how they feel. Eva Bianchi, Lucy Zhang Bencharit, Elizabeth Murnane, Basma Altaf, Isabella Douglas, James Landay, and Sarah Billington at Stanford showed in this 2024 study in Building and Environment that each design feature independently shifted a specific dimension of well-being. People spend roughly ninety percent of their time indoors, yet most research on how buildings affect well-being focuses on temperature and air quality. Psychological and social dimensions — belonging, creativity, stress, pro-environmental concern — receive less controlled study. This experiment used a novel immersive online survey method that embeds participants in realistic interior scenes through photos and videos rather than requiring them to visit a physical lab. papers.title: Place and placemaking in architecture: Integrating physical and psychological dimensions of spatial experience
papers.excerpt: A room is a space. A home is a place. The difference between the two is the emotional tie that connects a person to a physical setting — and that tie is precisely what modern architecture has systematically failed to produce. This paper examines the concept of "place" as it has developed across architecture, environmental psychology, and geography, arguing that place encompasses both physical and psychological dimensions, and that a built environment becomes a place only when its occupants develop an emotional bond with it that transforms abstract geometric space into a meaningful location in their lived experience. The distinction between place and space has deep roots in architectural and philosophical thought. Place implies an emotional tie, temporary or lasting, between a person and a particular physical setting — a felt attachment that makes this location different from every other location with similar physical properties. Space, by contrast, is geometric and abstract: coordinates, dimensions, areas, volumes — the measurable properties of a physical environment that can be specified on a drawing and reproduced anywhere. Modern architecture's emphasis on geometric space and ph

papers.title: Place theory: Perception, cognition, and affect in the experience of location
papers.excerpt: Where you are changes who you are — or at least, how you think, feel, and behave. The relationship between humans and place runs deeper than convenience or utility: place affects human perception, cognition, affective properties, and functional behavior in ways that are only partially captured by the physical description of a location. This review traces the development of place theory across geography, environmental psychology, and architecture, examining how the concept of place has evolved from a geographic coordinate to a multidimensional psychological construct. The foundational insight is that understanding place as a unified entity reveals something that analysis of its components cannot. A place possesses emergent character or atmosphere beyond its component parts — what the Germans call Stimmung and the Scandinavians call stemning. A Parisian café is not reducible to its square meterage, furniture specification, lighting level, and acoustic properties. It has an atmosphere that emerges from the interaction of these elements with the social practices, cultural associations, temporal rhythms, and personal memories that its occupants bring to it. This emergent quality is what

papers.title: See JSONL for full title
papers.excerpt: Run your hand across a red wall and a blue wall at the same temperature — the red wall feels warmer. This is not a metaphor; it is a measurable cross-modal illusion. Wastiels, Schifferstein, Heylighen, and Wouters demonstrated in a 2012 study in *Materials and Design* that color exerts a larger influence on the perceived warmth of architectural materials than surface roughness, even when people can touch the materials directly. The experiment presented participants with material samples at the Vrije Universiteit Brussel under controlled lighting conditions (a combination of diffuse natural and artificial light). Each sample varied in color (ten levels, from ice blue through light gray, pure white, ivory beige, chocolate brown, mango yellow, and ruby red) and in surface roughness (ten levels, spanning smooth to very textured). Participants assessed the warmth of each sample by rating it on a seven-point scale from "cold" to "warm" after examining it both visually and by touch. The instruction deliberately left "warmth" undefined — it could mean thermal warmth, visual warmth, or overall experiential warmth — to capture the integrated, multi-sensory assessment that architects and thei

papers.title: Hand movements reveal how the brain recognizes objects by touch — a window into haptic cognition
papers.excerpt: Close your eyes and pick up an object. Within seconds, your hand has identified its material, shape, weight, and temperature — and the hand movements you used to gather this information followed a systematic pattern. Susan Lederman and Roberta Klatzky demonstrated in a landmark 1987 study in *Cognitive Psychology* that haptic object recognition deploys a repertoire of stereotyped hand movements — exploratory procedures — each optimized for extracting a specific object property. The study systematically catalogued how participants explored objects by touch when vision was excluded. Six exploratory procedures emerged: lateral motion (rubbing the finger across the surface to assess texture), pressure (pushing to assess hardness/compliance), static contact (resting the hand on the surface to assess temperature), unsupported holding (lifting to assess weight), enclosure (wrapping the hand around the object to assess global shape and volume), and contour following (tracing edges to assess exact shape). The critical finding was that each exploratory procedure was preferentially deployed when the participant needed information about a specific property. When asked to identify texture, part

papers.title: Aural architecture as affect: How acoustic environments of sacred and concert spaces shape emotional experience
papers.excerpt: The room changes the music — and the music changes the room. This PhD dissertation examined "aural architecture as affect," investigating how the acoustic environments of architectural spaces impact human experience beyond mere intelligibility — shaping mood, spatial awareness, emotional intensity, and sense of place. Using physiological and self-report measures, the research demonstrated that architectural acoustics are not merely the technical backdrop against which sound is heard but an active medium through which sound acquires emotional meaning. The central experiment asked participants to listen to religious recitations — chanting, singing, and prayer — in acoustic environments that varied in their reverberation characteristics. The acoustic environment enhanced spiritual emotions and the intensity of emotional experience. This finding confirms what every churchgoer, mosque attendee, and temple visitor knows intuitively: the room amplifies the emotional power of the ritual. But the study goes further by separating the contribution of the acoustic environment from the contribution of the content. The critical comparison was between dry and wet recordings of the same religious

papers.title: Multisensory integration in virtual environments requires cross-modal consistency to feel real
papers.excerpt: A virtual room can look convincing but feel wrong — and the wrongness comes from cross-modal inconsistency. This 2024 study in *Multimedia Systems* investigated how the integration of visual, auditory, and haptic information affects the perceived realism and user experience of virtual environments, finding that multisensory coherence — the consistency of information across sensory channels — is a stronger predictor of perceived realism than the fidelity of any single channel. The research created virtual environments that varied independently in visual quality (rendering resolution, lighting realism, material textures), auditory quality (spatial audio accuracy, environmental sound richness, reverberation matching), and haptic quality (surface feedback, temperature simulation, vibrotactile response). Participants experienced conditions where all three channels were high-fidelity, all three were low-fidelity, and critically, conditions where channels were mismatched — high visual quality with low auditory quality, or high auditory quality with absent haptic feedback. The key finding: cross-modal consistency trumped single-channel fidelity. A virtual environment with three modestly re

papers.title: Wayfinding in transport environments: Cognitive, behavioral, and environmental factors
papers.excerpt: You land at an unfamiliar airport. The gate sign says B47, but you need to reach the domestic transfer desk, which could be in any direction. There are no windows to orient by, no views of the runway to anchor your mental map. The ceiling is uniform. The corridors branch identically. A sign points left to "Gates B1–B50" and right to "Connections" — but you do not know whether left is north, south, or sideways. You are wayfinding, and the building is making it harder than it needs to be. This review examines how spatial design, signage, landmarks, and cognitive maps interact to facilitate or hinder navigation in transport environments — airports, railway stations, bus terminals, and transit hubs. The review opens with a four-step model of wayfinding: orientation (determining where you are relative to where you need to be), route selection (choosing a path), route control (monitoring progress along the path and adjusting if needed), and recognition of the destination (knowing when you have arrived). A successful wayfinding system allows a person to accomplish all four steps: recognize their current location, establish a route plan, make efficient progress, and confirm arrival. Failur

papers.title: Non-visual effects of light demand spectral specifications — photopic lux alone is insufficient
papers.excerpt: A lighting specification that ignores the spectrum is a specification for vision — not for health. This 2022 study in *Lighting Research and Technology* demonstrated that the non-visual effects of light — circadian entrainment, alertness, mood regulation — depend on spectral composition (particularly the short-wavelength blue content that drives melanopsin-containing retinal ganglion cells) and cannot be predicted from photopic illuminance (lux) alone. The experimental design exposed participants to lighting conditions that were matched for photopic illuminance but differed in spectral power distribution and therefore in melanopic equivalent daylight illuminance (M-EDI). Conditions included warm-white LED (low melanopic content), cool-white LED (high melanopic content), and daylight-spectrum sources — all at the same photopic lux. Outcomes included salivary melatonin timing, subjective alertness, mood ratings, and cognitive task performance. The results confirmed the spectral dependence of non-visual effects. papers.title: Neuroarchitecture: How architectural fluency, complexity, and naturalistic patterns shape brain and behavior
papers.excerpt: Buildings shape the brain that perceives them. Not metaphorically — measurably. This paper investigated three architectural properties that the emerging neuroarchitecture literature identifies as determinants of neural and psychological response: architectural fluency (how easily the sensory-motor system processes a spatial configuration), architectural complexity (how much hierarchical visual information a design presents), and naturalistic patterns (how closely a design's forms, rhythms, and textures resemble those found in natural environments). Each property operates through a different neural mechanism, and each has distinct design implications. Architectural fluency describes the degree of spatial and visual alignment between a built environment and the processing dispositions of the human sensory-motor neural system. The sensory-motor system processes visual and spatial information through expectations shaped by embodied experience — decades of walking, reaching, navigating, and orienting in physical space. When a building's spatial organization aligns with these expectations — when circulation paths follow intuitive movement patterns, when visual hierarchies guide attention

papers.title: Invisible Places: sounding cities — a conference proceedings on sound, urbanism, and sense of place
papers.excerpt: What does a city sound like, and how does that sound shape the experience of urban life? The *Invisible Places: Sounding Cities* conference, held in Viseu, Portugal in July 2014, brought together researchers and practitioners from acoustic ecology, soundscape studies, urban planning, and sonic arts to explore a question that architecture and urban design have largely neglected: how the sonic dimension of the built environment contributes to — or undermines — sense of place. The conference proceedings collected papers that span the conceptual and methodological spectrum of soundscape research. Several contributions addressed the measurement problem: how to characterize the acoustic environment of a place in terms that capture human experience rather than purely physical parameters. Standard acoustic metrics (Leq, Lmax, spectral levels) describe the sound field but predict poorly for perceived quality. The papers explored perceptual metrics, semantic descriptors, and binaural recording techniques that better capture the spatial and temporal character of urban soundscapes. A theme across the proceedings is listening as a design tool. Several papers argued that architects and urban pla

papers.title: Interim study of procedures for investigating the effect of light on impression and behavior
papers.excerpt: Before John Flynn's 1973 study, lighting design was about illuminance — footcandles on the task plane. After it, lighting design became about impression — how the distribution of light makes a room feel. This seminal paper in the Journal of the Illuminating Engineering Society established the procedures for investigating how lighting arrangements affect subjective impressions of spaces, demonstrating that different lighting modes systematically and predictably influence perceptions of visual clarity, spaciousness, relaxation, and pleasantness. The study created the empirical foundation for impression-based lighting design that remains foundational fifty years later. Forty-six subjects evaluated six different lighting arrangements in the same room. The arrangements varied systematically along three physical dimensions: intensity level (bright versus dim), uniformity (uniform versus non-uniform distribution), and position (overhead versus peripheral placement of luminaires). Subjects rated each arrangement on 34 semantic differential scales — pairs of opposing adjectives (pleasant/unpleasant, spacious/cramped, tense/relaxed) anchored at opposite ends of a seven-point scale. papers.title: Place attachment predicts wellbeing — and the mechanism runs through identity, not just proximity
papers.excerpt: People who feel attached to the places they inhabit report higher psychological wellbeing. But is this because good places make us feel good, or because the bond itself — the act of identifying with a place, depending on it, feeling it as part of oneself — contributes independently to wellbeing? This master's thesis from the Middle East Technical University's Graduate School of Natural and Applied Sciences investigates the theoretical relationship between place attachment and wellbeing, arguing that the attachment mechanism matters, not just the environmental quality. Place attachment, as defined by environmental psychologists Lewicka, Scannell, and Gifford, has two primary dimensions. Place identity captures the degree to which a location becomes part of one's self-concept — "I am a person from this place, and this place is part of who I am." Place dependence captures the degree to which a location uniquely supports one's activities and goals — "This is the best place for what I need to do." The thesis reviews evidence that both dimensions predict wellbeing, but through different pathways. Place identity predicts wellbeing primarily through self-continuity and social identity. Peo

papers.title: Aesthetic satisfaction with interior finishing materials: IVR-based assessment reveals gradual convergence
papers.excerpt: First impressions of interior materials change — and they change in a predictable direction. This study used immersive virtual reality (IVR) to investigate how people assess the aesthetic quality of interior finishing materials, finding that the average user satisfaction level was initially 0.4 on a normalized scale and gradually approached a stable value with increased viewing time and interaction. The finding reveals that aesthetic evaluation of architectural materials is not instantaneous but unfolds over time, with implications for how materials are selected in design practice. The IVR methodology provided a controlled yet immersive environment for material assessment. Participants experienced interior surfaces — wood paneling, stone cladding, painted plaster, wallpaper, ceramic tile, concrete finishes — as they would appear on full-scale walls and floors within a virtual room. The VR presentation preserved spatial scale, viewing angle, and lighting conditions that flat material sample boards cannot replicate. A 10×10cm sample of oak veneer on a designer's desk communicates color and grain; the same oak veneer covering an entire wall in VR communicates scale, pattern repetition

papers.title: The Forest and the City: Urban woodland as cultural landscape
papers.excerpt: Cities and forests have grown together for millennia, in a relationship that is far more intimate than the modern distinction between "urban" and "natural" suggests. This book examines urban woodland as a cultural landscape — not wilderness preserved within city limits, but a designed, managed, and culturally meaningful environment that provides multiple benefits to city dwellers including physical and mental health, recreation, environmental regulation, and social cohesion. The argument is that urban forests are not relics of pre-urban nature but products of the same cultural processes that built the streets and buildings around them. Urbanization trends and historical development reveal that city forests have developed alongside urbanization, with close ties between forest management and city planning extending back to ancient civilizations. Roman cities maintained sacred groves within and adjacent to urban boundaries. Medieval European towns managed surrounding woodlands as critical economic resources for fuel, building material, and food products. The Renaissance introduced formal forest gardens and tree-lined promenades as elements of urban design. In the modern era, historica

papers.title: The specific distance tendency: Objects in darkness default to 2-4 meters regardless of true distance
papers.excerpt: In the dark, everything is two meters away. This paper examines a remarkable perceptual phenomenon: when an object is presented in an otherwise dark environment with little or no contextual information, the object is perceived to be at a distance of 2–4 meters regardless of its actual physical distance. This default distance — the specific distance tendency — reveals that the visual system does not see emptiness when depth cues are absent. Instead, it imposes a default spatial framework that places objects at a particular distance, as if the brain cannot tolerate the absence of a spatial model and fills in a best guess. The specific distance tendency was first documented in psychophysical experiments using luminous objects in completely dark rooms. Observers viewed a single glowing target — a point light, an illuminated shape, a phosphorescent disc — with no visible walls, floor, ceiling, or surrounding objects. When asked to estimate the target's distance, observers consistently reported 2–4 meters, whether the target was actually at 1 meter, 5 meters, or 20 meters. Near targets were perceived as farther than they were. Far targets were perceived as closer than they were. All targ

papers.title: Open-plan office noise impairs memory for serial order — and a riverside soundscape during breaks aids recovery
papers.excerpt: Open-plan offices trade acoustic privacy for spatial efficiency — and the cognitive price may be steeper than managers realize. Helena Jahncke, Staffan Hygge, Niklas Halin, Anne Marie Green, and Ulf Dimberg demonstrated in a 2011 study in the *Journal of Environmental Psychology* that exposure to open-plan office noise during cognitive work impairs serial recall memory and elevates stress, and that a subsequent restoration period with a nature-based soundscape (a riverside recording) partially reverses both effects. The experiment exposed 44 participants to two contrasting acoustic conditions: (1) typical open-plan office noise, recorded in situ with irrelevant speech, phone rings, keyboard clicks, and HVAC hum; and (2) a low-level reference condition. During exposure, participants performed standardized cognitive tasks targeting serial recall (remembering items in order), a process particularly vulnerable to acoustic disruption because fluctuating, meaningful sound captures the same phonological loop resources that serial memory requires. Performance on serial recall was significantly impaired by office noise compared to the reference condition — a finding consistent with the irre

papers.title: Facade design parameters shape perceived acoustic quality in urban spaces — a listening experiment
papers.excerpt: The materials and geometry of building facades determine not only how cities look but how they sound. This study evaluated the impact of design parameters — facade absorption, surface geometry, and building-to-street ratio — on the acoustic environment perceived by pedestrians, using a combination of acoustic simulation and perceptual listening tests. The research created auralized soundscapes for virtual urban street canyons in which facade parameters were systematically varied. Absorption coefficient ranged from highly reflective (glass, polished stone) to highly absorptive (vegetated facades, porous surfaces). Surface geometry ranged from flat to articulated (balconies, reveals, textured panels). Building height-to-street width ratio varied from low-rise open streets to tall narrow canyons. Participants evaluated the auralized scenes on perceptual dimensions including pleasantness, loudness, reverberance, spaciousness, and annoyance. The key finding: facade absorption was the single strongest predictor of perceived acoustic quality. Absorptive facades significantly reduced perceived loudness and improved pleasantness, even though the source sound level (traffic noise) was identi

papers.title: PDF-0213
papers.excerpt: Turn up the thermostat, change the lights, and play different sounds through speakers. What combination makes a room feel most comfortable? Wonyoung Yang and Hyeun Jun Moon at Hanyang and Dankook Universities showed in this 2019 study in Building and Environment that acoustics dominate: of the three physical indoor factors — temperature, illumination, and sound — sound has the greatest effect on overall comfort. Indoor comfort research usually studies one factor at a time: thermal comfort in one study, lighting quality in another, acoustic comfort in a third. But real rooms deliver all three simultaneously. How do they interact? Does warm light compensate for cold air? Does quiet compensate for dim lighting? The team tested 60 university students in a climate-controlled laboratory. They set three room temperatures (20, 25, 30 degrees Celsius) and three illumination levels (150, 500, 1000 lux). They crossed these with four sound types (babble, fan noise, music, and water) at four levels (45, 55, 65, 75 dBA). Each participant rated acoustic comfort, thermal comfort, visual comfort, and overall indoor comfort for 25-second exposures. Acoustic comfort rose when the temperature was ther

papers.title: A Kano Model framework for positive soundscape assessment: Activity context determines what makes a 'good' urban soundscape
papers.excerpt: What makes a soundscape positive? The answer depends entirely on who you ask and what they are trying to do. A planner defines a positive soundscape by noise regulation compliance. A serious listener — an audiologist, a musician, a field recordist — defines it by perceptual richness and acoustic fidelity. A space user defines it by whether the sound environment supports their current activity. This paper proposed a framework for improving urban soundscapes by borrowing the Kano Model from product development — a tool originally designed to classify the attributes of manufactured products by their relationship to customer satisfaction — and applying it to the assessment of positive soundscapes. The Kano Model classifies product attributes into three categories. Must-be attributes are baseline requirements: their absence causes dissatisfaction, but their presence does not create satisfaction (a working seatbelt in a car). One-dimensional attributes scale linearly with satisfaction: the more you have, the happier the customer (fuel efficiency). Attractive attributes are unexpected delights: their absence does not cause dissatisfaction, but their presence creates delight (a panoramic s

papers.title: PDF-0222
papers.excerpt: Green buildings win engineering awards for saving energy and water. But do the people inside them feel attached to them? Laura Cole, Sylvia Coleman, and Leila Scannell argue in this 2021 review in the Journal of Environmental Psychology that the same design features that make a building green — biophilic elements, visible sustainability, pro-environmental affordances, and good indoor climate — also make occupants bond with the building through the psychological process called place attachment. Place attachment is the emotional bond people form with specific settings. Stronger bonds predict higher well-being, lower depression, stronger pro-environmental behavior, and greater willingness to defend a place. Most researchers who study place attachment examine neighborhoods, cities, or natural areas. Almost none examine individual buildings, and none before this review examined green buildings specifically. Cole and colleagues use the four-process framework of Twigger-Ross and Uzzell (1996) to organize the evidence. The first process, distinctiveness, occurs when a building helps occupants stand out. papers.title: Noise, cognitive function, and worker productivity: A field experiment
papers.excerpt: Noisy workplaces are common, and the cost of noise is real — but the workers exposed to it do not know how much it is costing them. Joshua Dean demonstrated in a 2024 field experiment published in the American Economic Journal: Applied Economics that increasing noise by just 7 decibels — the difference between a dishwasher running nearby and a vacuum cleaner — reduced worker productivity by approximately 5 percent and impaired cognitive function, but had no effect on effort. Workers tried just as hard under noisy conditions; they simply performed worse. And when offered the chance to pay for quieter working conditions, their willingness to pay did not depend on whether their income was tied to performance, suggesting that they were not aware that quiet would increase their productive output. The study was a field experiment conducted with 128 sewing workers at a textile training facility in Kenya. The experimental design was between-subjects: workers completed production tasks (sewing pockets onto garments) across 4 rounds of 10 working days each, yielding 2,400 session-level observations nested within 157 room-session clusters. Noise levels were manipulated by running engine noise

papers.title: Wood in interior living rooms: Preference ratings, natural materials, and the role of windows and plants
papers.excerpt: People prefer rooms with wood — but not for the reason the timber industry hopes. The preference is not for wood specifically but for the cluster of environmental qualities that wood co-occurs with: natural materials, large windows, visible plants, and a warm material palette that signals domestic comfort and connection to the outdoors. This study used a q-sort experiment to investigate how wood use in interior living rooms affects preference ratings, revealing a preference structure that is more nuanced than "wood is good." The q-sort method required participants to rank 25 living room images from most to least preferred, sorting them into a forced quasi-normal distribution. This forced-ranking approach eliminates the "everything is nice" problem of Likert scales by requiring participants to make trade-offs — choosing which rooms they truly prefer over others rather than rating each in isolation. The resulting preference data reveal both the specific features that drive high ratings and the combinations that characterize the most and least preferred environments. papers.title: Soundscape terminology and definitions: A multidisciplinary review of conceptual foundations
papers.excerpt: What exactly is a soundscape? The answer depends on who you ask. An acoustic ecologist uses the term to describe the total sonic environment of a landscape. A noise engineer uses it to describe the perceptual quality of a sound field. An urban planner uses it to describe the acoustic character of a public space. An ISO standards committee uses it to describe "the acoustic environment as perceived, experienced, and/or understood by a person or people, in context." This review examined the conceptual foundations of the soundscape field, documenting the diverse terminology and definitions that have proliferated as the concept has moved across disciplines — and arguing that this diversity is both a sign of intellectual vitality and a barrier to scientific progress. The soundscape field encompasses multiple disciplines and approaches — acoustic ecology, psychoacoustics, environmental psychology, urban planning, architectural acoustics, music composition, and sound art — each bringing its own vocabulary, methods, and assumptions. The result is that diverse terminology and definitions exist for core soundscape concepts, and the same word often means different things in different research

papers.title: Living space characteristics predict psychological wellbeing — daylight, space, and greenery matter
papers.excerpt: The physical characteristics of your living space predict your psychological wellbeing — and the predictors go beyond square footage. This study in the *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health* examined how specific design features of residential environments — natural light, spatial dimensions, green views, acoustic conditions, and material quality — relate to occupants' psychological wellbeing, finding that environmental quality predicts mental health outcomes after controlling for demographic and socioeconomic confounders. The research assessed living spaces through a combination of objective measurement and validated self-report instruments. Environmental variables included daylight availability (measured or estimated by orientation, window area, and floor level), acoustic quality (noise from traffic, neighbors, mechanical systems), spatial adequacy (room size relative to occupancy), green space views (presence and quality of visible vegetation from windows), and overall maintenance conditions. Psychological wellbeing was measured using standardized instruments covering emotional state, life satisfaction, perceived stress, and depressive symptoms. Natu

papers.title: PDF-0248
papers.excerpt: How complex should a painting be to look its best? Zekun Sun and Chaz Firestone at Johns Hopkins University answered with a Goldilocks principle: not too simple, not too complex, but just right. In this 2022 study in Perception, they showed that people prefer shapes of moderate skeletal complexity over shapes that are either too plain or too intricate. Artists and architects have long debated how much ornamentation is enough. Berlyne (1971) proposed an inverted-U relationship between stimulus complexity and aesthetic pleasure, but testing the idea has been hard because complexity itself is hard to measure objectively. Sun and Firestone used an information-theoretic approach: they defined complexity as the amount of information in a shape's internal skeletal structure. They generated a library of 2D polygon shapes algorithmically and varied complexity by smoothing out structural features — effectively reducing the information in each shape's skeleton. They then rendered these shapes as stylized paintings and mounted them on framed canvases displayed in a virtual room. Participants viewed pairs of shapes and chose which painting looked better. Experiment 1 revealed a clear Goldilocks

papers.title: Optical metamaterials demonstrate metric signature transitions — bridging electromagnetic theory and visual perception
papers.excerpt: Light does not always behave the way Euclidean geometry predicts. Igor Smolyaninov and Evgenii Narimanov demonstrated in this 2010 study that optical metamaterials — engineered structures with unusual electromagnetic properties — can create metric signature transitions where the effective geometry of light propagation changes character, moving from the hyperbolic (one spatial dimension behaves like time) to the conventional elliptical signature. The significance for architectural science lies in what this reveals about the fundamental nature of visual perception. The built environment is perceived through light, and the properties of that light — how it propagates, reflects, diffracts, and interferes — determine what visual information reaches the eye. Metamaterial surfaces can manipulate light propagation in ways that natural materials cannot, bending, focusing, and redirecting light according to principles that transcend ordinary optics. The experimental setup used thin-film metamaterial structures with precisely engineered permittivity tensors. By varying the structure's parameters, the researchers created conditions where the effective metric of the electromagnetic field change

papers.title: Memory retrieval in rats shows familiarity and recollection as distinct processes — implications for spatial cognition
papers.excerpt: How do you know you have been somewhere before — is it a vague sense of familiarity or a vivid recollection of specific details? Howard Eichenbaum, Magdalena Sauvage, Norbert Fortin, and Andy Yonelinas argued in this 2008 paper that these represent two distinct memory processes with different neural substrates, based on evidence from behavioral studies in rats using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis. The dual-process model of recognition memory proposes that familiarity and recollection are separable. Familiarity is a fast, continuous signal — like the feeling that a face or a place "seems known" without any specific contextual information. Recollection is a slower, threshold process — the retrieval of specific episodic details (when you were there, what happened, who you were with). The debate in this paper centers on whether rats demonstrate the same dual-process pattern seen in humans, which would suggest conserved neural mechanisms for spatial and contextual memory. The ROC analysis in rats showed asymmetric curves consistent with dual-process retrieval — evidence that rats, like humans, use both a continuous familiarity signal and a threshold recollection signal

papers.title: PDF-0274
papers.excerpt: Round a wall's corners and watch people's feelings change. Tugce Elver Boz, Halime Demirkan, and Burcu Urgen at Bilkent University showed in this 2022 study in Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts that curving the walls of virtual rooms shifts how people rate the space — making it more fascinating and exciting but less familiar. Previous work on architectural aesthetics used photographs or changed one variable at a time (color alone, size alone). Nobody had varied the shape of walls, ceilings, and floors together while measuring aesthetic response on a full set of validated scales. The team built rooms in VR and changed the curve of boundaries, room size, light level, texture, and color. Participants rated each room on many aesthetic scales drawn from the literature. Principal component analysis revealed three key axes. First, familiarity — how recognizable and comfortable the space feels. Second, excitement — how stimulating and energizing it is. Third, fascination — how captivating and intriguing it appears. Curved walls strongly raised fascination and excitement but lowered familiarity. Larger rooms boosted all three axes. papers.title: Pedestrian density increases walking speed and physiological stress — until a crowd threshold triggers avoidance
papers.excerpt: Walk through a busy train station and your heart rate rises, your skin conductance increases, and you speed up — not because you are late but because the crowd itself triggers physiological arousal. Mira Beermann's dissertation at Forschungszentrum Jülich examines this feedback loop between pedestrian density, walking speed, and psychological stress, providing evidence that the relationship between crowding and stress is not linear but has thresholds that change behavior. The research combined controlled laboratory experiments with field measurements. In the laboratory, participants walked through corridors at different pedestrian densities (from uncrowded to highly congested) while physiological measures — heart rate variability, electrodermal activity, and cortisol — were recorded continuously. Walking speed was measured with precision tracking systems. In the field, natural pedestrian behavior was observed in train stations and urban corridors at varying densities. The central finding: density and stress have a non-linear relationship. At low to moderate densities, increasing the number of pedestrians around a walker does not significantly change physiological stress markers. Bu

papers.title: Meeting environmental challenges: The role of human identity — intrinsic values drive sustainable behavior
papers.excerpt: More information about climate change does not produce more sustainable behavior. This is not a failure of communication — it is a failure of psychology. Tom Crompton and Tim Kasser argue in this 2009 monograph, Meeting Environmental Challenges: The Role of Human Identity, that the barrier to environmental action is not ignorance but values. Specifically, extrinsic values (wealth, status, image, power) systematically suppress the concern for nature and community that environmental behavior requires, while intrinsic values (personal growth, community connection, affiliation with nature) support it. The implication for designers of the built environment is uncomfortable: a building that frames sustainability as cost savings may undermine the very psychological orientation that produces lasting environmental behavior. The argument synthesizes decades of social psychological research, particularly Schwartz's circumplex model of human values. Schwartz demonstrated across large cross-cultural samples that human values organize into a circular structure in which adjacent values are psychologically compatible and opposing values conflict. papers.title: Architecture psychology and the neglect of human psychology in modern apartment design
papers.excerpt: When the COVID-19 pandemic forced billions of people to stay home, many discovered that their homes were not places they wanted to be. The small apartments, windowless bedrooms, and identical layouts of mass-produced urban housing — designed for efficiency and cost minimization — failed to provide the psychological comfort needed for prolonged occupancy. This paper argues that the failure was predictable: modern apartment design does not take thermal comfort, spatial psychology, or sense of belonging into consideration, and the consequence is that people do not feel attached to spaces that were designed without regard for how the human brain orients, remembers, and emotionally bonds with place. The paper builds its argument from three domains. The first is hippocampal spatial mapping. Cells in the human brain's hippocampus are activated by sensory stimuli — visual, auditory, olfactory, and somatosensory inputs — and they play a central role in location encoding and spatial memory. Human sensing and ordering of objects, interpretation of environmental variables, and formation of spatial relationships are processed in accordance with the hippocampal spatial mapping system. papers.title: Pattern perception in architecture: Light, color, gender, and the innate response to artificial form
papers.excerpt: Humans read buildings before they understand them. The perception of architectural form begins with an innate ability to detect patterns — rhythms, symmetries, proportions, contrasts — that the visual system extracts automatically from the built environment. This review examined how three perceptual variables — pattern recognition, light variation, and color — shape the aesthetic and emotional experience of architecture, with particular attention to gender differences in color response. The foundation is pattern perception. Humans perceiving artificial structures deploy the same pattern-detection mechanisms that evolved for reading natural environments — detecting edges, symmetries, spatial frequencies, and figure-ground relationships. The innate ability to perceive patterns in artificial forms means that architectural composition is not a learned cultural skill but a perceptual engagement that the visual system performs automatically. A colonnade registers as a rhythmic pattern before it registers as a structural system. A facade with regular fenestration registers as a visual frequency before it registers as a series of windows. papers.title: PDF-0296
papers.excerpt: Since 1960, the United States poured roughly four trillion dollars into computers, software, and associated infrastructure — about 10% of GNP every year. The returns should have been spectacular. They were not. Tom Landauer's *The Trouble with Computers* (MIT Press, 1995, 425 pp.) marshals econometric evidence to deliver an uncomfortable verdict: for every dollar spent on IT, the U.S. economy gained approximately one dollar back — a nil return on investment. The computer revolution, Landauer argues, helped organizations do more of the same rather than do things differently. Jon May's review in the *Journal of Environmental Psychology* (1996) identifies the core of Landauer's argument. The easy productivity gains — automating repetitive information handling — were a one-off benefit, captured early. Beyond that, IT demanded that whole ways of working be redesigned, not just accelerated. Yet organizations rarely redesigned; they layered new technology atop old processes and wondered why productivity stalled. Landauer locates the solution in User Centred Design, Development and Deployment (UCD). The prescription is concrete: build systems that do what purchasers want, in a way that end

papers.title: Biophilic architecture improves mental wellbeing — a conference review of design principles and evidence
papers.excerpt: Biophilic design is not decoration — it is a mental health intervention. This 2023 IOP conference paper reviews the evidence linking biophilic architecture principles to mental wellbeing outcomes, synthesizing findings from environmental psychology, neuroscience, and clinical studies to argue that incorporating natural elements into built environments produces measurable psychological benefits. The review organizes biophilic design principles into three categories from Kellert's framework: direct experience of nature (plants, water, daylight, natural ventilation, natural materials), indirect experience of nature (images of nature, natural colors, natural geometries, biomimicry), and experience of space and place (prospect-refuge, spatial variability, transitional spaces). Each category is examined for evidence of mental health impact. Direct nature experience shows the strongest evidence. Indoor plants reduce perceived stress and improve mood. Daylight access improves circadian function, sleep quality, and emotional regulation. Natural ventilation improves perceived air quality and comfort satisfaction. Water features reduce anxiety indicators. The evidence base is substantial, dra

papers.title: Designers' finishing material selection for residential interiors: Aesthetic, functional, and client-related determinants
papers.excerpt: When an interior designer selects a floor tile, a wall finish, or a countertop material, what drives the decision? The answer is not a single criterion but a negotiation among competing demands — durability versus beauty, client preference versus design intent, budget versus sensory quality. This qualitative study investigated how designers balance technical specifications with sensory properties and user preferences when selecting finishing materials for residential interior spaces, revealing a structured decision framework that operates across three domains. The study used semi-structured interviews with practicing interior designers, employing qualitative coding to identify the determinants that shape material selection decisions. Analysis identified three main categories of determinants: material-related determinants (MRD), project-related determinants (PRD), and human-related determinants (HRD). Each category contains multiple sub-themes that together describe the full decision space within which material selection occurs. Material-related determinants encompass the intrinsic properties of the material itself. Participants mostly focused on physical and mechanical performance,

papers.title: The NExT conceptual model: Comfort, satisfaction, health, and well-being in buildings
papers.excerpt: Ask a hundred office workers whether they are comfortable. Some will interpret the question as asking about temperature. Others will think of chair support, noise, or air quality. A few will hear it as a question about whether they feel well, or whether they are satisfied with the building. The confusion is not theirs — it is the field's. Comfort, satisfaction, health, and well-being are used almost interchangeably in building performance research, yet they describe different psychological and physiological states with different causes and different design solutions. The NExT conceptual model proposed in this 2024 paper in Building and Environment attempts to untangle them. The model's central claim is that comfort, satisfaction, health, and well-being in buildings are distinct yet highly interrelated constructs, and that treating them as synonyms leads to design errors. Comfort is a perceived transient state arising from the immediate physical environment — thermal conditions, air movement, acoustic conditions, and lighting in the present moment. Satisfaction is an evaluative judgment comparing expectations against accumulated experience over time. Health is an objective physiolog

papers.title: A PhD builds an evidence-based tool for human-centric lighting that prioritizes health alongside energy
papers.excerpt: Lighting standards exist to ensure visibility. They do almost nothing to ensure health. Armin Amirazar's PhD dissertation at UNC Charlotte addresses this gap by developing an evidence-based decision-support tool that helps architects design lighting systems that promote circadian health, visual comfort, and psychological wellbeing — not just adequate illumination for task performance. The core problem is that existing lighting standards (ASHRAE/IES, EN 12464) specify illuminance levels for task visibility: 300 lux for general office work, 500 lux for detailed tasks, 30 lux for corridors. These specifications treat lighting as a visual delivery system. But light also regulates circadian rhythms through melanopsin-containing retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs), affects mood through serotonergic pathways, and influences stress through cortisol regulation. None of these non-visual effects are captured by traditional illuminance specifications. A room that meets the standard for task lighting can simultaneously disrupt the occupant's circadian rhythm, suppress melatonin at the wrong time of day, and fail to provide the spectral composition that supports alertness.
