papers.title: Impact of built environment features on brain activity: A critical review of neuroscience methods in architecture
papers.excerpt: Can we measure what a building does to the brain? A growing body of research uses EEG, fMRI, and other neuroimaging tools to directly record brain responses to architectural features — ceiling height, spatial enclosure, lighting conditions, views, material surfaces, and urban versus natural environments. This critical review examined the published literature on the impact of built environment features on measured brain activity, following PRISMA reporting items for systematic reviews to assess what has been established, where the evidence converges, and where critical methodological limitations constrain confidence. The review applied inclusion criteria requiring that studies be published in recognized journals, investigate the built environment in partial or entirety, and have a generic scope — not limited to a single clinical population or pathological condition. The resulting literature reveals a field with genuine findings but significant growing pains. The most consistent finding involves the brain's response to green versus built environments. papers.title: Five dimensions of spatial experience: Duration, orientation, transition, variation, and destination
papers.excerpt: Architecture is experienced in time, not in an instant. A photograph captures a building's appearance at a single moment from a single position. An occupant experiences a building as a temporal sequence — moving through rooms, turning corners, ascending stairs, pausing at thresholds, discovering views — over minutes, hours, and years. This review proposes that spatial experience is composed of five essential dimensions: duration, orientation, transition, variation, and destination. Together, these dimensions constitute a vocabulary for describing, analyzing, and designing the temporal experience of architecture. Duration is the most fundamental and most neglected dimension. Every spatial experience has a temporal extent — how long the occupant spends in a space, how long a corridor takes to traverse, how long a view is sustained before movement changes the visual field. Duration is not merely a quantity of time but a quality of attention: a five-minute walk through a richly varied spatial sequence feels different from a five-minute walk through a featureless corridor, even though the clock measures them identically. papers.title: Nature views through windows and pictures: Mental health benefits of visual nature access in buildings
papers.excerpt: The prescription is simple: look at nature. The evidence is robust: viewing nature through windows or pictures in the built environment produces improved mental health outcomes including reduced stress and enhanced well-being. This review synthesized the evidence base for visual nature access — both real (through windows) and represented (through photographs, paintings, and digital displays) — as a design intervention for mental health in buildings. The window view pathway is the best-documented mechanism. Ulrich's landmark 1984 study established that hospital patients with views of trees recovered faster, required less pain medication, and received fewer negative nursing notes than patients viewing a brick wall. Subsequent studies extended the finding to offices (workers with nature views report higher job satisfaction and lower stress), schools (students with classroom nature views perform better on standardized tests), and homes (residents with views of vegetation report higher well-being and lower psychological distress). The consistency across building types suggests that the mechanism is robust — it is not specific to the hospital context where it was first demonstrated. The

papers.title: Mu-opioid receptor gradients in the visual cortex: A neurochemical basis for aesthetic pleasure in viewing
papers.excerpt: The pleasure of looking at a beautiful building may have a chemical signature in the brain. This paper examines the distribution of mu-opioid receptors along the ventral visual processing stream, revealing a gradient from sparse receptor density in early visual areas (V1–V4) to progressively denser concentrations in higher visual areas (inferotemporal cortex, perirhinal cortex, and prefrontal regions). The gradient suggests that processing complex, meaningful visual stimuli — the kind of stimuli that architecture at its best provides — produces greater endogenous opioid modulation than processing simple stimuli. Mu-opioid receptors are the same receptor class that mediates the analgesic and euphoric effects of morphine and endorphin. They are distributed throughout the brain in regions associated with reward, pleasure, social bonding, and pain relief. The discovery that they are also distributed systematically within the visual processing hierarchy adds a hedonic dimension to perception: seeing is not just information extraction but potentially pleasure generation, depending on what is being seen and how deeply the visual system processes it. The ventral visual stream is the brain'

papers.title: Emotional responses to multisensory environmental features: Natural sound, enclosure, and architectural character
papers.excerpt: What makes an outdoor space feel inviting rather than indifferent? This study investigated the emotional responses that specific environmental features — natural sounds, spatial enclosure, and architectural character — produce in people who experience them, revealing that the emotional quality of a place is driven by identifiable, designable features rather than by subjective idiosyncrasy. The study used structured observations and emotional response assessments in real outdoor environments, identifying the specific features that triggered positive and negative emotional responses. The findings organize around three categories of environmental features that architects and landscape designers can directly specify. The first category is natural aural elements. Presence of natural aural elements — flowing water in lake or bluespace areas, birdsong, wind in trees, rustling leaves — produced positive emotional responses described as inviting, relaxing, creative, inspiring, and restorative. papers.title: Design for quiet living — acoustic comfort as a primary design driver in residential architecture
papers.excerpt: Noise is the most common environmental complaint in residential buildings — and it is the design variable most often treated as an afterthought. This doctoral thesis at Politecnico di Torino argues that acoustic comfort should be a primary design driver in residential architecture, not a remedial specification applied after the spatial design is complete. The role of architects extends beyond aesthetic composition to mediating complex assemblies of human needs and technical constraints, and acoustic performance should enter the design equation alongside daylight, ventilation, and spatial organization at the earliest conceptual stage. The thesis examines how architectural design decisions at multiple scales affect acoustic comfort in dwellings. At the urban scale, building orientation relative to noise sources, setback distances, the use of barrier buildings, and site layout determine the noise exposure at the building envelope. A building oriented with bedrooms facing a busy road receives fundamentally different acoustic treatment requirements than the same building rotated 90 degrees. At the building scale, facade construction, glazing specification, room adjacency planning, and v

papers.title: Building facade design shapes the acoustic character of urban streets — not just noise levels
papers.excerpt: The acoustic character of an urban street depends as much on the buildings that flank it as on the traffic that fills it. Cristina Calleri, Arianna Astolfi, colleagues demonstrated this in a 2018 study in *Building Acoustics*, combining ODEON acoustic simulation with listening tests to show that facade absorption coefficient, facade geometry, and building setback systematically alter how urban sound is perceived — not just how loud it is, but how it sounds. The research used computational acoustic modeling (ODEON room acoustics software adapted for outdoor urban scenarios) to simulate sound propagation in street canyon geometries. The simulated environments varied in three facade parameters: surface absorption coefficient (from highly reflective glass and stone to absorptive vegetated or porous surfaces), facade articulation (flat versus articulated with balconies, recesses, and projections), and building height-to-street width ratio. For each configuration, the software calculated impulse responses that were used to auralize — to create audible simulations of — the same sound source (road traffic) in each street geometry. papers.title: Perception is cultural — material culture shapes how people see, hear, and touch their environments
papers.excerpt: The same architectural space is not perceived the same way by everyone — because perception itself is shaped by culture. Richard Carp argues in this contribution on perception and material culture that sensory experience is not a neutral registration of physical stimuli but an actively constructed process shaped by cultural training, material traditions, and the habitual practices of daily life. Human perception is saturated with cultural and historical learning, not purely physiological. What you see in a room depends not only on the room's physical properties but on the perceptual frameworks your culture has trained you to deploy. This claim challenges a foundational assumption in environmental psychology and evidence-based design. Studies showing preferences for fractal patterns, prospect-refuge configurations, moderate spatial complexity, or biophilic elements typically assume that these responses reflect evolved, species-wide perceptual mechanisms — universal neural responses to environmental features that trigger the same reaction in every human brain. Carp does not deny evolutionary contributions but argues that cultural learning modulates, amplifies, redirects, and sometime

papers.title: Acoustic perception in large interior spaces: A qualitative study of occupant feelings and cognitive effects
papers.excerpt: Walk into a cathedral, a train station, or a shopping mall and the sound hits you before you register the architecture. The rumble of voices merging into a wash of unintelligible noise, the slap of footsteps amplified by hard surfaces, the electronic announcements bouncing off distant walls — large interior spaces create acoustic environments that are qualitatively different from the rooms that acoustic research typically studies. This qualitative study investigated how people actually perceive and evaluate acoustic environments in large interior spaces, using in-depth interviews to capture the experiential dimensions that questionnaires and sound-level meters miss. The study extracted 932 interview labels from interviewees' descriptions of their acoustic experience in large spaces. These labels were coded into categories that revealed five dominant perceptual themes. Loud background noise was the most frequently mentioned acoustic feature, with 156 mentions across all interviews — confirming that background noise level is the primary acoustic concern in large spaces. But the second and third most frequent themes were not about noise level at all. A large number of simultaneous sou

papers.title: Cool-white morning light lifts mood — but the CCT-affect relationship plateaus above 8500 K
papers.excerpt: Does the color of morning light change how you feel? Chen and Westland tested this with 16 participants exposed to varying correlated color temperatures (CCTs) — from warm 2500 K to cool 8500 K — and measured mood using the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS). Published in the *Journal of the International Colour Association* in 2022, the study found that higher CCT light in the morning boosted positive affect, but only up to a threshold. The experimental design exposed participants to five CCT conditions (2500 K, 4000 K, 5500 K, 6500 K, and 8500 K) at constant illuminance, presented in the morning during a standardized task. EEG was recorded simultaneously to capture neural correlates of the affective response. Each session lasted long enough to allow circadian and mood effects to develop beyond mere novelty. The behavioral results showed an inverted pattern for warm light: 2500 K consistently produced lower positive affect and was described as making participants feel drowsy rather than alert. As CCT increased through 4000 K and 5500 K, positive affect scores rose. The peak appeared around 6500–8500 K — but critically, the improvement between 6500 K and 8500 K was minim

papers.title: Light in architecture: Beyond function to meaning, mystery, and subjective perception
papers.excerpt: Light is the oldest architectural material and the least understood. Despite its functional roles — enabling vision, supporting circadian entrainment, modulating thermal gain — natural light has little contemporary significance in mainstream architectural practice beyond the metrics of daylight factor and solar heat gain coefficient. This review argues that light in architecture deserves a richer conceptual framework, one that acknowledges its capacity to evoke multiple interpretations — some explicit, others obscure — that shape the emotional, spiritual, and aesthetic experience of space. The functional account of light in architecture reduces it to a delivery system: photons to the task plane, adequate illuminance for visual performance, glare control within acceptable limits, energy-efficient daylighting strategies that reduce electric lighting loads. This account produces competent designs that meet standards but miss the quality that makes great architecture memorable. No one remembers the daylight factor of the Pantheon — but everyone remembers the shaft of light that descends through the oculus and sweeps across the coffered dome as the earth rotates. The functional account

papers.title: Neuroscience, spatial cognition, and space syntax: Bridging neural mechanisms and architectural design
papers.excerpt: Turn left at the atrium. Or was it right? The corridors look identical — same flooring, same ceiling height, same featureless walls — and your hippocampus is struggling to build a cognitive map because the building's geometry gives it nothing distinctive to anchor on. Ruth Dalton, Christoph Hölscher, and Hugo Spiers showed in this 2015 book chapter that neuroscience, spatial cognition research, and space syntax analysis can be woven together to explain precisely why some buildings are easy to navigate and others defeat their users. The synthesis produces something none of the three disciplines delivers alone: a neurally grounded theory of wayfinding that maps directly onto architectural design variables. The chapter addresses a gap between three fields that study spatial behavior at different scales. Neuroscience has identified the cellular hardware: hippocampal place cells that fire when an animal occupies a specific location, head-direction cells in the presubiculum that provide an internal compass, grid cells in the medial entorhinal cortex that tile the environment with a hexagonal coordinate system, and border cells that fire near environmental boundaries. papers.title: Circular economy principles applied to buildings require material recovery thinking at the design stage
papers.excerpt: Demolition is design failure at the material level. This review in *Resources, Conservation and Recycling* examined how circular economy principles — designing for disassembly, material recovery, component reuse, and lifecycle extension — apply to the building sector, arguing that the transition from linear (extract → build → demolish → landfill) to circular construction requires design decisions made at the earliest project stages. Buildings are the largest material stock in the global economy. Approximately 40% of global material consumption by mass goes into construction, and demolition waste constitutes the largest waste stream in most industrialized countries. The linear model of building construction treats these materials as consumable — they enter the building as new products and leave it decades later as waste. The circular model treats them as stocks to be maintained in useful service through repeated cycles of use, refurbishment, and recovery. The review identifies the design strategies that enable circularity. Design for disassembly: using mechanical connections (bolts, clips, interlocking joints) rather than chemical bonds (adhesives, welds, monolithic concrete) so tha

papers.title: Human Territoriality: An ecological reassessment
papers.excerpt: Watch two strangers in a library. One spreads a jacket over the adjacent chair, fans notebooks across the table, and positions a coffee cup at the boundary of their claimed workspace. The other, arriving later, reads those signals instantly — and sits elsewhere. Neither speaks. Neither makes eye contact. Both have just enacted a territorial negotiation that ethologists would recognize in robins, stickleback fish, and howler monkeys. But whether the human version runs on the same machinery as the animal version — whether humans defend space by instinct or by cultural convention — remains unresolved after decades of research. This review maps the entire landscape of that debate. The paper assembles and critically evaluates definitions, classification systems, and theoretical frameworks for human territorial behavior, comparing them systematically to the animal territoriality literature that inspired them. Its central finding is sobering: despite forty years of research at the time of writing, the study of human territoriality remained in a pre-paradigmatic stage — rich in definitions, poor in theory, and lacking a comprehensive framework that could organize the scattered empirical fi

papers.title: PDF-0356
papers.excerpt: Look out the window of a 30th-floor apartment and see greenery, water, or a wall of neighboring towers. Which view helps your brain? Mohamed Elsadek, Zhang Deshun, and Binyi Liu at Tongji University showed in this 2024 study in Building and Environment that all window views beat no window view, but green views with water produce the strongest physiological and psychological benefits. High-rise living is now the default in dense Asian cities. Residents on upper floors look out at three dominant scene types: built structures (neighboring towers, roads), greenery (parks, trees), and water bodies (rivers, lakes). Previous studies linked nature views to lower stress, but none had separated greenery from water or measured both brain and body responses. The team used drone-captured aerial panoramas taken from 90 meters — the perspective of a high-rise window. They created six visual conditions: built-only, green-only, water-only, green-plus-water, green-plus-built, and a control (no window). Forty-five participants viewed each condition while wearing a portable EEG headset (Ergo-LAB) and a heart rate variability monitor. They also completed anxiety assessments and subjective feeling quest

papers.title: Soundscape perception depends on the listener as much as the sound — a PhD tracking psychoacoustic and personal factors
papers.excerpt: Two people stand on the same street corner, exposed to the same sonic environment. One finds it pleasant; the other finds it stressful. The difference is not in the sound — it is in the listeners. Mercedeh Erfanianghasab's PhD thesis at University College London's Institute for Environmental Design and Engineering investigates this listener-side variation, tracking how psychoacoustic parameters (the measurable properties of sound) interact with listener characteristics (noise sensitivity, age, cultural background, prior expectations) to shape both subjective soundscape evaluations and physiological stress responses. The thesis bridges two research traditions that have largely operated independently. Psychoacoustics — rooted in physics and auditory science — characterizes sound using parameters like loudness, sharpness, roughness, fluctuation strength, and spectral content. Soundscape research — rooted in environmental psychology — characterizes the subjective experience of sonic environments using perceptual dimensions like pleasantness, eventfulness, and familiarity. Erfanianghasab argues that neither tradition alone can predict how a specific person will respond to a specific son

papers.title: Gender differences in spatial navigation: Males show better maze performance but design moderates the gap
papers.excerpt: Men navigate mazes better than women — but the size of the advantage depends on the maze. This study compared male and female participants navigating three maze designs, finding that males showed better maze completion times and fewer navigation errors than females, but that the magnitude of the gender difference varied significantly across maze designs. The interaction between gender and maze design is the study's most architecturally relevant finding: it demonstrates that spatial design decisions can amplify or attenuate gender differences in navigational ability. The gender difference in spatial navigation is one of the most replicated findings in cognitive psychology. Males outperform females on large-scale spatial tasks — wayfinding, mental rotation, map reading, and spatial orientation — across cultures, age groups, and testing conditions. The effect size is moderate (d ≈ 0.5–0.8 for mental rotation, smaller for real-world navigation) and appears to reflect both biological factors (testosterone-related hippocampal development, evolutionary selection pressures on male ranging behavior) and experiential factors (gender differences in spatial play, exploration encouragement, and

papers.title: Density does not inevitably produce crowding stress — design mediates the relationship
papers.excerpt: More people per square meter does not inevitably mean more stress. Daniel Stokols, publishing in this foundational review in the *Journal of the American Institute of Planners*, drew a distinction that reshaped environmental psychology: density is a physical variable (people per unit area), but crowding is a psychological experience (the perception that available space is insufficient). High density produces crowding only when it impairs the occupant's goals, restricts behavioral freedom, or exceeds the perceived capacity of the environment to accommodate the people in it. The distinguishing insight is that the same physical density can produce crowding in one context and not in another. A nightclub at 2 persons per square meter feels energizing; a library at the same density feels oppressive. The difference is not the density but the interference with desired behavior. In the nightclub, social proximity supports the activity (dancing, socializing). In the library, the same proximity interferes with it (reading, concentrating). Crowding emerges when density creates behavioral interference, loss of perceived control, or sensory overload that exceeds the individual's tolerance. Stoko

papers.title: Interim study of procedures for investigating the effect of light on impression and behavior
papers.excerpt: Walk into a room lit only by wall sconces and track lights aimed at the perimeter, and the room feels intimate, warm, and slightly mysterious. Walk into the same room with all overhead fluorescents on, and it feels exposed, clinical, and efficient. The architecture has not changed. The furniture is identical. The color palette is the same. Only the lighting has shifted — and with it, the entire psychological impression of the space. John Flynn, Terry Spencer, Osyp Martyniuk, and Clyde Hendrick demonstrated this effect systematically in a seminal 1973 study that established the methodology for investigating how lighting arrangements affect subjective impressions of spaces. The study used a demonstration room outfitted with six different lighting arrangements that varied systematically along three dimensions: overhead versus peripheral light distribution, uniform versus non-uniform intensity pattern, and bright versus dim overall illuminance. Forty-six participants entered the room under each lighting condition and rated their impressions on 34 semantic differential scales — bipolar adjective pairs (friendly–hostile, clear–hazy, spacious–cramped, relaxing–tense, pleasant–unpleasant,

papers.title: Isovists predict beauty and pleasingness: Linking spatial geometry to aesthetic experience in architecture
papers.excerpt: Can the geometry of what you see predict whether you find a space beautiful? This study tested whether isovist measures — quantitative descriptions of the visual field available from a given vantage point — predict human aesthetic judgments of architectural spaces. The answer is yes: isovists significantly explain ratings of pleasingness, beauty, and interestingness, providing the first direct empirical link between computable spatial geometry and subjective aesthetic experience. The study used a rating experiment in which participants evaluated architectural spaces — presented as images or experienced in situ — on three aesthetic dimensions: pleasingness (how much they liked the space), beauty (how aesthetically valuable they found it), and interestingness (how much they wanted to explore it). Simultaneously, the researchers computed isovist properties for each space: the area of the visible field, the perimeter of the visibility boundary, the number of vertices (corners and edges) in the isovist polygon, the compactness of the visible field, and the density (ratio of visible area to bounding area). papers.title: Multi-sensory VR workshops for participatory design: Usability, engagement, and soundscape evaluation
papers.excerpt: Can non-experts evaluate soundscapes they have never visited? Virtual reality may provide the answer. This study explored the use of multi-sensory VR environments as tools for participatory design workshops, investigating whether ordinary citizens — not acoustic specialists, not architects, not trained evaluators — can meaningfully assess and provide design feedback on environmental soundscapes when they experience them through immersive virtual reproductions. The workshop participant population included 57 percent female (12) and 43 percent male (9) participants, a balanced gender composition that reduces the risk of gender-biased response patterns. Participants were recruited from the general public rather than from architecture, acoustics, or design professions, ensuring that the feedback reflected lay perception rather than expert evaluation. This population choice is methodologically important: if VR-based participatory design tools are to be used in community engagement processes — where residents have opinions about their sound environment but no technical training — the tools must be usable and meaningful for non-experts. papers.title: Effects of speech intelligibility and masking sound on cognitive performance and acoustic satisfaction in open-plan offices
papers.excerpt: You are trying to hold seven digits in your head — a phone number someone just dictated — while two colleagues three meters away discuss a client deadline. You hear the words "Tuesday" and "budget" and your serial recall collapses. The digits scatter. This is the irrelevant speech effect, and Annu Haapakangas's doctoral dissertation at the University of Turku quantified exactly when it strikes, how badly it hurts, and — most importantly — which masking sounds can block it. The dissertation investigated how speech intelligibility affects cognitive performance in open-plan offices and whether sound masking can restore the losses. The core finding is that the relationship between speech intelligibility and cognitive disruption is non-linear. Speech becomes maximally disruptive at a Speech Transmission Index (STI) of 0.65 — the level where words are clearly intelligible but the listener cannot choose to ignore them. Below STI 0.35, where speech dissolves into an unintelligible murmur, disruption drops sharply. The practical implication is that reducing speech intelligibility through masking is more effective than reducing speech volume. Haapakangas conducted two main laboratory studies

papers.title: Two-component model of soundscape perception: Pleasantness and eventfulness explain 71% of variance
papers.excerpt: What does a soundscape feel like? Not loud or quiet — those are physical descriptions. But pleasant or annoying, eventful or monotonous — those are perceptual descriptions that capture how a listener actually experiences the acoustic environment. This study applied principal components analysis to emotional and cognitive responses to 219 soundscape clips, revealing that two components — pleasantness and eventfulness — explained 71 percent of the variance in how people perceive acoustic environments. The 219 soundscape clips represented a wide range of urban and natural acoustic environments: busy intersections, quiet parks, café terraces, construction sites, residential streets, train stations, forests, and beaches. Participants rated each clip on six semantic differential scales capturing different aspects of the perceptual experience. The principal components analysis identified the latent structure underlying these ratings — the fundamental perceptual dimensions that organize the listener's response to any acoustic environment. papers.title: Luminaire preference and glare: Why regular arrays of recessed luminaires are disliked despite being standard
papers.excerpt: The most common way of lighting offices is also the least preferred. This study compared subjective preferences for different luminaire types and lighting arrangements, finding that regular arrays of recessed luminaires — the default ceiling grid of fluorescent troffers or LED panels that dominates commercial architecture worldwide — are consistently rated as less preferred than alternative luminaire arrangements that provide more varied, more directional, or more architecturally integrated light distribution. The preference deficit of regular recessed arrays is a design failure hidden in plain sight. The lighting industry manufactures millions of 600×600mm recessed luminaires annually. Building specifications default to them because they are inexpensive, energy-efficient, easy to install in suspended ceiling grids, and produce uniform illumination that meets code requirements. But uniform illumination is not what occupants want. The study demonstrates that preference is driven by qualities that regular arrays specifically lack: spatial variation, visual interest, directional character, and the absence of exposed high-luminance sources in the field of view. papers.title: Sound as a supportive design intervention for improving health care experience in the clinical ecosystem: A qualitative study
papers.excerpt: A patient lies on a gurney in a cardiac surgery recovery room. The heart monitor beeps. A ventilator hisses. Somewhere down the corridor, a door alarm sounds and a nurse calls for assistance. The ambient noise level in the ward regularly hits 72 decibels — louder than a vacuum cleaner, and more than 30 dB above the 40 dB that the World Health Organization recommends for hospital rooms at night. Now imagine replacing that alarm-and-hiss soundscape with a gentle stream running over stones, overlaid with distant birdsong. Timothy Iyendo at the European University of Lefke reviewed the evidence for whether that substitution does what it feels like it should: reduce patient stress, lower anxiety, and speed recovery. The answer, drawn from a 2017 review in Complementary Therapies in Clinical Practice, is that pleasant natural sounds reliably reduce self-reported anxiety and physiological stress markers in clinical settings — and in some studies, they reduce pain perception and analgesic medication use. This is not an add-on therapeutic intervention. Iyendo argues that it is a design intervention: sound should be treated as an architectural material, specified and controlled with the same

papers.title: Open-plan office noise impairs cognitive performance — but nature sounds and restoration breaks help
papers.excerpt: Open-plan offices save floor space and money. They also impair cognitive performance, elevate stress, and reduce job satisfaction. Helena Jahncke's doctoral thesis at Luleå University of Technology examined both sides of this equation: how noise in open-plan offices degrades cognitive function, and what restorative interventions — including nature sounds and quiet breaks — can mitigate the damage. The thesis combines multiple experiments. Participants performed standardized cognitive tasks — serial recall, proofreading, sustained attention, and mathematical operations — under different acoustic conditions: quiet, speech noise (irrelevant background conversation, the dominant noise source in open-plan offices), and masked speech noise (speech with added broadband or nature-based masking sounds). Physiological and self-report measures tracked stress, fatigue, and perceived disturbance. The core finding confirmed decades of prior research with sharper precision: irrelevant speech noise significantly impaired performance on tasks requiring serial processing and working memory. The disruption operates through the changing-state hypothesis — it is the fluctuating, meaningful nature of sp

papers.title: Wayfinding in healthcare facilities: Cognitive processes, environmental cues, and architectural design solutions
papers.excerpt: Getting lost in a hospital is not just inconvenient — it is clinically significant. Patients arriving late to appointments miss treatment windows. Visitors searching for ICU rooms experience anxiety that compounds the stress of the medical situation. Staff navigating inefficient corridors waste hours per week that could be spent on patient care. This review examined wayfinding in healthcare facilities, analyzing the cognitive processes involved, the environmental cues that support or hinder navigation, and the architectural design solutions that can transform spatial confusion into spatial clarity. Wayfinding in architectural discourse constitutes the process of determining a route and navigating through physical space from an origin to a destination. The term, introduced by Kevin Lynch in 1960 and formalized by Passini in 1984, encompasses not just the physical act of moving through space but the cognitive work of reading spatial cues, forming mental maps, making navigation decisions, and monitoring progress toward a goal. Wayfinding is a complex phenomenon depending on a variety of cognitive processes including spatial memory, landmark recognition, decision-making at choice point

papers.title: Effects of audio-visual interactions on soundscape and landscape perception and their influence on satisfaction with the urban environment
papers.excerpt: Stand in a city square and close your eyes. The soundscape changes — birdsong that blended into the background now dominates, and a distant jackhammer you barely noticed becomes intrusive. Open your eyes again and the sound itself seems different, because what you see reshapes what you hear. Jin Yong Jeon and Hyun In Jo at Hanyang University set out to measure exactly how much vision and hearing cross-talk in real urban settings, and how that cross-talk shapes whether people like a place or want to leave it. Their core finding: visual information explained 57% of the variance in prominent sound source perception when audio and visual channels were combined, compared to 36% for audio alone (F(8, ?) = 38.10, p < 0.01, partial η² = 0.57). Vision did not merely add to auditory experience — it restructured it. Natural soundscapes were rated more positively when paired with green landscapes than with commercial streetscapes, and the same park scene was rated as less natural when traffic noise replaced birdsong. The interaction was asymmetric: vision dominated, but sound modulated it significantly. This is preliminary evidence from a single laboratory study using a convenience sample, but

papers.title: Architectural lessons from environmental psychology: The case of biophilic architecture
papers.excerpt: A patient recovering from gall bladder surgery in a Pennsylvania hospital room with a window facing a stand of deciduous trees went home nearly a full day earlier than a matched patient across the hall whose window faced a brown brick wall. The tree-view patient also requested fewer doses of strong analgesic medication and received fewer negative evaluative comments from nurses. Roger Ulrich published that finding in 1984, and it became one of the most cited results in environmental psychology. Yannick Joye at the Free University of Brussels used it as the anchor for a 2007 review in Review of General Psychology that asked a broader question: what does environmental psychology know about the mechanisms connecting nature contact to human well-being, and how should that knowledge reshape architectural practice? Joye's central argument is that biophilic architecture — buildings that incorporate natural elements, natural forms, and nature views — taps evolved perceptual and emotional systems that were shaped by millions of years of selection in natural environments. The responses are not learned preferences or cultural conventions. They are rapid, automatic, and affect-driven: environm

papers.title: Systematic review of theories on the built environment and human behavior: From post-1960s to contemporary practice
papers.excerpt: What theories actually govern the relationship between buildings and behavior? This systematic review examined the theoretical landscape that connects the built environment to human psychological and behavioral outcomes, identifying the theories that have been proposed, tested, applied, and — in many cases — left incomplete since the field's emergence in the 1960s. The review identified that theories from the post-1960s period through contemporary research span a wide range of mechanisms, from evolutionary adaptation to cognitive mapping to social construction, with no single framework yet achieving the integrative ambition that the complexity of person-environment interaction demands. The theoretical inventory is substantial. Environmental psychology, ecological psychology, behavioral geography, architectural psychology, and design research have each contributed frameworks for understanding how spatial, material, luminous, thermal, acoustic, and social properties of the built environment affect perception, cognition, emotion, behavior, health, and well-being. The review organizes these frameworks by mechanism type. Evolutionary theories — including prospect-refuge theory, the sava

papers.title: Human-centric school lighting boosts alertness and concentration — a Swedish field study
papers.excerpt: The lights in a typical classroom are designed for visibility — enough illuminance to read a textbook without eyestrain. Tove Karlsson's work on human-centric school lighting goes further, testing whether spectrally tuned lighting that varies in CCT and intensity across the school day can boost student alertness, concentration, and wellbeing compared to conventional fixed-spectrum lighting. The field study deployed tunable LED lighting systems in school classrooms, programmed to deliver cool, blue-enriched light (high melanopic content) during morning lessons to promote circadian alertness, gradually transitioning to warmer, lower-intensity light during afternoon sessions. Control classrooms retained their standard fixed-CCT fluorescent or LED lighting at conventional levels. Students' alertness, concentration, and self-reported wellbeing were measured repeatedly throughout the school day using validated instruments. The key findings: students under human-centric lighting reported higher alertness during morning sessions and better sustained concentration during afternoon sessions compared to students under conventional lighting. The morning benefit aligns with circadian biology —

papers.title: Factors affecting place attachment in residential apartments: A study of Tehran
papers.excerpt: A woman in north Tehran waters the geraniums on her apartment balcony every morning, arranges the cushions on the bench she placed there, and watches the Alborz mountains turn pink at sunrise. She has lived in this unit for four years and turned down a larger flat in a newer building because, she says, "this one feels like mine." Azedeh Khaki Ghasr and colleagues at Shahid Beheshti University wanted to understand what makes that feeling — place attachment, the emotional bond between a person and a physical location — and which features of an apartment and its surroundings actually drive it. Their 2024 study in the International Journal of Architectural Engineering and Urban Planning surveyed 73 residents of mid-rise apartment buildings in Tehran and identified five factors that shape place attachment. The factors are: environmental or physical (landscaping, greenery, air quality, views), sociocultural (community ties, shared cultural identity, neighborly interaction), perceptual-cognitive (spatial clarity, visual order, legibility of layout), economic (property value, affordability, investment security), and historic (neighborhood history, familiar landmarks, continuity with person

papers.title: PDF-0402
papers.excerpt: Does culture shape how you feel in a public square? Parya Khandan and Hossein Rezaei showed in this 2023 study in Quality & Quantity that personality traits rooted in Persian cultural patterns predict how residents of Kermanshah perceive and bond with their public spaces. Most sense-of-place research assumes that physical features alone determine how people connect with a location. Khandan and Rezaei argued that cultural context modifies this relationship: the same square reads differently to people with different cultural values. Using a descriptive-analytical method, the authors surveyed residents about their perceptions of public spaces and their personality traits. They found that culture-specific traits (collectivist orientation, spatial hierarchy expectations, aesthetic preferences rooted in Iranian Islamic design) shaped cognitive maps, affordance readings, and attachment levels. Residents with strong collectivist traits rated social affordances higher; those with heritage aesthetic preferences bonded more with traditionally designed spaces than with modernist ones. For architects designing in cross-cultural contexts, the study shows: sense of place is not universal. A desig

papers.title: EEG and HRV confirm that restorative soundscapes in VR reduce stress — biometric validation of soundscape design
papers.excerpt: Self-report says a forest soundscape is relaxing — but is the body actually relaxing? June Sic Kim, Haram Lee, Yunjin Lee, and colleagues tested this in a 2023 study that combined immersive virtual reality with continuous EEG and heart rate variability (HRV) measurement to assess whether soundscapes designed for restoration produce measurable physiological changes, not just subjective reports of relaxation. Participants were immersed in VR environments with different soundscapes: restorative (nature sounds — birdsong, water, wind through trees), urban (traffic, construction, crowd noise), and control (neutral ambient). The visual VR environment was held constant across conditions, isolating the acoustic variable. EEG was recorded from frontal and parietal electrodes, and HRV was derived from continuous ECG recording throughout each condition. The EEG results: restorative soundscapes increased frontal alpha power — the spectral band associated with relaxed wakefulness, internal attention, and reduced cognitive load. Urban soundscapes increased frontal beta power — associated with arousal, external vigilance, and cognitive effort. The alpha-to-beta ratio distinguished the two conditi

papers.title: A neuroaesthetic approach to the design of open spaces in high-rise buildings
papers.excerpt: Step out of a lift on the thirty-fourth floor of a residential tower in Seoul and into a sky garden. The ceiling soars to a double height. Planters overflow with ferns. Through floor-to-ceiling glass you can see a mountain ridge twelve kilometres away. Your shoulders drop, your breathing slows, and something in the back of your brain registers the space as safe, interesting, and worth exploring. Li Qi and Cho Taigyoun wanted to know exactly which design features trigger that sequence — and whether neuroscience can tell architects how to build sky gardens that reliably produce it. Their 2022 paper in the Journal of the Korean Society of Design Culture synthesizes the neuroaesthetic evidence for four classes of spatial design features — ceiling height, openness, biophilic elements, and visual complexity — and translates each into actionable guidelines for the communal open spaces (lobbies, sky gardens, terraces, atria) that punctuate high-rise residential buildings. The review is a design framework, not a new experiment. It applies existing findings to a specific architectural problem that is acutely relevant in dense Asian cities where most residents live in towers and the quality o

papers.title: A review of artificial windows: Psychological and physiological effects of simulated daylight and views
papers.excerpt: Seal a person in a windowless room and watch what happens to their mood. Stress rises, energy falls, and the walls seem to close in. Staff report lower mood and productivity; employees experience lower job satisfaction and higher absenteeism. The effect is not trivial — windowless environments are associated with measurably poorer sleep, reduced alertness, and disrupted circadian rhythms. Haiyang Li, Zhen Tian, Sijun Zhang, and Min Zou at Hunan University reviewed the evidence on artificial windows in this 2024 study in Building and Environment and asked: can a screen, a light panel, or a simulated skylight replace a real window? The answer is nuanced — yes, partially, and the degree depends on three design variables that most installations get wrong. The review covered studies that tested artificial windows against no-window controls or against real windows across several building types: offices, hospitals, underground facilities, and laboratories. Windowless spaces are everywhere — basements, interior offices, operating rooms, subway stations, underground retail, server rooms — and growing as urban density pushes more occupied floor area away from exterior walls. papers.title: Natural shapes in architectural design: Evolutionary preference, biomimicry, and the neuroscience gap
papers.excerpt: Architects have imitated nature since the first column was carved to resemble a palm trunk. But the contemporary enthusiasm for biomimetic and biomorphic design — curving facades, fractal fenestration, organic-looking structural systems — rests on a claim that has more intuition behind it than evidence. This review examined the relationship between natural shapes and architectural design, finding that while the interaction with nature and natural shapes produces a positive impact on humans and increases energy investment in imitating natural forms, the scientific basis for these design choices remains underdeveloped. The positive human response to natural shapes is well documented at the stimulus level. People prefer images of natural landscapes over urban scenes, curved forms over angular forms, fractal patterns at mid-range dimensions over random patterns. These preferences appear early in development, persist across cultures, and have been linked to evolutionary mechanisms: organisms that preferred environments rich in natural features — water, vegetation, shelter, prospect — had survival advantages, and those preferences were transmitted genetically. papers.title: PDF-0425
papers.excerpt: Visit a virtual museum three times. Each visit, find the same hidden treasure and escape the same simulated fire. By the third visit, you navigate faster and feel less stressed. Jing Lin, Lijun Cao, and Nan Li at Tsinghua University showed in this 2019 study in Advanced Engineering Informatics that repeated exposure to the same indoor environment improves wayfinding performance and buffers the stress of emergency egress — but the two effects interact. Prior wayfinding research typically tested participants once. But real buildings are used repeatedly: workers commute to the same office, shoppers return to the same mall. Each visit strengthens the cognitive map. How quickly does that strengthening happen, and does it persist under stress? These questions matter because building evacuations happen to people who may or may not know the building. The team built an immersive VR museum using a head-mounted display. Participants divided into three groups: one visit, two visits, or three visits, each separated by two weeks. Every visit included a relaxed treasure-hunting task (find a hidden object) followed by a fire emergency egress task (escape the building while smoke fills the corridor

papers.title: Architects and non-architects perceive acoustic environments differently: A qualitative framework comparison
papers.excerpt: Architects and non-architects hear the same room differently. Walk into a hospital ward and an architect hears reverberation time, background noise level, and speech intelligibility — technical parameters that map onto measurable acoustic standards. A patient in the same ward hears whether the space feels calm or chaotic, whether conversations feel private or exposed, whether the acoustic environment signals safety or institutional indifference. Xuhui Liu, Jian Kang, Hui Ma, and Chao Wang at Tianjin University and University College London showed in this 2021 study in Applied Acoustics that this perception gap is systematic: trained architects focus on controlling the physical parameters of sound, while non-architects focus on interpreting and experiencing it. The study used qualitative research methods — semi-structured interviews with architects and non-architects about their perceptions of acoustic environments in various building types. Qualitative coding analysis of architect interviews identified five core categories of perception, while analysis of non-architect interviews identified four core categories. papers.title: Illuminance and color temperature shape comfort, cognition, and physiology in offices and schools — but not linearly
papers.excerpt: Turn up the lights in a classroom and students report feeling more alert. Raise the color temperature toward blue-white and their reaction times improve. But push either parameter too far and the benefits reverse — visual comfort drops, physiological stress markers rise, and the learning advantage vanishes. Chao Liu and colleagues, in a 2024 systematic review in the *Journal of Building Engineering*, screened 122 papers on indoor lighting in schools and offices and found a consistent pattern: illuminance and correlated color temperature (CCT) improve subjective comfort and cognitive performance, but the relationships are non-linear and interact with each other. The review organizes the evidence into three outcome domains. First, subjective evaluations — how people rate the lighting for comfort, pleasantness, and visual quality. Higher illuminance generally improves comfort ratings, but only up to a point; above approximately 500–750 lux (depending on the task and age group), glare complaints increase and satisfaction declines. Higher CCT (cooler, bluer light) is rated as more activating but less comfortable for extended work, creating a tension between alertness and visual preferen

papers.title: Community spaces in permanent supportive housing: Design features that promote social interaction and recovery
papers.excerpt: A common room can save a life — if people actually use it. In a permanent supportive housing facility in Florida, a resident who had spent eleven months avoiding other people started sitting in the lounge near the mailboxes every afternoon. She watched others collect their letters, nodded a few times, was eventually nodded back at, and over weeks began joining the evening cooking group. That trajectory — from isolation to observation to tentative participation to membership — is exactly what communal spaces in supportive housing are designed to produce. Yelena McLane and Jill Pable at Florida State University wanted to know which specific design features make that trajectory happen, and which ones block it. Their 2020 study in the Journal of Interior Design used space syntax analysis, surveys, and qualitative interviews to analyze communal gathering spaces in two permanent supportive housing facilities — one in the UK and one in Florida — that serve residents recovering from chronic homelessness. Permanent supportive housing combines stable shelter with on-site mental health and substance abuse services. Its residents often struggle with isolation, social anxiety, and the aftereffe

papers.title: Biophilic design on campus strengthens place attachment and quality of life for university students
papers.excerpt: University campuses shape student identity. The quad where friendships form, the library courtyard where afternoon light filters through trees, the walkway where the building's facade opens to a garden — these places become part of who students are during a formative period. Pouya Mousighichi, Paniz Mousavi Samimi, and Baharak Mousapour investigated whether the biophilic design parameters of campus architecture directly predict how strongly students attach to their university and how positively they evaluate their campus life. Published in *The Journal of Architecture* in 2024, the study quantifies the relationships that landscape architects have long claimed but rarely measured. The study operationalized biophilic design through three parameters drawn from the 14 patterns framework: physical connection with nature (direct contact with natural elements — vegetation, water, natural materials), visual connection with nature (views of natural scenes from interior spaces), and auditory connection with nature (soundscapes featuring birdsong, water, wind, and other natural sounds rather than mechanical noise). papers.title: Biological versus computer metaphors for spatial cognition: Why physiologically plausible models outperform computational ones
papers.excerpt: How does the brain build a map of space? The answer depends on whether you think the brain is a computer or an organism. This study compared two metaphors for theories of spatial cognition — the computer metaphor, which models spatial processing as symbolic computation over abstract representations, and the biological metaphor, which models spatial processing through physiologically plausible mechanisms grounded in neural architecture. The finding is that the biological metaphor utilizing physiologically plausible mechanisms provides a more accurate and more productive framework for understanding how organisms — including human building occupants — navigate, orient, and build spatial knowledge. The computer metaphor dominated cognitive science for decades. It models spatial cognition as information processing: sensory inputs are encoded into symbolic representations (spatial coordinates, landmark labels, route descriptions), stored in memory (spatial databases, cognitive maps as data structures), and retrieved for computation (path planning algorithms, distance estimation calculations). This model is computationally precise, elegant, and implementable in software — but it describes

papers.title: Beyond sight: Multisensory architectural discourse and the phenomenology of environmental understanding
papers.excerpt: Architecture is not a visual art — or at least, it should not be only a visual art. This paper examines contemporary architectural discourse on environmental understanding, arguing that the emphasis on sensory perception beyond sight alone suggests a more complete, embodied, and phenomenologically grounded approach to design. The building as photograph and the building as experience are fundamentally different objects, and the discipline's century-long privileging of the visual has produced buildings that look extraordinary but feel ordinary — or worse. The visual dominance of architectural culture is pervasive. Design is communicated through drawings — plans, sections, elevations, perspectives, renders — that are exclusively visual media. Design is evaluated through photographs that capture a building's formal composition in a single frozen frame. Design is awarded through competitions judged from image boards. Design is taught through studio critiques where projects are pinned to a wall and assessed from a distance. At no point in this process does anyone hear the building, touch its surfaces, smell its materials, feel its temperature, or move through its spaces. The entire appar

papers.title: Hue-heat effect quantified: An analytical model incorporating CCT into thermal comfort prediction
papers.excerpt: Change the color of the light and people feel warmer — even when the thermostat stays put. This is the hue-heat effect, and it has been observed for decades without being precisely quantified. Amir Omidvar and Arianna Brambilla at Shiraz University of Technology and the University of Sydney showed in this 2021 study in Building and Environment that the effect is real enough to model mathematically: lighting color temperature shifts thermal sensation by up to 0.8 units on the standard PMV (Predicted Mean Vote) comfort scale, and they developed an analytical expression that predicts the shift for any combination of air temperature and CCT. The hue-heat hypothesis proposes that warm-colored light (low correlated color temperature, reddish — 2700 to 3000 K) makes people feel warmer, while cool-colored light (high CCT, bluish — 5000 to 6500 K) makes them feel cooler. Previous studies confirmed the effect exists — subjects in rooms lit with warm light report higher thermal sensation votes than subjects in identically heated rooms lit with cool light — but none had quantified the effect precisely enough to incorporate into standard thermal comfort prediction models like Fanger's PMV equat

papers.title: Thermal comfort is not thermal neutrality — the body seeks thermal pleasure through alliesthesia
papers.excerpt: The thermostat on the wall aims for thermal neutrality — a condition where the body is neither too warm nor too cold. Thomas Parkinson's PhD thesis at the University of Sydney argues that this target is physiologically and psychologically wrong. Humans do not seek thermal neutrality. They seek thermal pleasure — and the two are different things, governed by different neural mechanisms. The concept that unifies the thesis is alliesthesia, coined by physiologist Michel Cabanac in 1971: the hedonic value of a stimulus depends on the body's internal state. A cool breeze feels delightful when you are overheated and unpleasant when you are chilled. The same physical stimulus — the same air temperature, the same air velocity — produces opposite affective responses depending on the body's thermal load. Thermal pleasure, therefore, is not a property of the environment but a property of the relationship between the environment and the body's homeostatic need. papers.title: EEG shows nature-rich learning environments boost frontal alpha asymmetry and cognitive performance
papers.excerpt: Most of what we know about neuro-architecture comes from photographs shown in scanners. Shreya Rai, Venu Shree, P.S. Chani, and Farhan Asim wanted something closer to reality. In a 2024 study published in *Visions for Sustainability*, they measured frontal alpha asymmetry — an EEG marker of approach motivation and positive affect — while participants experienced learning environments with varying degrees of nature integration. The nature-rich environments won, and the EEG confirmed the behavioral data. The study used a between-subjects design comparing learning environments that differed in biophilic design density. Participants performed standardized cognitive tasks — including working memory (digit span), visual retention (Benton Visual Retention Test), processing speed (Digit Symbol Substitution Test), and sustained attention — in environments that ranged from conventional (no natural elements) to nature-integrated (vegetation, natural materials, daylight access, and natural views). EEG was recorded continuously during both the task performance and baseline rest periods. papers.title: Gaudí's Sagrada Família: Biomimicry, biophilic design, and the structural logic of nature in architecture
papers.excerpt: Nature does not build cathedrals — but Antoni Gaudí tried to build one the way nature would. The Sagrada Família in Barcelona stands as the most ambitious experiment in architectural biomimicry ever attempted: a building whose structural system, spatial organization, light strategy, and ornamental program derive systematically from the geometry of biological organisms. This review examines how Gaudí's design philosophy combines biomimicry with biophilic qualities, creating a synthesis that functions simultaneously as engineering innovation, spiritual architecture, and built-environment evidence for the human response to natural form. Gaudí's structural innovations translate biological principles into architectural structure. The equilibrated structure — a weighted block system developed through physical models using hanging chains and sandbags — eliminates lateral load and thrust in thin vaults without framework, creating a structural system that works entirely in compression. This is how trees work: a tree trunk and its branching canopy distribute gravitational and wind loads through compressive and tensile pathways that follow the geometry of natural growth. papers.title: PDF-0468
papers.excerpt: A student sitting in the back of a lecture hall strains to hear the professor. The air conditioner hums. Traffic leaks through the windows. The student behind her whispers. By semester's end, her exam scores trail the front-row students by a full grade. Abdul Wafi Razali, Nazli Bin Che Din, Musli Nizam Yahya, and Raha Sulaiman at Universiti Malaya showed in this 2024 systematic review in the Journal of Building Engineering that classroom acoustic comfort depends on six design enablers, not just how loud the teacher talks. Good acoustics are the invisible infrastructure of learning. Students spend 45–75% of class time listening, and what they hear depends not just on the teacher's voice but on how the room handles sound. Past studies linked poor acoustics to lower grades, higher teacher burnout, and worse outcomes for non-native speakers and students with hearing loss. But no review had organized the design factors into a usable framework for architects. The team reviewed 74 publications and identified six design enablers for classroom acoustic comfort. Enabler one: material — the absorption coefficients of ceiling tiles, wall panels, and floor coverings control how much sound bounc

papers.title: Restaurant noise feeds on itself — the Lombard effect makes diners shout, making it louder still
papers.excerpt: Restaurants get louder than physics alone predicts. Jens Holger Rindel, publishing in a 2017 study, developed a prediction model that accounts for a phenomenon most acoustic calculations ignore: the Lombard effect, in which speakers unconsciously raise their voice level in response to background noise — which then raises the background noise further, which raises voice levels again, in a positive feedback loop that can drive sound levels in a busy restaurant above 80 dB(A). Traditional room acoustic models predict restaurant noise by summing the acoustic power of individual sound sources (diners) in a room with known absorption and geometry. These models treat each diner as a fixed-output source, typically assuming a speech level of 55–60 dB(A) at one meter. Rindel showed that this assumption is wrong because speech level is not fixed — it rises with ambient noise at a rate of approximately 0.5 dB per dB of background noise increase (the Lombard slope). In a room where ten diners produce a modest background level, adding ten more produces a disproportionate increase because each diner's speech level ratchets upward. The model Rindel developed includes the Lombard feedback as an ite

papers.title: Interior housing quality predicts child psychological wellbeing — independent of socioeconomic status
papers.excerpt: The physical quality of a child's home predicts their psychological wellbeing — and this effect persists after controlling for family income, parental education, and neighborhood quality. Kimberly Rollings and Gary Evans demonstrated this in a 2019 cross-sectional study in *Environment and Behavior*, measuring the interior physical conditions of homes where children (mean age approximately 9 years) lived and linking those conditions to standardized measures of psychological functioning. Interior housing quality was assessed through direct observation of the home environment, rating physical characteristics including structural quality (maintenance, disrepair, crowding), noise levels, natural light access, thermal comfort, indoor air quality indicators, and the presence of clutter and disorder. This observational assessment captures environmental quality that survey methods miss — the actual state of walls, windows, lighting, and spatial order, not just the occupant's perception of them. The central finding: poorer interior housing quality was associated with elevated psychological distress in children, including higher levels of both internalizing symptoms (anxiety, withdrawal, dep

papers.title: Lighting CCT and intensity modulate emotion — physiological and psychological evidence
papers.excerpt: The color and intensity of artificial light do not just affect what you can see — they affect how you feel. A warm, dim lamp at 2700 K and 100 lux makes a dining room feel intimate and relaxing. The same room lit to 6500 K and 1000 lux by a cool-white LED panel feels clinical and activating. Dalia Saleem and Morteza Nazari-Heris reviewed the evidence for these emotional responses in a 2022 book chapter, tracing the physiological and psychological pathways through which lighting correlated color temperature (CCT) and intensity modulate human emotion. The two pathways operate in parallel, sometimes reinforcing each other and sometimes creating design tension that only tunable lighting systems can resolve. The physiological pathway operates through the melanopsin system. Intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) in the retina respond preferentially to short-wavelength blue light, peaking near 480 nm. These cells project to three downstream targets: the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) for circadian entrainment, subcortical emotion centers including the amygdala and habenula for acute alertness and mood modulation, and the olivary pretectal nucleus for pupillary control

papers.title: Thermal comfort affects cognitive performance — a dissertation on temperature and mental work
papers.excerpt: Can the temperature of your office change the quality of your thinking? Elizabeth Anne Scarbrough's doctoral dissertation investigates the relationship between thermal comfort and cognitive performance, examining how temperature deviations from the comfort zone affect different types of mental work — and finding that the relationship is neither simple nor linear. The research combined controlled laboratory experiments with physiological monitoring and cognitive testing. Participants performed standardized cognitive tasks — working memory, attention, executive function, and creative thinking — under thermal conditions that varied from slightly cool to comfortable to slightly warm. Body temperature, skin temperature, and self-reported thermal comfort were measured continuously. The central finding: moderate thermal discomfort — both warm and cool — impaired performance on tasks requiring sustained attention and working memory, but the impairment was asymmetric. Warm conditions (above 26°C) produced greater cognitive decrements than equivalently uncomfortable cool conditions. The mechanism appears to be that warmth increases metabolic load and diverts physiological resources toward th

papers.title: Light and colour affect mood and physiology through different pathways — but we cannot yet predict which way
papers.excerpt: Warm colors feel activating. Red and yellow quicken the pulse, or so designers assume. But the physiologically activating wavelengths sit at the opposite end of the spectrum — short-wavelength blue light suppresses melatonin and drives alertness through intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs). Siniscalco and Bortolotti, presenting at the XVII Color Conference in Lecco (2023), lay out the methodological challenge this disconnect creates for anyone trying to design lighting that reliably steers mood and performance. Their conference paper describes the process of building a rigorous experimental protocol to test whether predictable correlations exist between the physiology and psychology of light and colour perception. The premise is straightforward: since the discovery of ipRGCs and their role in circadian regulation, designers have a biological mechanism to exploit. Light synchronizes hormonal rhythms, sleep quality, alertness, cognitive performance, and mood (Cajochen, 2007). But most of these effects have been demonstrated only under controlled laboratory conditions with artificial light. Whether they generalize to real-world interior environments — with thei

papers.title: Soundscape-integrated landscape architecture: Designing for auditory-visual congruence in urban parks
papers.excerpt: Stand at the edge of Klyde Warren Park in Dallas and listen. What you hear shapes your experience of the park as powerfully as what you see — and landscape architects almost never design for it. Beth Sipzner at UT Arlington argued in this 2024 master's thesis in Landscape Architecture that integrating acoustic design into landscape practice can transform user experience in parks, plazas, and streetscapes. To make the case, she both synthesized the soundscape literature and conducted an on-site assessment of Klyde Warren Park, producing one of the few studies that combines soundscape theory with a specific landscape evaluation. The thesis identifies the primary aural elements in urban soundscape perception: traffic noise, people and crowd sounds, and water sounds are the dominant sources that shape acoustic experience in urban parks. The primary visual elements are traffic, greenery, and water — a striking parallel suggesting that the same environmental features drive both sensory channels. Auditory and visual stimuli show a positive correlation in urban environments: when soundscape quality improves (more natural sounds, less traffic noise), visual quality assessments also improve,

papers.title: Bright light reduces sleepiness and boosts vitality during daytime mental fatigue
papers.excerpt: Tired after a long morning of focused work? Bright light may revive you — but not instantly. Karin Smolders and Yvonne de Kort at Eindhoven University of Technology showed in a 2014 study in the Journal of Environmental Psychology that bright light at 1000 lux reduced subjective sleepiness, boosted vitality, and improved self-control capacity in mentally fatigued office workers during daytime hours. The alerting effect emerged with a delay rather than appearing immediately, suggesting that bright light builds its benefit over the exposure period — a temporal dynamic that has direct implications for how workplace lighting systems are programmed. Most research on bright light's alerting effects has focused on night-shift workers and sleep-deprived individuals, where the circadian system is fighting the biological clock. But office workers also experience mental fatigue during the day — depleted attention, sagging motivation, reduced capacity for self-regulation — and this daytime fatigue is the more common workplace problem. Smolders and de Kort asked whether bright light helps this specific, ubiquitous form of cognitive depletion. Twenty-eight participants completed a 2×2 within-sub

papers.title: Spatial layout predicts face-to-face consultation in university research centers: A space syntax study
papers.excerpt: Can the layout of a research lab make scientists more creative? Umut Toker and Denis Gray compared six university research centers (URCs) in this 2008 study in Research Policy and found that it can: spatial access and visual openness predicted how often researchers talked face to face — and those conversations predicted stronger research outcomes. The study is one of the clearest demonstrations that architectural layout has measurable effects on the social behaviors that drive knowledge production. The six URCs were organizationally similar — comparable funding levels, similar disciplinary profiles, equivalent staffing structures — but spatially different. Some had labs and offices clustered tightly around shared corridors. Others distributed researchers across floors or buildings with longer walking distances and fewer visual connections between workspaces. This organizational similarity combined with spatial variation created a natural experiment: if research outcomes differ across centers with similar organization but different layouts, the layout is the explanatory variable. papers.title: PDF-0518
papers.excerpt: Listen to fifty recordings of urban outdoor soundscapes and rate each one on 116 descriptive scales. Then let the math reveal the hidden structure. Osten Axelsson, Mats Nilsson, and Birgitta Berglund at Stockholm University showed in this 2010 study in the Journal of the Acoustical Society of America that soundscape perception reduces to three principal components: pleasantness, eventfulness, and familiarity. Soundscape research studies how people perceive the full acoustic environment rather than just measuring decibels. Murray Schafer coined the term in the 1960s, but the field has lacked a quantitative model of how listeners organize their impressions. Previous studies used small sets of scales and found inconsistent dimensions. Axelsson, Nilsson, and Berglund designed a comprehensive listening experiment. One hundred participants heard binaural recordings of 50 outdoor urban soundscapes from Stockholm. They rated each excerpt on 116 attribute scales — from pleasant-unpleasant and calm-chaotic to familiar-unfamiliar and natural-artificial. Principal component analysis of the average ratings extracted three components that together explained 74% of the variance. The first compone

papers.title: Effects of low-frequency noise on health, cognition, and annoyance
papers.excerpt: The hum you barely notice may be hurting you. Not the traffic roar that makes you raise your voice, not the construction racket that makes you wince — but the low, steady, almost subliminal drone of an HVAC system, a distant highway, or a rooftop heat pump, vibrating at frequencies between 20 and 250 Hz. Birgitta Berglund, Peter Hassmén, and R.F. Soames Job reviewed the evidence on low-frequency noise in this 1996 study in The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America and found that its effects run deeper than annoyance: sleep disruption, reduced cognitive performance, headaches, and chronic stress — all measurable at sound levels that standard noise metrics classify as acceptable. Low-frequency noise is defined in this review as the frequency range of 20 to 250 Hz, with some researchers setting the upper boundary at 200 Hz. Below 20 Hz lies infrasound, which the review treats as a separate phenomenon. The critical property of low-frequency noise is that it travels extended distances with very little energy loss — it can cross great distances without the atmospheric absorption and barrier attenuation that reduce higher-frequency sound. papers.title: Neuroarchitecture principles and student preferences for campus public learning spaces
papers.excerpt: Can neuroarchitecture principles improve campus public spaces? Before answering "how," a more basic question comes first: do students actually want the environmental qualities that neuroscience research identifies as cognitively and emotionally beneficial? Qihang Zhou and Xingxing Fang at Hainan University tested this alignment in a 2024 study in Land, surveying a representative sample of students about their satisfaction with existing campus learning spaces and their preferences for design improvements. The convergence was striking: students intuitively want what the neuroscience recommends — natural light, acoustic comfort, soothing colors, and flexible layouts — and the spaces where these elements are most absent produce the highest dissatisfaction. University public spaces — libraries, student centers, open learning areas, specialized classrooms — are typically designed for functional density: maximum seats per square meter, standardized lighting from overhead fluorescent panels, acoustically untreated surfaces, and fixed furniture layouts. papers.title: Formal aesthetics of material texture in design: Perception, regularity, contrast, and congruence
papers.excerpt: Run your fingertips across a surface and your brain builds a picture before your eyes confirm it. The roughness of unfinished concrete, the coolness of polished marble, the warmth of oiled walnut — these tactile properties activate judgment systems that predate language and operate faster than conscious analysis. Hengfeng Zuo and Mark Jones explored the formal aesthetics of material texture in design, asking how roughness, pattern, scale, and material congruence shape aesthetic judgments about products, surfaces, and architectural environments. Their work argues that texture is not a secondary design attribute — it is a primary lever of aesthetic experience that operates through sensory channels designers rarely specify. Aesthetic experience, the paper argues, comes from sensations and senses, not primarily from intellectual understanding. When a person encounters a designed surface, the immediate response is perceptual — is it rough or smooth, regular or irregular, warm or cool, familiar or alien? — and this perceptual response dominates the aesthetic judgment before any cognitive evaluation (this is oak, this was expensive, this matches the firm's brand) can begin.
