papers.title: Passive Design Comfort Assessment Model: Six comfort domains for indoor environmental quality
papers.excerpt: Approximately 90 percent of a person's lifespan is spent indoors — a statistic that elevates indoor environmental quality from a design nicety to a public health imperative. This paper proposes a Passive Design Comfort Assessment Model that addresses the full spectrum of indoor environmental quality through six comfort domains: thermal, visual, acoustic, air quality, biophilic, and psychological/social. The model addresses physiological, biophilic, and psychological/social aspects of comfort that are typically treated separately in building science, integrating them into a single assessment framework for passive building design. The six-domain model represents a fundamental expansion of what "comfort" means in building specification. Traditional comfort assessment focuses on thermal comfort (predicted mean vote, operative temperature) and sometimes visual comfort (illuminance, glare). Acoustic comfort is addressed through building regulations but rarely integrated with thermal and visual assessment. Air quality is addressed through ventilation standards but not connected to comfort perception. Biophilic quality — the presence of natural elements, daylight patterns, material texture

papers.title: Olfactory patina in heritage architecture: Yak butter lamps, cultural smell, and the sensory archaeology of place
papers.excerpt: Architecture is not only seen and heard — it is smelled. And in some buildings, the smell is the architecture. This qualitative study investigated the olfactory dimensions of heritage buildings, focusing on how accumulated smells — olfactory patinas deposited over hundreds of years of use — become defining features of architectural identity, cultural meaning, and place attachment. The central case study is a Tibetan Buddhist temple where the accumulation of soot from burning of yak butter lamps creates a distinctive olfactory patina: a rancid, butyric acid note combined with carbon smoke, beeswax, and incense, layered into stone, wood, and textile surfaces over centuries of continuous ritual practice. This is not a pleasant smell by any conventional comfort metric. Air quality standards would flag the particulate matter. Olfactory comfort questionnaires would score it poorly. But the olfactory patina from historical religious practice carries cultural significance that transcends hedonic judgment — it is the smell of devotion, of continuity, of the hundreds of thousands of butter lamps lit by pilgrims across generations. For the monks and pilgrims who inhabit the temple, the smell

papers.title: Weathering transforms cemetery landscapes — an MLA thesis traces the design tension between permanence and decay
papers.excerpt: Stone endures, but it does not stay the same. This 1998 Master of Landscape Architecture practicum by Koski-Harja at the University of Manitoba investigates the dichotomy of weathering in cemeteries — how the same process that slowly erodes monuments and pathways also creates patina, biological colonization, and age-character that many visitors find deeply moving. The thesis argues that weathering is not simply deterioration to be prevented but a design variable to be understood and, in some cases, embraced. Cemeteries occupy a unique position in landscape architecture because they are designed to persist across timescales that most built environments need not consider. A commercial building's design life is 30–50 years. A cemetery must remain meaningful for centuries. Over those centuries, every material the landscape architect specifies will weather: granite will stain, marble will dissolve, bronze will patinate, wood will decay, lawn will give way to mosses and lichens. The design question is whether to resist this process (through maintenance, material selection, and protective treatments), embrace it (through materials and planting strategies that age gracefully), or navigate

papers.title: Cross-cultural architecture: Beyond Euro-American 'high-style' to a universal theory of built environment
papers.excerpt: Does architecture mean the same thing everywhere? The word suggests a universal category — human-made structures for human habitation. But the conceptual framework within which architecture is studied, taught, and practiced is overwhelmingly Euro-American, built on assumptions about permanence, enclosure, and formal composition that do not apply to many of the world's building traditions. Paul Memmott and James Davidson explored this problem in their 2008 paper in Traditional Dwellings and Settlements Review, developing a cross-cultural theory of architecture that accounts for Indigenous Australian building traditions alongside Western ones — and, in doing so, reveals the conceptual limits of "capital-A Architecture." The starting premise is inclusive: architecture across different cultures possesses intrinsic value within its own cultural context. A Yanomami shabono, a Mongolian ger, an Aboriginal Australian wurley, and a Palladian villa are all architecture — all represent human choices about how to organize space for living. But the Euro-American concept of "high-style" or "capital-A architecture" has failed to achieve a unified position that encompasses non-Euro-American spatia

papers.title: PDF-0679
papers.excerpt: Walk into an apartment where the resident has hung family photos on every wall, arranged furniture to create a reading corner, and painted the bedroom a color that reminds them of childhood. That apartment is heavily personalized. Walk into the next unit and find bare walls, factory-standard furniture, and no sign of the occupant's history. Won Pil Kim and Ki Hyun Lee showed in this 2003 study in Architectural Research that how much people personalize their living spaces depends on their childhood emotional experiences, and that the pattern differs across cultures. Place identity theory holds that the self is partly defined by the physical settings a person inhabits. People personalize their homes to express identity, signal status, and create a sense of belonging. Cooper-Marcus (1976) argued that home serves as a symbol of self. Proshansky (1983) held that place identity forms from accumulated memories of meaningful settings. But whether childhood emotional experiences in specific places predict adult living space choices had not been tested cross-culturally. papers.title: The Practice of Biophilic Design
papers.excerpt: Humans evolved in nature for more than 99 percent of our species' history, yet we now spend most of our time in sealed buildings with no view of a tree, no sound of water, and no variation in light beyond the flicker of a fluorescent tube. This mismatch between our deep biology and modern architecture is the foundation of Stephen Kellert and Elizabeth Calabrese's framework for biophilic design — the practice of integrating natural elements, patterns, and processes into the built environment to restore what evolution designed us to need. The argument rests on a straightforward observation: we are not indifferent to nature. Evolution shaped our perceptual and physiological systems to detect and respond to natural stimuli across a register that runs from threat to restoration. We recoil from snakes and spiders instinctively — subjects shown images of snakes, spiders, frayed electric wires, and handguns respond aversively to the biological threats but remain indifferent to the technological ones, even though handguns are objectively more dangerous in modern environments. This asymmetry demonstrates that the human stress response is calibrated to ancestral risks, not contemporary ones.

papers.title: Nature views, stress recovery, and biophilic compensation: Evidence for designing health-promoting buildings
papers.excerpt: The evidence for nature's therapeutic value in buildings begins with a view from a hospital window. Hospitalized patients with window views of outdoor landscape with trees experienced lower pain, reduced use of narcotic and milder analgesics, shorter hospital stays, fewer negative nurses' notes, and fewer postoperative complications compared to matched patients whose windows faced a brick wall. Roger Ulrich's 1984 study — one of the most cited papers in environmental design research — established the principle that visual access to nature is not a luxury amenity but a measurable health intervention. This review traces the evidence from Ulrich's foundational study through three decades of subsequent research, documenting how nature exposure in built environments influences stress recovery, mood, cognitive function, and compensatory behavior when nature is absent. The stress recovery pathway is the best-documented mechanism. Subjects exposed to a stressor followed by nature scenes or urban scenes showed faster recovery and more positive mood if shown nature scenes compared to urban scenes. The recovery was physiological, not just subjective: cortisol levels declined faster, blood pre

papers.title: Virtual nature videos reduce stress-related brain activity: Digital biophilia as a design intervention
papers.excerpt: You cannot always bring people to nature — but you can bring nature to people. This review examined whether brief exposure to virtual nature videos produces measurable stress reduction, finding that virtual nature reduced stress-related brain activity and increased relaxation compared to control conditions. The finding positions digital nature as a legitimate, deployable intervention for buildings where access to real nature is architecturally impossible. The evidence base spans EEG, fMRI, and autonomic nervous system studies. Brief exposure to virtual nature videos — typically 5–15 minutes of forest scenes, water features, meadows, or mixed natural landscapes presented on screens or through VR headsets — produced measurable neurological and physiological changes. EEG studies showed increased alpha power (associated with relaxed wakefulness) and decreased beta power (associated with cognitive effort and stress) during and after nature video exposure. Autonomic measures showed decreased heart rate, increased heart rate variability (indicating parasympathetic dominance), and reduced skin conductance level. Self-report measures confirmed the physiological findings: participants report

papers.title: Office plants improve perceived health and wellbeing — but not in the way designers expect
papers.excerpt: Put a plant on every desk and health improves. Or does it? Nicole Hähn, Emmanuel Essah, and Tijana Blanusa tested this assumption in a 2021 case study published in *Intelligent Buildings International*, measuring changes in perceived health, wellbeing, and performance in an actual workplace before and after the introduction of living plants. The results confirmed the biophilic hypothesis — but with nuances that challenge simplistic prescriptions. The study used a pre-post design in a real office environment. Employees completed validated questionnaires assessing perceived health (self-reported symptoms, sick days, physical comfort), psychological wellbeing (stress, mood, satisfaction with the workspace), and self-assessed productivity before and after a planting intervention that introduced a variety of indoor plants into the workspace. Environmental conditions — temperature, relative humidity, lighting levels, and CO2 concentrations — were monitored throughout to control for confounding changes. Perceived health and wellbeing improved significantly after the plant intervention. Employees reported fewer physical symptoms, lower perceived stress, and greater satisfaction with their

papers.title: Free sorting of urban soundscapes reveals traffic and human activity as primary perceptual categories
papers.excerpt: How do ordinary listeners categorize the sounds of the city? Not by decibel level. Not by frequency spectrum. But by source — specifically, by whether vehicles or people dominate the acoustic scene. This study used free sorting of 16 urban soundscapes to reveal the spontaneous perceptual categories that listeners create when organizing acoustic environments, finding that two main categories emerged: traffic-dominated soundscapes where vehicular sounds are the primary perceptual content, and human activity-dominated soundscapes where voices, footsteps, and social activity define the acoustic character. Free sorting is a methodological choice with specific advantages. Rather than asking participants to rate soundscapes on experimenter-defined dimensions (loudness, pleasantness, complexity), free sorting asks participants to create their own groups by placing similar soundscapes together and separating dissimilar ones. The categories that emerge are participant-generated rather than experimenter-imposed, revealing how listeners naturally organize their acoustic experience without theoretical prompts. The 16 soundscapes included recordings from streets, parks, plazas, markets, resident

papers.title: PDF-0695
papers.excerpt: Open a classroom window and fresh air flows in — but so does traffic noise, aircraft rumble, and the neighbor's lawnmower. Matteo Pellegatti, Simone Torresin, Chiara Visentin, Francesco Babich, and Nicola Prodi showed in this 2023 PRISMA review in Building and Environment that ventilation and classroom acoustics fight each other: better air through open windows often worsens the sound environment, and the effect on student learning depends on which noise comes through. Indoor air quality and acoustics both shape how well students learn. ASHRAE and European codes push for more outdoor air. Acoustic standards push for lower background noise. When windows serve as the main ventilation path, these two goals collide. The team selected 37 studies published after 1990 using PRISMA guidelines. The studies covered primary schools through universities and tested three noise sources: fan hum from mechanical systems, urban sounds through open windows (traffic, sirens, construction), and natural sounds (birdsong, rain, wind through leaves). papers.title: Going beyond quietness — positive soundscapes require designed sound, not just noise reduction
papers.excerpt: Quiet is not enough. This study in the *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health* demonstrated that positive acoustic experiences in urban environments require more than just the absence of noise — they require the presence of desirable sounds, intentionally designed into the built environment. The research evaluated perceived soundscape quality in urban open spaces that varied in both noise level and positive sound content. The positive sounds included natural elements (birdsong, water, wind through vegetation), human social sounds (conversation, children playing), and designed elements (music, fountains, acoustic installations). Noise levels were measured objectively, and perceived soundscape quality was assessed using the ISO 12913 perceptual framework (pleasantness, eventfulness, calmness). The key finding: perceived soundscape quality did not correlate linearly with noise level. Some moderate-noise spaces with rich positive soundscapes were rated as more pleasant than quiet spaces with impoverished soundscapes. A park with birdsong and a fountain at 55 dB(A) was rated more positively than a quiet courtyard at 45 dB(A) with only ventilation hum. The pre

papers.title: Curved contours increase beauty judgments and approach decisions in architecture — a neural and behavioral study
papers.excerpt: Curved rooms are judged more beautiful than angular rooms — and the brain's approach-avoidance system confirms it. Oshin Vartanian, Gorka Navarrete, and colleagues demonstrated in a 2013 study combining behavioral ratings with fMRI that the contour of architectural interiors (curved versus rectilinear) systematically affects both aesthetic judgments and approach-avoidance decisions, with curved spaces activating reward circuitry and rectilinear spaces activating the anterior midcingulate cortex, a region associated with conflict monitoring and avoidance. The experiment presented participants with images of architectural interiors that varied in contour (curved walls, arches, rounded furniture versus angular walls, sharp corners, straight edges) while measuring both behavioral responses (beauty ratings, approach/avoid decisions) and neural activity via fMRI. Curved interiors were rated as significantly more beautiful than rectilinear interiors. Critically, participants were also more likely to want to "enter" curved spaces (approach) and to want to "leave" angular spaces (avoid). The beauty and approach effects were correlated but partially independent — some curved rooms were judge

papers.title: Interior space geometry and the brain: Curved versus orthogonal forms activate anterior cingulate cortex
papers.excerpt: Do curved rooms activate the brain's pleasure circuit? The evidence says yes — with caveats. This review examined the neural response to the form and geometry of interior space, focusing on the contrast between curved and orthogonal architectural configurations. The central finding across multiple neuroimaging studies is that curved interiors activate the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and produce higher ratings of aesthetic pleasure compared to angular interiors with equivalent spatial properties. The ACC activation is neuroanatomically specific and functionally interpretable. The anterior cingulate cortex is a medial prefrontal region involved in reward processing, emotional evaluation, and approach-avoidance decision-making. It is activated when participants view beautiful faces, listen to pleasurable music, taste preferred flavors, and experience monetary rewards. The finding that it is also activated by curved architectural interiors places architectural aesthetic experience within the same neural reward system that processes other forms of beauty and pleasure. Curved spaces are not merely visually different from angular spaces — they are neurally rewarding in the same way th

papers.title: PDF-0712
papers.excerpt: Architects assume that a well-designed room makes people feel good. But does anyone actually measure the feeling with brain scans and physiological sensors rather than questionnaires? Isabella Bower, Richard Tucker, and Peter Enticott at Deakin University searched the literature systematically in this 2019 review in the Journal of Environmental Psychology and found that almost nobody does. The review started with 237 records. After screening abstracts and applying full-text criteria, only 8 studies (across 7 papers) met the bar: they measured both neurophysiological indicators (EEG, fMRI, galvanic skin response, heart rate, cortisol) and subjective indicators (self-report scales) of emotional response to interior built environments. The near-total dropout rate — 99.7% excluded — reveals how thin the evidence base is. The eight included studies used a variety of stimuli: photographs of rooms, virtual rooms, and one real room. The neurophysiological measures varied: some used EEG power spectra, others used heart rate variability, one used fMRI. Self-report measures ranged from semantic differentials to the PANAS scale. No two studies used the same combination of stimulus, measure, an

papers.title: Interior color preference in residence halls: Blue dominates, but gender and building context moderate choice
papers.excerpt: If you could paint your room any color, what would you choose? For most people, the answer is blue. This study investigated interior color preference across residential buildings using a large sample of residence hall occupants, finding a clear preference hierarchy — blue, then green, then violet — moderated by gender and by the architectural context of the building itself. The primary result was straightforward: blue was the most preferred interior color at 34.7 percent, followed by green at 23.1 percent, violet at 14.2 percent, and other colors trailing. The blue preference in interior spaces is consistent with the broader color preference literature, which has documented blue dominance across dozens of studies in different countries, age groups, and settings. But a universal preference is not the same as a universal recommendation — the fact that blue is most preferred on average does not mean that every room should be blue. The standard deviation around the preference mean, the significant moderators, and the minority preferences all matter for design specification. The first moderator is gender. Interior color preference as a function of participant sex showed significant diff

papers.title: PDF-0714
papers.excerpt: Paint the walls of a virtual room blue. Then measure heart rate, breathing, skin conductance, and brain waves. Isabella Bower, Gillian Clark, Richard Tucker, Aron Hill, Jarrad Lum, Michael Mortimer, and Peter Enticott at Deakin University showed in this 2022 study in Psychophysiology that the color of interior walls changes both how the body reacts and how the brain fires — in ways that self-report questionnaires miss entirely. Built spaces expose everyone to color every day. Workplace walls, hospital corridors, school classrooms — each uses color to set a mood, but rarely backed by evidence about what that color does to the nervous system. Prior studies asked people how colors made them feel. Bower and colleagues measured what colors actually do to the body and brain. Eighteen people entered a CAVE automatic virtual room — a room-sized immersive VR system with projected walls and ceiling. Each person saw three conditions: a resting-state black room, a white room, and a blue room. The rooms were identical in shape and furniture; only wall color differed. While each person sat in the room, the team recorded 64-channel EEG, breathing rate, skin conductance, and heart rate variability

papers.title: Color perception in architecture depends on context — not just the color itself
papers.excerpt: A red wall in a hospital room and a red wall in a nightclub produce radically different experiences — even if the paint is identical. Anna Jaglarz reviews the perception of color in architecture and urban space in this 2023 study in *Buildings*, arguing that architectural color perception is fundamentally contextual: the same hue produces different psychological responses depending on the spatial context, lighting conditions, material surface, cultural setting, and the observer's activity and expectations. The review synthesizes evidence from color psychology, environmental psychology, and architectural research to catalog the contextual factors that modulate the experience of color in built environments. Lighting is primary: the same surface color appears warmer under incandescent light and cooler under fluorescent or LED light. More fundamentally, the spectral composition of the illuminant changes the reflected spectrum of colored surfaces, meaning that a color chosen under showroom lighting will look different under the spectral distribution of the installed luminaires. Surface texture modulates color perception. A matte-painted wall absorbs light, producing soft, diffused color

papers.title: Individual lighting control saves energy and increases satisfaction — people choose dimmer than the standard
papers.excerpt: Give office workers control over their own lights and two things happen simultaneously: they report higher satisfaction, and they use less energy. Guy Newsham and Jennifer Veitch demonstrated this in a study published in the *International Journal of Lighting Research and Technology* in 2000, testing what happened when participants could adjust the lighting in their workstation to their personal preference. The experiment placed participants in a realistic open-plan office simulation and provided them with individually controllable overhead lighting plus a task light. After working for an extended period, each participant adjusted their lighting to their preferred level. The researchers measured both the satisfaction ratings and the actual illuminance levels chosen. The central finding: most participants chose illuminance levels below the standards recommended by lighting guidelines (typically 500 lux for office work in North American standards). When given free choice, participants tended to select 300–400 lux at the desktop, supplemented by task lighting as needed. This is roughly 20–40% below the design illuminance that standard practice specifies — meaning that the standard ove

papers.title: PDF-0719
papers.excerpt: Drive from Zurich's old town into the peri-urban ring and watch the landscape flatten. Half-timbered houses give way to standardized apartment blocks. Chain stores replace local shops. Birdsong fades to traffic hum. Adrienne Grêt-Regamey and Marcelo Galleguillos-Torres at ETH Zurich showed in this 2022 study in Scientific Reports that this flattening does not just look dull — it measurably dulls how people feel, weakens their bond with place, and shrinks their willingness to engage with where they live. Cities worldwide now converge on the same suburban formula: tract housing, big-box retail, standardized road grids. This peri-urban belt erases the visual, auditory, and social cues that make one place feel different from another. Past work showed that emotions drive pro-environmental behavior and place bonding, but nobody had measured whether homogenized landscapes actually trigger weaker emotional responses than diverse ones. The team recruited 285 people (41.4% women, mean age 46, SD 17) on streets in Utrecht (Netherlands) and Olten-Aarau (Switzerland). A mobile VR lab installed in a van showed each person 360-degree audio-visual scenes of urban, peri-urban, and rural landscapes

papers.title: Neural systems for navigation use place cells, grid cells, and head direction cells — a primer for architects
papers.excerpt: How does the brain build a map of a building? Hugo Spiers and Caswell Barry review the neural systems supporting spatial navigation in this 2015 article, summarizing how place cells (which fire in specific locations), grid cells (which tile an environment in hexagonal patterns), and head direction cells (which encode facing direction) work together to create the brain's internal GPS. Place cells in the hippocampus fire when an animal is in a particular location in an environment, regardless of facing direction. Each environment activates a unique constellation of place cells — the hippocampus represents "room A" with a different population code than "room B." When the environment changes, the population remaps — a process that effectively gives each space a unique neural signature. Grid cells in the entorhinal cortex fire in a regular hexagonal pattern that tiles the entire environment, providing the metric framework — the coordinate system — over which place cells are plotted. Grid cells at different anatomical positions produce hexagonal grids at different scales, creating a multi-resolution spatial representation that can encode both local position and global distance. papers.title: PDF-0726
papers.excerpt: Stand in a pocket park in Shenyang on a holiday afternoon and you can barely move. The crowd presses in from all sides. Your mood drops, your stress rises, and you silently decide never to come back. Jun Zhang, Ruoming Qi, and Huina Zhang at Northeast Forestry University showed in this 2023 study in Sustainability that crowding in small urban micro public spaces (UMPS) triggers negative emotions, suppresses positive emotions, and reduces people's willingness to return. Chinese cities face a paradox. Rapid expansion has paved over open land, leaving residents with tiny parks, pocket plazas, and narrow green corridors as their primary contact with public outdoor space. These micro public spaces are decentralized, flexible, and cheap to build. But they crowd easily. Past work linked perceived crowding to negative affect in retail, tourism, and transit settings. This study asked whether the same pattern holds in UMPS and whether the attractiveness of the space can buffer the crowding effect. The team used two methods. First, they distributed a psychological questionnaire to UMPS users in Shenyang, measuring perceived crowding, positive emotions, negative emotions, space attractiveness,

papers.title: The enactive perspective on architectural experience: Embodied sensorimotor engagement with built form
papers.excerpt: You do not look at a building — you move through it, reach for its handles, feel its floors beneath your feet, hear your voice reflected from its walls. Architectural experience is not a visual phenomenon processed by a disembodied mind; it is governed by the embodied sensorimotor activity of the human organism as a unified system, and the enactive perspective in cognitive science provides the theoretical framework for understanding this embodiment. This paper proposes enactive cognition as a guide to studying architectural experience for two key reasons: its capacity to account for the profound connectedness of the organism and the world in an active and dynamic relationship shaped by the body, and its strong foothold in phenomenological thinking that corresponds to existing architectural theory and qualitative design approaches. The enactive perspective, developed by Francisco Varela, Evan Thompson, and Eleanor Rosch, holds that cognition is not the internal representation of an external world but the active bringing-forth of a meaningful world through the organism's sensorimotor engagement with its environment. Applied to architecture, this means that the meaning of a space is n

papers.title: Perceived danger in urban environments is predicted by physical features — not just crime statistics
papers.excerpt: People feel unsafe in specific urban environments — and the physical features of those environments predict the fear better than actual crime rates. This 1998 study in the *Journal of Environmental Psychology* investigated the environmental predictors of perceived danger in urban settings, finding that visual features including concealment opportunities, limited prospect, poor lighting, and signs of physical disorder (graffiti, broken fixtures, overgrown vegetation) systematically predicted fear responses. The study used a combination of site assessment and participant evaluation. Urban locations were photographically documented and rated for physical features: visibility (sight lines, lighting level, openness), concealment potential (hiding places, overgrown vegetation, recessed doorways), maintenance quality (graffiti, litter, broken elements), and escape route availability. Participants viewed images or visited locations and rated their perceived danger and willingness to use the space. Perceived danger was most strongly predicted by low prospect (restricted sight lines), high concealment (places where threats could hide), and physical disorder (signs that no one is maintaining

papers.title: Nature-based workplace interventions improve employee health outcomes — a systematic review
papers.excerpt: Do plants, daylight, and nature views actually improve worker health, or is it just a wellness fad? This 2022 systematic review in *Frontiers in Public Health* compiled the evidence for nature-based interventions in workplace settings, finding consistent associations between nature contact at work and improved mental health, reduced stress, and enhanced job satisfaction — though the causal evidence remains imperfect. The review included studies of indoor plants, window views of nature, daylight access, green roofs, workplace gardens, and nature-themed interior design. Health outcomes examined included self-reported stress, burnout, anxiety, depression, job satisfaction, sick leave, and physiological stress markers (cortisol, blood pressure, HRV). Indoor plants showed the most consistent evidence: workplaces with plants were associated with lower perceived stress, higher job satisfaction, and modest improvements in self-reported health. The effect sizes were small but consistent across studies, suggesting a genuine if modest intervention. Window views of nature showed stronger effects but were available in fewer studies and were confounded with floor level, building quality, and oth

papers.title: PDF-0751
papers.excerpt: Can smart lighting make your home healthier? Moe Soheilian, Géza Fischl, and Myriam Aries at Jönköping University reviewed the evidence in this 2021 paper in Sustainability and found a gap: most smart lighting research targets commercial buildings, while homes — where people spend most of their waking hours — remain understudied. Smart lighting systems can adjust color temperature, intensity, and timing automatically. In offices, these features have been linked to better circadian health and mood. But residential settings have different demands: relaxation in the evening, alertness in the morning, and activities that range from cooking to sleeping. The review covered studies on energy savings, user comfort, and well-being in residential smart lighting. Energy savings were well documented: smart systems reduce consumption by 20-40% through occupancy sensing and daylight harvesting. But comfort and well-being evaluations were sparse. Few studies measured sleep quality, mood, or other health outcomes in home settings. The authors call for research that connects lighting protocols to circadian health markers in real homes. For residential architects and smart-home developers, the revie

papers.title: PDF-0757
papers.excerpt: Ask people to set a dimmer to their preferred light level and they tend to pick the middle of whatever range the dimmer offers. Steve Fotios and Chris Cheal at the University of Sheffield showed in this 2010 study in Lighting Research and Technology that stimulus range bias — not true preference — explains most results from preferred-illuminance adjustment tasks. Lighting standards for offices often cite a preferred illuminance of about 500 lux. This value comes from studies in which participants turned a dimmer until the room 'felt right.' Fotios and Cheal noticed a pattern: when studies used a wider dimming range, participants chose higher values; when the range was narrower, they chose lower values. The 'preferred' illuminance tracked the middle of the available range, not a fixed biological or psychological anchor. The team reviewed previous studies and found that the mean preferred illuminance in each study fell close to the geometric mean of the adjustment range. They then ran their own experiment, giving participants different dimming ranges. The results confirmed the hypothesis: preferred illuminance shifted with the stimulus range, behaving like a centering bias rather tha

papers.title: Dynamic lighting that tracks the sky improves alertness and satisfaction in offices
papers.excerpt: What if office lights changed color and intensity throughout the day, following the sky? A small but carefully controlled experiment at Aalborg University Copenhagen tested a double dynamic lighting system — one that varied both illuminance and correlated color temperature (CCT) to follow the natural daylight cycle — against static office lighting, finding improvements in perceived alertness, visual comfort, and workspace satisfaction. Four participants worked in an office equipped with tunable LED luminaires over several weeks, experiencing both dynamic and static conditions in a counterbalanced design. The dynamic condition tracked outdoor sky conditions in real time: cool, bright light in the morning (high CCT, high illuminance), gradually warming and dimming through the afternoon, and shifting to warm, low-intensity light by late afternoon. The static condition maintained conventional fixed office lighting (4000 K, 500 lux) throughout the day. Under dynamic lighting, participants reported higher alertness during morning and early afternoon sessions — aligned with the circadian prediction that blue-enriched morning light promotes wakefulness. They also reported better visual com

papers.title: Colored light and arousal: How chromatic lighting additions modulate emotional activation in interior spaces
papers.excerpt: White light illuminates — but colored light transforms. This study investigated how cool and warm white light combined with colored light additions (red, green, blue, yellow) affects emotional arousal in interior spaces, finding that colored light significantly influences arousal level, with chromatic additions increasing emotional activation beyond what white light alone produces. The experimental design crossed two white light baselines (cool white at approximately 6500K and warm white at approximately 2700K) with four chromatic additions (red, green, blue, yellow), plus the white-only baselines as controls. Participants experienced each lighting condition in a controlled interior space and reported their emotional arousal — the degree of activation, stimulation, or excitation they felt — along with other affective dimensions. The primary finding is that colored light additions increase emotional arousal relative to white-only baselines. A room illuminated with warm white light plus a red accent produces higher arousal than the same room with warm white light alone. A room with cool white light plus a blue accent produces higher arousal than cool white alone. The chromatic inform

papers.title: Lighting in the workplace as the visual environment that affects occupant mood: A literature review
papers.excerpt: Of the twelve comfort factors that shape how people experience indoor environments — temperature, humidity, air quality, noise, vibration, odor, layout, color, furniture, view, personal control, and lighting — lighting exerts the strongest influence on occupant perception. This review discusses research on the relationship between artificial lighting and mood in the workplace, organizing findings into three focal areas: the effects of illuminance level, the effects of correlated color temperature, and the effects of mixed or interacting factors. The conclusion is blunt: despite decades of lighting research, architects and facility managers still specify office lighting primarily for visual task performance (can you read the document?) rather than for psychological state (does the light make you feel alert, calm, focused, or anxious?). The illuminance level evidence is the most straightforward. Higher illuminance at the workplane — above roughly 500 lux — increases subjective visual comfort and reduces perceived eyestrain. Workers under brighter light report feeling more alert, more energetic, and less sleepy, particularly during afternoon hours when circadian dip suppresses arousal

papers.title: LEED-certified buildings do not consistently outperform non-certified buildings on occupant satisfaction
papers.excerpt: Does a green building certificate guarantee that occupants will be more satisfied? Sergio Altomonte, Stefano Schiavon, and colleagues analyzed occupant satisfaction data from a large database of post-occupancy evaluations, comparing LEED-certified buildings with non-certified buildings — and found that LEED certification alone does not consistently predict higher occupant satisfaction. The study drew on thousands of individual satisfaction responses from the CBE (Center for the Built Environment) dataset, covering thermal comfort, air quality, lighting, acoustics, cleanliness, and overall satisfaction. LEED-certified buildings were compared with matched non-certified buildings of similar type, age, and climate zone. LEED buildings performed somewhat better on air quality satisfaction — likely reflecting the ventilation rate requirements that exceed standard codes. But they performed no better — and in some cases worse — on acoustic satisfaction, thermal comfort, and lighting satisfaction. The acoustic finding is consistent with concerns that LEED's emphasis on open-plan layouts, natural ventilation, and daylighting can create acoustic problems: more glass means more sound reflectio

papers.title: Architectural variables modulating indoor circadian light exposure: Windows, surfaces, and spatial design
papers.excerpt: The light that matters most for health is not the light on the desk — it is the light in the eye. Circadian photobiology has established that melanopsin-expressing retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) in the inner retina drive the non-visual effects of light on sleep, alertness, mood, hormone secretion, and cognitive performance. But the translation from photobiology to architectural design has been hampered by a missing link: what architectural variables determine how much circadian-effective light actually reaches the occupant's eye? This review identifies five key architectural factors that modulate indoor circadian light exposure and provides the conceptual framework for bridging between what the biologist measures (melanopic equivalent daylight illuminance at the cornea) and what the architect draws (windows, surfaces, spaces). The first factor is window location and orientation. A south-facing window in the Northern Hemisphere delivers substantially more circadian-effective light than a north-facing window of identical size, because the south-facing window receives direct solar radiation with high short-wavelength content for much of the day, while the north-facing window receives

papers.title: PDF-0767
papers.excerpt: Give office workers better daylight and bigger views and they sleep longer and think better. Mohamed Boubekri, Jaewook Lee, Piers MacNaughton, May Woo, Lauren Schuyler, Brandon Tinianov, and Usha Satish showed in this 2020 study in the International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health that one week in a daylight-optimized office produced 37 extra minutes of sleep and 42% higher scores on a cognitive test. Previous studies linked daylight to circadian rhythms and cognitive performance, but most relied on surveys or controlled lab exposures. Boubekri's team tested real knowledge workers in real offices over a full work week. The team recruited 30 knowledge workers and had each spend one full work week in each of two office environments. The offices shared the same layout, furniture, and orientation. The key difference: one office had electrochromic glass that maximized daylight and views (40.6 equivalent melanopic lux), while the other had traditional blinds that limited both (316 equivalent melanopic lux). Each participant wore a wrist actigraph to track sleep and took a cognitive simulation test (SMS) that measured higher-order decision-making. In the optimized dayl

papers.title: Ten questions concerning well-being in the built environment
papers.excerpt: What, precisely, does it mean for a building to promote well-being? Is it the absence of discomfort — no glare, no noise, no thermal complaints? Or is it the active presence of positive states — engagement, vitality, belonging, meaning? The answer matters because the two definitions produce different buildings. An office designed to eliminate complaints may achieve thermal neutrality while remaining psychologically inert. An office designed to promote flourishing would also control temperature but would additionally provide daylight, views, social spaces, acoustic variability, and a sense of occupant control. A multi-author expert review published in Building and Environment in 2020 formulated ten research questions that mark the boundary between what building science knows about well-being and what it does not. The paper begins by distinguishing well-being from the concepts it is routinely confused with. Comfort — the quality of being at thermal, visual, acoustic, and olfactory ease — is a necessary condition but not a sufficient one. Health — the absence of disease and the presence of physiological functioning — overlaps with well-being but is not identical to it. Satisfaction —

papers.title: PDF-0773
papers.excerpt: Sit at your desk on a warm afternoon while a colleague two rows away takes a phone call. You feel hotter than you should. The thermostat reads the same temperature as yesterday, when you were comfortable, but today the background noise is higher and the room feels stuffy. Leonidas Bourikas, Stephanie Gauthier, Nicholas Khor Song En, and Peiyao Xiong showed in this 2021 study in Energies that this experience has a measurable basis: thermal comfort, noise perception, and air quality perception interact in cross-modal ways that change how satisfied office workers feel beyond what any single factor predicts alone. Indoor comfort is usually assessed by measuring thermal, acoustic, visual, and air quality factors one at a time, each with its own metric and threshold. But occupants encounter all of these factors together. How warm you feel may depend on how noisy the room is. This study asked whether the factors interact. The team used a three-stage design in three mixed-mode university office buildings in southern England. Stage 1 surveyed twenty-six occupants to establish baseline comfort. Stage 2 collected 1,079 longitudinal comfort surveys examining thermal sensation votes, noise perc

papers.title: Indoor soundscape for health and well-being: Expert perspectives on characterization, management, and design
papers.excerpt: The building industry is shifting from delivering satisfactory spaces to exploring how the built environment can actively enhance health and well-being — and indoor soundscape research is at the frontier of this shift. This paper brings a perceptual perspective to building and room acoustics, arguing that the goal of acoustic design should not be spaces that meet noise criteria but spaces that sound good according to occupants' preferences and needs. Through a thematic analysis of structured interviews with a panel of acoustics experts, the study explored how indoor soundscapes should be characterized, managed, and designed — and what health-related outcomes their design can influence. The traditional approach to building acoustics specifies acoustic performance through quantitative metrics: reverberation time (RT60), speech transmission index (STI), sound reduction index (SRI/Rw), and noise rating curves. These metrics address the physics of sound in rooms but do not address the perceptual experience of the acoustic environment. A meeting room can meet every acoustic specification and still feel acoustically dead — or acoustically harsh — depending on whether the specified reverbe

papers.title: Background noise and annoyance in open-plan offices: The subjective cost of acoustic exposure
papers.excerpt: Open-plan offices are loud — and the people who work in them know it. This study measured the relationship between background noise level and reported annoyance in open-plan office spaces, confirming that increased background noise level produces higher levels of reported annoyance among office occupants as measured via subjective questionnaires. The finding is not surprising. What is consequential is its magnitude, its specificity, and its implications for how architects design workspaces. The relationship between noise level and annoyance in offices is not simply linear. At low background noise levels (below approximately 35 dB LAeq), occupants report minimal annoyance — the acoustic environment fades from awareness. As noise rises through the 40–50 dB range, annoyance increases steeply — each additional decibel produces a disproportionate increase in subjective disturbance. Above approximately 55 dB, annoyance saturates — the environment is already perceived as noisy, and further increases produce diminishing increments of additional complaint. This S-shaped dose-response curve means that the critical design zone is 35–50 dB: the range where architectural decisions about acousti

papers.title: Biophilic design for restoration and therapy: Strategies that support psychological well-being in workplaces and healthcare
papers.excerpt: Biophilic design is not a decorating strategy — it is a therapeutic intervention. This review examined the application of biophilic design strategies in built environments, focusing on how specific nature-based design elements produce enhanced restoration and therapy support for psychological well-being within workplaces and healthcare facilities. The evidence is now sufficient to move beyond the claim that "nature is good" toward specifying which biophilic strategies produce which psychological outcomes in which building types. The biophilic design framework, as formalized by Kellert and Calabrese, identifies three categories of nature-in-buildings: direct experience of nature (living plants, water features, natural light, natural ventilation, animal life), indirect experience of nature (natural materials, images of nature, biomorphic forms, natural colors, nature sounds), and spatial conditions referencing nature (prospect, refuge, mystery, complexity, order, transitional spaces). Each category activates different restoration pathways. Direct nature experience produces the strongest restorative effects. Living plants reduce perceived stress, improve mood, and increase self-report

papers.title: Healthcare facility design and moral distress: How the built environment imposes suffering on caregivers
papers.excerpt: Healthcare workers are burning out — and the buildings they work in are making it worse. This review examined how the confluence of environmental, organizational, and spatial factors in healthcare facilities imposes moral suffering, fear, outrage, disgust, and depletion among healthcare workers. The built environment is not merely a backdrop to clinical care — it is an active agent that either supports or undermines the psychological resilience of the people who provide that care. Moral distress occurs when a clinician knows the ethically correct action but is constrained from performing it. The built environment creates constraints that produce moral distress in ways that organizational policies alone cannot. A nurse who knows that a dying patient needs privacy but must care for them in a shared ward with only a curtain partition experiences moral distress that the building created. A physician who knows that natural light accelerates recovery but practices in a windowless ICU cannot provide that therapeutic condition. A rehabilitation therapist who knows that garden access motivates patient exercise but works in a facility with no accessible outdoor space faces a building-imposed

papers.title: PDF-0785
papers.excerpt: Show someone a photo of an overgrown weedy field and they grimace. Show them a small forest with a visible path curving out of sight and their rating jumps. Rachel Kaplan, Stephen Kaplan, and Terry Brown at the University of Michigan showed in this 1989 study in Environment and Behavior that four domains of landscape variables predict how much people prefer a scene, and that how the scene looks to the viewer matters more than what the land actually contains. Why do people prefer some landscapes over others? Researchers had tried several approaches. Some measured the physical landscape itself: slope, soil type, how much canopy covered the ground. Some classified the land cover: forest, farm, water, field. Some scored how easy the scene was to read using the Kaplans' own framework: coherence (does it make sense?), complexity (how much is going on?), legibility (could you find your way?), and mystery (does it promise more if you move forward?). Others scored how the viewer perceived the scene: how open, how smooth, how spacious it felt. But no study had tested all four approaches head to head to find out which one best predicted what people preferred. The team photographed fifty-nine

papers.title: Forest spatial structure matters for restoration — canopy closure and species mix predict psychological recovery
papers.excerpt: Not all forests restore equally. A walk through a dense conifer plantation produces a different psychological experience from a walk through an open mixed-species forest with sunlit clearings. This study in *Frontiers in Forests and Global Change* investigated how the spatial structure of forests — canopy closure, species composition, understory density, and stand arrangement — predicts the restorative quality of forest environments, providing evidence that forest design, not merely forest presence, determines the psychological benefit. The research used a combination of ecological measurements and psychological assessments. Forest plots were characterized by their spatial attributes: canopy openness (measured by hemispherical photography), species diversity (richness and evenness indices), stem density, understory vegetation density, and the proportion of broadleaf versus conifer species. Participants walked through or viewed these characterized forest environments and completed validated restoration measures — Perceived Restorativeness Scale, mood inventories, and stress indicators. The key findings identified specific structural predictors of psychological restoration. Moderate

papers.title: PDF-0789
papers.excerpt: The 2011 Great Eastern Japan Earthquake and subsequent tsunami killed nearly 20,000 people and triggered the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster — a compound catastrophe that exposed fundamental failures in building design, urban planning, and disaster preparedness. Mohamad Zayat reviews the lessons learned in this 2022 conference paper for the *5th International Conference of Contemporary Affairs in Architecture and Urbanism (ICCAUA)*. The paper takes a comparative case study approach, examining the Fukushima disaster alongside other nuclear incidents (Daini Nuclear Power Plant, which survived the same tsunami without catastrophe) to identify what went wrong structurally and systematically. The core argument is that preparedness-phase investment — building design, infrastructure hardening, and evacuation planning — is "7 times better, safer and cheaper" than recovery-phase response. For the built environment, the key lessons are architectural and urban-planning ones: (1) coastal building codes must account for tsunami wave heights that exceed historical precedent, not merely match the tallest wave previously recorded; (2) critical infrastructure (nuclear plants, hospitals, emergenc

papers.title: Biomimetics in architecture: Research trends in learning from biological design strategies
papers.excerpt: Nature has been engineering for 3.8 billion years — architecture for about 10,000. The application of biomimetics to architecture represents an attempt to close this experience gap by learning from biological organisms that have already solved design problems that architects face: how to regulate temperature without external energy, how to create structures that are simultaneously strong and light, how to manage water, air, and light through form alone. Biomimetics — also called biomimicry, bio-inspired design, or nature-inspired architecture — transfers functional principles from biological systems to human design. The transfer operates at multiple levels. At the lowest level, biological form is imitated aesthetically: a building is shaped like a pinecone, a shell, or a bone, without necessarily replicating the functional principle that gives the biological form its shape. At a middle level, biological mechanisms are extracted and applied: the self-cleaning properties of lotus leaves inspire self-cleaning facade coatings, the thermoregulatory ventilation of termite mounds inspires passive cooling systems, the structural efficiency of trabecular bone inspires lightweight space fram

Heart rate variability was not affected by green designs. However, improvement in impressions was found for tropical and Japanese designs. Japanese design was more effective in decreasing negative emotions than tropical design. EEG during 5-min exposure showed limited frequency bands and regions of interest affected. Indoor greening with tropical design promoted positive mood states while Japanese design inhibited negative mood states. No significant differences in cognitive task performance between designs. papers.title: Natural light benefits building occupants through circadian, psychological, and visual pathways
papers.excerpt: Daylight is not just illumination — it is a biological signal, a psychological resource, and a driver of visual performance, each operating through different mechanisms with different spectral and intensity requirements. Lisa Edwards and Paul Torcellini at the National Renewable Energy Laboratory reviewed these pathways in a 2002 technical report that remains influential because it was among the first to organize the daylighting evidence by mechanism rather than by outcome, revealing that a single design decision — the size, orientation, and glazing of a window — simultaneously affects three independent systems in the building occupant, and failure on any one of them undermines the others. The circadian pathway operates through the retinal ganglion cells sensitive to short-wavelength blue light. Morning daylight exposure entrains the circadian system, promoting daytime alertness, stable sleep-wake cycles, and appropriate cortisol rhythms. The intensity required for circadian entrainment — above 1000 lux at the eye with adequate short-wavelength content — is well above typical artificial lighting levels (300–500 lux at the task plane, translating to perhaps 100–200 lux at the eye) a

papers.title: Neural correlates of architectural perception: ACC emotional evaluation and PPA spatial processing operate independently
papers.excerpt: When you look at a building, two things happen in your brain simultaneously and independently. This study used neuroimaging to investigate the visual perception of architectural environments, finding that the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) activates for emotional evaluation while the parahippocampal place area (PPA) activates for spatial processing — and that these two processes operate in parallel without depending on each other. The functional independence is the study's central contribution. The ACC — a medial prefrontal region associated with reward processing, emotional evaluation, and aesthetic judgment — responds to the emotional quality of the architecture: is this building beautiful or ugly, inviting or forbidding, pleasant or aversive? The PPA — a medial temporal region specialized for processing the spatial layout of scenes — responds to the spatial structure of the architecture: the room's proportions, the layout of walls and openings, the navigational significance of the spatial configuration. These two evaluations happen simultaneously but are computed by different neural systems. The independence means that a building can be spatially well-organized but emotionally

papers.title: Natural lighting improvements in residential spaces: Effects on perceived happiness and sadness
papers.excerpt: Can more daylight make you happier? This experimental study tested whether natural lighting improvements in residential spaces affect perceived happiness and sadness, finding that increasing daylight access produced measurable changes in self-reported emotional state. The result connects a designable architectural variable — window size, orientation, and obstruction — to an emotional outcome that matters for public health. The experimental manipulation improved natural lighting in residential spaces through specific architectural interventions: increasing window area, reducing external obstructions that blocked daylight, improving glazing transmittance, or redesigning room layouts to position living areas closer to windows. The improvements were real, not virtual — participants experienced actual changes in the daylight availability in their own homes, providing ecological validity that laboratory studies with simulated windows cannot match. The perceived happiness finding is the positive result. Residents in spaces with improved natural lighting reported higher happiness scores than their pre-intervention baselines and than matched control residents whose lighting conditions were

papers.title: PDF-0803
papers.excerpt: Hand someone an unfamiliar object behind a curtain. Can they identify it by touch alone? Tobias Kalisch, Jan-Christoph Kattenstroth, Rebecca Kowalewski, Martin Tegenthoff, and Hubert Dinse at Ruhr-University Bochum showed in this 2012 study in PLoS ONE that haptic object recognition declines significantly with age — and that cognitive ability, not just finger sensitivity, drives the decline. Humans rely heavily on vision to navigate the world. But touch remains critical for manipulating objects, detecting textures, and judging materials. In architecture, people touch handrails, door handles, wall surfaces, and furniture constantly. If haptic perception degrades with age, then the tactile qualities of built environments become even more important for older occupants. The team tested 81 older adults (age 60-85) on three tasks: cross-modal haptic object recognition (touch an object, then pick it from a visual lineup), tactile acuity (two-point discrimination on the fingertip), and general cognitive performance (Raven's Progressive Matrices). All three measures declined with age. But the key finding was that haptic performance correlated more strongly with cognitive ability than with t

papers.title: Personal control over the indoor environment improves comfort and reduces complaints
papers.excerpt: Give people control over their thermal and lighting environment and they report higher comfort — even when the physical conditions remain unchanged. This 2000 study in the *Journal of Environmental Psychology* demonstrated that perceived personal control over indoor environmental conditions moderates the relationship between environmental quality and occupant satisfaction, with higher perceived control producing higher satisfaction at any given environmental quality level. The study assessed occupants in office buildings that varied in the degree of personal environmental control available — from buildings with no individual control (centralized HVAC, fixed lighting) to buildings with individual thermostats, operable windows, task lights, and adjustable blinds. Occupant satisfaction was measured alongside objective environmental conditions (temperature, illuminance, noise levels). The primary finding: perceived control predicted satisfaction independently of the objective environmental conditions. Occupants with individual thermostats were more comfortable than occupants without them, even when both groups experienced the same average temperature. The thermostat provided the percep

Hills look steeper when we are tired: apparent slant increased with fatigue. papers.title: Multisensory architecture for the visually impaired: Texture, sound, heat, and aroma as spatial cues
papers.excerpt: For a sighted person, a building is primarily a visual experience. For a visually impaired person, a building is a texture, a sound, a thermal gradient, and a scent — and the quality of that building depends entirely on how well its architect designed for those non-visual channels. This review examined how architecture designed with multisensory elements — texture, sound, heat, and aroma — rather than visual form alone produces enhanced spatial perception, understanding, and functional ability for visually impaired users. The visually impaired population navigates buildings through a sensory hierarchy that inverts the sighted user's experience. Tactile quality — the texture of floor surfaces, wall finishes, handrails, and door hardware — becomes the primary source of spatial information. A change in floor material from carpet to tile signals a threshold between zones. A textured handrail ridge indicates a stairway landing. A smooth wall surface that becomes rough at a corner indicates a change in direction. These tactile cues, which sighted users barely notice, constitute the spatial language through which visually impaired users read the building. Acoustic space provides the secon

papers.title: Sensory sensitivity in architectural space: How visual, auditory, olfactory, tactile, and kinesthetic domains differ
papers.excerpt: Not all senses matter equally in architecture — but which ones matter most, and for what? This study measured sensitivity levels to sensory stimuli in architectural space across five sensory domains: visual, auditory, olfactory, tactile, and kinesthetic. The finding reveals a hierarchy of sensory sensitivity that should inform how architects allocate design attention across sensory modalities. The sensitivity hierarchy is not uniform across people or contexts. Different individuals show different sensory sensitivity profiles — some are visually dominant, others auditorily sensitive, others tactilely attuned. But the population-level pattern shows consistent trends. Visual sensitivity is highest in terms of discrimination acuity — people can detect smaller differences in visual properties (color, brightness, form) than in other modalities. Auditory sensitivity is highest in terms of interference — sound is the most intrusive modality, the one most difficult to ignore, the one that produces the strongest disruption when the stimulus is unwanted. Olfactory sensitivity is highest in terms of emotional association — smell triggers the strongest emotional memories and the most immediate

papers.title: Curvilinear versus rectilinear architecture: Approach-avoidance behavioral motivation in spatial geometry
papers.excerpt: Curved spaces make you want to enter. Angular spaces make you hesitate. This review examined the relationship between curvilinear versus rectilinear architectural forms and approach-avoidance behavioral motivation — the fundamental directional response that determines whether an organism moves toward or away from a stimulus. The finding that curved architectural geometry produces stronger approach motivation than angular geometry connects abstract spatial form to the deepest layer of behavioral regulation. Approach-avoidance is the most basic behavioral decision an organism makes. Before it evaluates aesthetics, before it assesses function, before it reads signage, the nervous system computes a rapid approach-or-avoid signal based on the visual properties of the environment. This computation involves the amygdala (threat detection), the nucleus accumbens (reward prediction), and the motor cortex (movement preparation). The approach signal prepares the body to move forward, explore, and engage. The avoidance signal prepares the body to retreat, withdraw, or freeze. The association between curvature and approach has been documented across multiple experimental paradigms. Participants

papers.title: Green building design strategies: Indoor environmental quality and occupant health outcomes
papers.excerpt: Green buildings are supposed to be healthy buildings — but are they? This review examined the evidence linking green building design strategies to indoor environmental quality (IEQ) and occupant health outcomes, finding that the relationship is positive but more complex than the marketing materials suggest. Green design strategies — enhanced ventilation, daylight optimization, low-VOC materials, thermal comfort targeting — do produce improved IEQ, but the health benefits depend on which strategies are implemented, how well they are commissioned, and whether the building's operational performance matches its design intent. The IEQ framework encompasses four environmental domains: thermal comfort (operative temperature, humidity, radiant asymmetry), indoor air quality (ventilation rate, CO₂ concentration, particulate matter, volatile organic compounds), visual quality (daylight availability, illuminance, glare, view), and acoustic quality (background noise, reverberation, speech privacy). Green building certification systems (LEED, BREEAM, WELL, Green Star) award credits for design strategies that improve performance in one or more of these domains. But certification credits are awar

papers.title: Green emotion — emotional perception mediates the restoration benefit of green space exposure
papers.excerpt: Green spaces do not just reduce stress — they generate positive emotions that mediate the restorative benefit. Yu, Zhong, Zhu, and Sun investigated the role of emotional perception in green space restoration, testing whether positive affect triggered by green environments is a mediating mechanism (not just a byproduct) of the restoration process. The study measured emotional responses to green environments using both self-report affect scales and implicit measures of emotional processing. Participants were exposed to environments varying in greenness — from urban scenes with no vegetation to parks with dense canopy — and their emotional responses, attention restoration, and stress recovery were measured. The mediation analysis showed that positive emotional perception partially mediated the restoration effect of green space exposure on cognitive attention recovery. In other words, green spaces produced positive emotions, and those positive emotions contributed to the cognitive recovery — the restoration was achieved partly through the emotional pathway, not solely through the attentional pathway (fascination and involuntary attention) proposed by Kaplan's ART. The finding adds an e

papers.title: Building Wellbeing: The SACRA model for quantifying psychological outcomes of architectural design
papers.excerpt: Can you measure what a building does to your mind? This review proposes that you can — and that you must. The Building Wellbeing scale, based on the SACRA model, provides a multi-item measurement instrument that produces quantified and transferable psychological wellbeing metrics for architectural evaluation. The ambition is to make psychological wellbeing a specifiable, measurable, and verifiable building performance criterion — as concrete and actionable as energy use, structural load, or acoustic isolation. The SACRA model identifies five dimensions of building-related psychological wellbeing. Safety: the perception that the building protects occupants from physical harm, environmental hazard, and social threat — a precondition for all other wellbeing outcomes because psychological engagement with a space requires a baseline sense of physical security. Aesthetics: the perceptual and emotional response to the building's formal, material, and spatial qualities — the dimension that conventional architectural culture already attends to, but that evidence-based design has struggled to operationalize. Comfort: the physiological satisfaction of thermal, acoustic, luminous, and air qual

papers.excerpt: # PDF-0820: Neuroscience Forefront Review on neural mechanisms of material perception, termed shitsukan in Japan ## Paper Type narrative_review ## Core Argument Neuroscience Forefront Review on neural mechanisms of material perception, termed shitsukan in Japanese (quality-sense). Material perception entails perceiving and recognizing surface quality or internal state of an object based on sensory stimuli. Cortical areas in the ventral visual pathway are strongly involved. Vision is the main focus but every sensory modality contributes. papers.title: Haptic feedback in VR enhances material and temperature perception — making virtual surfaces feel real
papers.excerpt: Virtual reality can show you a marble countertop, but can it make you feel its coolness and smoothness? Steeven Villa and colleagues examined whether ultrasound mid-air haptic feedback enhances material and temperature perception in virtual environments, bridging the gap between visual simulation and multisensory architectural experience. The study tested whether adding haptic cues — delivered by an ultrasound-based mid-air haptic device that creates tactile patterns on the user's skin without physical contact — improved participants' ability to identify materials and perceive temperature in a VR environment. Participants interacted with virtual surfaces (wood, stone, metal, fabric) rendered with varying visual textures and temperatures. In some conditions, haptic feedback patterns were designed to correspond to the expected tactile properties of each material (smooth and cool for metal, warm and rough for wood). In control conditions, no haptic feedback was provided, or incongruent haptic feedback was delivered. The results showed that congruent haptic feedback significantly enhanced participants' material identification accuracy and temperature perception compared to vision-only

papers.title: Material selection in architecture shapes aesthetic expectations — and the two interact in ways designers rarely test
papers.excerpt: The material a building is made from — or appears to be made from — shapes the aesthetic expectations visitors bring to it before they experience anything else. This 2010 study in *METU JFA* investigates the relationship between material selection and aesthetic expectation in architecture, arguing that material perception is not a passive registration of surface properties but an active, expectation-laden interpretation shaped by prior experience, cultural associations, and sensory memory. Every material carries associations. Wood signals warmth, craft, and natural origin. Glass signals modernity, transparency, and precision. Concrete signals mass, permanence, and industrial process. Stone signals geological time and cultural tradition. These associations are not universal — they vary with culture, experience, and context — but they are real psychological phenomena that operate before and during the aesthetic evaluation of a building. The review synthesizes evidence from material science, perceptual psychology, and architectural theory to identify the variables that mediate the material-aesthetics relationship. Surface texture is primary: the human visual system is tuned to detect

papers.title: Haptic design from blind users: Experience-based parameters for inclusive architectural practice
papers.excerpt: The people who know most about how buildings feel are the people who cannot see them. This qualitative study drew on experience-based haptic design parameters from blind users to produce more inclusive architectural design — reversing the usual direction of expertise by positioning visually impaired individuals not as users requiring accommodation but as expert informants whose embodied knowledge of tactile spatial experience exceeds that of any sighted designer. The methodological innovation is important. Rather than asking sighted architects to imagine what blind users need, the study asked blind users to describe the tactile qualities of their built environments — what they notice, what helps them, what confuses them, what feels good, and what feels dangerous. The resulting haptic design parameters emerge from lived experience rather than theoretical speculation, grounding the design recommendations in the phenomenology of tactile spatial navigation. The first set of parameters concerns surface differentiation. Blind users identify spatial zones, thresholds, and decision points primarily through changes in floor surface texture and material. A transition from carpet to tile indi

papers.title: Building material perception as a design tool for upgrading architecture
papers.excerpt: Run your hand along a ceramic tile that has been printed with a wood-grain texture. Your eyes register warmth — the golden tones, the organic grain — but your fingertips report something cold, hard, and unyielding. The mismatch jars. That perceptual conflict sits at the center of this paper, which argues that the gap between how a building material looks and how it feels, sounds, and smells is not a flaw to be hidden but a design variable to be deliberately controlled. The paper builds a theoretical framework for using material perception as a compositional tool in architecture. Its central claim is that architects already manipulate material perception — every painted concrete surface that mimics stone, every laminate floor that imitates hardwood, every glass balustrade over a thirty-story drop — but they do so without a systematic understanding of the perceptual mechanisms involved. The framework aims to make that implicit practice explicit and evidence-informed. This is a theoretical contribution that synthesizes perception research, material science, and architectural case studies rather than reporting new empirical data. The authors organize their argument around two themes. T

Visual system uses covariation of local surface orientation and intensity as a cue to opacity vs translucency. Three key findings: (i) identical luminance gradients can appear either translucent or opaque depending on perceived 3D surface orientation; (ii) illusory percepts of translucency can be induced by embedding opaque surfaces in diffuse light fields eliminating orientation-intensity covariation; (iii) illusory opacity can be generated when transparent materials are embedded in light fields where surface orientation and intensity covary. Most significant information used to compute perceived opacity and translucency of surfaces arises at a level of representation where 3D shape is made explicit ## Methods & Sample - **N** = 14 - **Design**: within_subjects - **Stimuli/Setting**: Perception and misperception of surface opacity Phillip J.
