papers.title: Material perception, affordances, and sustainability: A mini review
papers.excerpt: Pick up a wooden cup and a plastic cup shaped the same way. Before you sip from either, you already know which feels more natural, which you prefer, and which you would call sustainable. That instant judgment travels through a chain of perceptual processing that Francesca Strappini, Sabrina Fagioli, Stefano Mastandrea, and Claudia Scorolli mapped in this 2024 mini review in Frontiers in Psychology: the brain extracts material properties through touch and vision, those properties generate affordances for grasping and using objects, affordances shape aesthetic responses, and aesthetic responses drive willingness to adopt sustainable alternatives. The review bridges three fields that rarely communicate: psychophysics of material perception, Gibsonian affordance theory, and environmental psychology of sustainability. Its central claim is that these three literatures describe successive links in a single causal chain, and that understanding that chain is essential for designing products and environments that people will actually treat sustainably. A recycled material that scores well on lifecycle assessment but feels cheap or looks artificial may trigger negative affordances and aesthet
papers.authors: Strappini, F., Fagioli, S., Mastandrea, S. & Scorolli, C.

papers.title: Memoryscapes: Audio walks, sense of place, and the integration of art and geography
papers.excerpt: Put on headphones, step outside, and press play. A voice in your ears begins narrating the history of the street you are standing on — who lived here in 1940, what the corner shop sold, how the bomb crater across the road was filled in and planted over. As you walk, the narrative adjusts to your location: pass the old church and you hear recordings of parishioners remembering the bells. Turn into the side alley and the artist's voice drops to a whisper, describing what lovers and smugglers once did in this exact passage. The buildings have not changed, but the place has transformed. This is the audio walk, and Toby Butler's 2007 review of the memoryscape concept examines how these site-specific listening experiences deepen sense of place by integrating art, oral history, and cultural geography into the act of walking through space. Audio walks layer site-specific sound, narrative, and music over physical environments, creating multisensory experiences that transform perception of ordinary places. Unlike guided tours that point out architectural features from the outside, audio walks create an interior experience of exterior space — the listener's attention is directed inward by the

papers.title: Indoor acoustics for autism-friendly spaces require lower reverberation and reduced background noise
papers.excerpt: For people with autism spectrum conditions, the acoustic environment is not background — it is foreground. Federica Bettarelli and colleagues review the indoor acoustic requirements for autism-friendly spaces in this 2021 study in *Applied Sciences*, synthesizing evidence on how sensory processing differences in autism interact with architectural acoustics. Autistic individuals frequently report heightened sensitivity to sound. Sounds that neurotypical individuals process as background (HVAC hum, conversation murmur, fluorescent lighting buzz) can be experienced as intrusive, distracting, and even painful. This sensory difference means that acoustic design standards developed for neurotypical populations are insufficient for spaces serving autistic users. The review identifies specific acoustic parameters that must be addressed. Reverberation time should be lower than standard recommendations — shorter than 0.5 seconds in occupied conditions, compared to the 0.6–0.8 seconds typical of educational and institutional spaces. Excess reverberation makes speech less intelligible and increases the overall noise level, both of which are particularly problematic for individuals with auditor

papers.title: Urban street tree canopy reduces heat stress and increases pedestrian thermal comfort
papers.excerpt: A mature street tree can reduce the perceived temperature on a summer sidewalk by several degrees — a thermal intervention that no mechanical cooling system can replicate outdoors. This 2021 study in *Landscape and Urban Planning* quantified the microclimate benefits of urban street tree canopy, measuring both physical thermal variables and pedestrian thermal comfort under varying canopy conditions. The research combined microclimate measurement (air temperature, mean radiant temperature, humidity, wind speed) with thermal comfort assessment (Universal Thermal Climate Index — UTCI) and pedestrian perception surveys in urban streets with systematically varying tree canopy cover. Sites ranged from treeless streets with full solar exposure to streets with dense canopy providing near-complete shade. The physical measurements confirmed large microclimate effects. Under dense tree canopy, mean radiant temperature (MRT) — the dominant variable for outdoor thermal comfort — was reduced by 10–20°C compared to fully exposed locations. Air temperature reductions were more modest (1–3°C) but consistent. The UTCI reduction under canopy translated from "strong heat stress" to "moderate heat stre

papers.title: Classroom acoustic design predicts speech intelligibility — and children are more vulnerable than adults
papers.excerpt: Children cannot hear as well as adults in noisy rooms — not because their ears are worse, but because their brains are less skilled at extracting speech from noise. This 2024 study in *Building Acoustics* examined the relationship between classroom acoustic design and speech intelligibility, demonstrating that children require better acoustic conditions than adults to achieve the same level of speech understanding. Speech intelligibility depends on three acoustic parameters that the classroom architect can control: reverberation time, background noise level, and the speech-to-noise ratio at the listener's ear. In adult listeners, speech remains intelligible at signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) as low as +3 to +5 dB. Children require SNRs of +10 to +15 dB — meaning their listening environment must be significantly quieter relative to the speech signal for them to understand the teacher's words. The study measured acoustic conditions in classrooms and correlated them with speech intelligibility test scores for child listeners. Classrooms with reverberation times below 0.6 seconds and background noise levels below 35 dB(A) provided adequate intelligibility for most children. Classrooms th

papers.title: PDF-0844
papers.excerpt: Most people believe that a row of trees along a highway blocks traffic noise. They are partly right and mostly wrong. Fan Yang, Zhi Yi Bao, and Zhu Jun Zhu at Zhejiang University showed in this 2011 study in the International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health that landscape plants reduce psychological noise even when their physical sound dampening is negligible, and that this perceptual shift shows up in brain activity. Typical urban vegetation strips cut only 1 to 3 decibels of sound per 30 meters of depth. Humans perceive a halving of loudness at about 10 to 15 dB of reduction. So the physical effect of most planted buffers is far too small to notice by ear. Yet ninety percent of survey respondents in this study believed that plants reduce noise, and fifty-five percent overestimated the plants' actual dampening power. The gap between perceived and actual noise reduction suggests that the primary mechanism is psychological: seeing vegetation changes how the brain processes the noise. The team combined two approaches. First, a questionnaire survey captured public beliefs about landscape noise dampening. Second, an EEG experiment recorded brain activity in forty pa

papers.title: Noise pollution damages cardiovascular and cognitive health — not just hearing
papers.excerpt: Noise is the pollutant you cannot see, cannot smell, and cannot close a door against — sound travels through walls, around corners, and through the gaps in every window seal. Walihabib Hemmat, Hesam, and Atifnigar, in a 2023 comprehensive review, trace the pathways through which chronic noise exposure damages human health far beyond the obvious target of the auditory system. The cardiovascular pathway is the most thoroughly documented. Chronic exposure to environmental noise — especially road traffic noise above 55 dB(A) during the day and 45 dB(A) at night — triggers sustained activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and the sympathetic nervous system. The stress hormones released (cortisol, adrenaline, noradrenaline) elevate blood pressure, increase heart rate, and promote systemic inflammation. Large epidemiological studies have linked long-term traffic noise exposure to increased incidence of hypertension, ischemic heart disease, stroke, and myocardial infarction. The World Health Organization estimates that environmental noise in Western Europe alone causes 12,000 premature deaths annually and 48,000 new cases of ischemic heart disease per year. The cognitiv

papers.title: Bright light effects on psychophysiology depend on time of day — morning light boosts, evening light disrupts
papers.excerpt: The same bright light can be beneficial or harmful — depending on when you receive it. This study compared the psychophysiological effects of bright light exposure delivered at different times of day, finding that morning bright light improved alertness, mood, and cognitive performance, while evening bright light suppressed melatonin, delayed sleep onset, and impaired next-morning function — even though the light stimulus was physically identical. The experiment used a within-subjects design where participants experienced the same bright light exposure (above 2500 lux) during either the morning, afternoon, or evening on separate experimental days. Salivary melatonin, subjective alertness, mood ratings, and cognitive performance (reaction time, working memory) were measured at multiple timepoints. Morning bright light produced the most positive outcomes: earlier melatonin offset (confirming circadian phase advance), increased daytime alertness, improved mood, and faster reaction times. These benefits are consistent with the circadian biology of light: morning exposure reinforces the circadian wake signal, promoting consolidated wakefulness throughout the day. Afternoon bright light

papers.title: Energy behavior change requires social and structural interventions — information alone is insufficient
papers.excerpt: Telling people to save energy does not make them save energy. This 2016 study in *Energy Research and Social Science* reviewed the evidence for behavior-change interventions aimed at reducing household and building energy consumption, finding that information-based approaches (knowledge campaigns, energy audits, feedback displays) produce modest, short-lived effects, while structural interventions (default settings, automation, social norms, financial incentives) produce larger and more durable behavior change. The hierarchy of effectiveness emerged clearly. Structural interventions — changing the default thermostat setting, installing automated lighting controls, redesigning the choice architecture so that the energy-efficient option is the easiest option — produced the largest and most consistent energy savings. These interventions work because they do not require conscious effort, motivation, or attention from the occupant. The building works efficiently because its systems are designed to work efficiently, not because the occupant remembers to turn things off. Social norm interventions — telling occupants how their energy use compares to their neighbors — produced moderate effe

papers.title: Prospect-refuge theory in spatial design: The biological basis for environmental preference
papers.excerpt: The most profound insight in environmental preference research is also the simplest: humans prefer places where they can see without being seen. Jay Appleton's prospect-refuge theory, first articulated in 1975, proposes that landscape and architectural preferences are rooted in the survival advantages that environmental configurations provided to our ancestors — and this review traces how the theory has been applied, refined, and empirically tested across four decades of research in architecture, landscape design, and environmental psychology. The core mechanism is evolutionary. Perceived openness and the ability to see in an environment without being seen satisfies a biological human need to observe (prospect) without exposure to danger (refuge). An ancestor who selected a resting site with a wide view of approaching threats (prospect) and a protected back (refuge) — a cave mouth overlooking a savanna, a hilltop with dense cover behind — survived predation and hostile encounters more reliably than one who rested in the open or in total enclosure. The preference was selected, became genetically encoded, and persists in modern humans as an automatic, pre-cognitive spatial evaluation

papers.title: Trees, grass, or concrete? Effects of different VR courtyard environments on stress recovery
papers.excerpt: Trees, grass, or concrete? When a stressed office worker steps into a courtyard during a break, does it matter what the courtyard contains? Qi Huang and colleagues tested this question directly in a 2020 study in Landscape and Urban Planning, using virtual reality to isolate the effect of vegetation type on physiological stress recovery. The answer is clear: both grass and trees reduce stress more effectively than concrete, but the pattern of recovery differs between physiological and affective measures — and grass may be the better investment. The study used a between-groups experimental design with 89 participants randomly assigned to one of three VR courtyard environments: concrete only (no vegetation), grass (a courtyard with maintained lawn but no trees), and trees (a courtyard with trees and ground cover). Baseline physiological data analysis confirmed no significant differences in age, gender, baseline systolic blood pressure, or baseline diastolic blood pressure across the three groups, establishing a clean comparison. Stress was induced using a five-minute mathematics performance assessment (MPA) test with added social evaluation pressure — a validated stress protocol that

papers.title: PDF-0871
papers.excerpt: Smell cedar, see grain, feel warmth. Wood engages the nose, the eyes, and the fingertips — and each channel triggers measurable changes in the body. Harumi Ikei, Chorong Song, and Yoshifumi Miyazaki at Chiba University reviewed the evidence in this 2017 article in the Journal of Wood Science and found that wood-derived stimuli consistently shift the autonomic nervous system toward relaxation. Humans evolved in forests. The biophilia hypothesis predicts that natural materials, including wood, should calm the body. But the scattered evidence had never been pulled together across sensory channels. This review asked: what happens to brain activity, heart rate, blood pressure, and cortisol when people smell, see, or touch wood? The review covered three channels. First, olfactory: inhaling volatile organic compounds from hinoki cypress, cedar, and other wood species reduced prefrontal cortex activity (measured by near-infrared spectroscopy), lowered sympathetic nervous system tone (measured by heart rate variability), and reduced blood pressure. The calming effect appeared within minutes. Second, visual: viewing wood panels versus white panels or steel surfaces produced similar autonomic

papers.title: Biophilic design through colour, light, and materiality: A literature review and practitioner survey
papers.excerpt: Biophilic design has a specificity problem. The concept — integrating nature into the built environment to improve occupant well-being — is well-supported by evidence and widely endorsed in principle. But when an architect sits down to specify the color of a wall, the type of light fixture, or the material for a countertop, the biophilic design literature offers surprisingly little guidance. This 2022 paper in Interiority addressed the gap by conducting both a systematic literature review and a practitioner survey, examining how three specific interior design variables — colour, light, and materiality — function as vehicles for biophilic experience. The literature review examined 19 articles that directly address biophilic design benefits mediated through colour, light, or materiality. Categorization of these articles by biophilic feature revealed an uneven evidence base: four articles addressed colour, eight addressed lighting, and nine addressed materiality. The lighting literature is the most developed, with controlled studies linking spectral composition, daylight access, and dynamic light variation to circadian entrainment, mood, and cognitive performance. papers.title: The potential of biophilic fractal designs to promote health, performance, and well-being: A review of experiments and applications
papers.excerpt: Your eyes are fractal machines. When you gaze at a landscape — a coastline, a tree canopy, a cloud formation — your eye movements trace a search pattern whose statistical structure is itself fractal, with a fractal dimension of approximately D = 1.4. This is not a metaphor. Eye-tracking studies show that the trajectory of human gaze across a visual scene, measured as a two-dimensional path over time, produces a fractal pattern at the same dimension regardless of what the viewer is looking at, regardless of the task, and regardless of whether the viewer has a neurological condition. Richard Taylor reviewed this evidence in a 2021 paper in Sustainability and proposed a "fractal fluency" model: the human visual system evolved to process mid-range fractal patterns with maximum efficiency, and this tuning explains why fractal environments reduce stress, enhance perception, and promote aesthetic pleasure. The fractal fluency model rests on three empirical pillars. The first is perceptual: mid-D fractals (D = 1.3 to 1.5) enhance visual detection sensitivity. In discrimination tasks, participants viewing mid-D fractal images showed superior accuracy in distinguishing between fractal patter

papers.title: Architectural form, allostatic overload, and the neuroimmunological case for biophilic and biomorphic design
papers.excerpt: Your building may be making you sick — not through mold, radon, or volatile organic compounds, but through the shape of its walls. Jessica Valentine explored this possibility in a 2023 paper in the International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, connecting two bodies of evidence that have not previously been linked: the neuroarchitecture literature showing that architectural features trigger acute stress responses, and the allostatic load literature showing that chronic or repeated stress produces cumulative physiological damage across the cardiovascular, metabolic, immune, and neuroendocrine systems. The bridge between them is allostatic overload — the point at which the body's regulatory systems, overwhelmed by repeated activation, begin to break down. The neuroarchitecture evidence is now substantial. Exposure to stress-inducing architectural features — sharp angles, low ceilings, narrow corridors, visual complexity that exceeds processing capacity, spatial configurations that restrict prospect or eliminate refuge — produces acute stress responses in humans without conscious perception. The stress response operates below awareness: occupants do not think "this

papers.title: Waterscapes promote health — a systematic review of water features and human wellbeing
papers.excerpt: Water features — fountains, streams, ponds, coastlines — consistently predict positive health and wellbeing outcomes, often more strongly than green space alone. This systematic review in the *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health* compiled evidence for the health-promoting effects of waterscapes, finding that proximity to and visual or auditory contact with water features is associated with reduced stress, improved mood, and enhanced restoration. The review categorized water exposure by type: large natural water bodies (ocean, lakes, rivers), small natural water features (streams, ponds), and designed water features (fountains, reflecting pools, water walls). All three categories showed positive associations with psychological wellbeing, but the mechanisms and magnitudes differed. Natural water bodies showed the strongest associations — coastal proximity predicted lower stress, better mental health, and greater physical activity. The evidence base for blue space health benefits has grown rapidly, paralleling and sometimes exceeding the green space literature. The mechanisms are multiple: visual attraction (the aesthetic pleasure of water surfaces), acou

papers.title: Bright light consolidates sleep in elderly insomniacs — and improves next-day cognitive performance
papers.excerpt: Elderly people with sleep maintenance insomnia — the inability to stay asleep through the night — improved both their sleep continuity and their next-day cognitive performance after exposure to bright artificial light. Published in the *Journal of Sleep Research* in 1996, this study demonstrated that the circadian mechanism underlying sleep fragmentation in older adults can be addressed through the built environment rather than through medication. Sleep maintenance insomnia is pervasive among older adults. Unlike sleep onset insomnia (difficulty falling asleep), maintenance insomnia involves repeated awakenings during the night and early morning waking, producing fragmented sleep that reduces total sleep time and impairs daytime function. The circadian hypothesis proposes that age-related weakening of the circadian signal — particularly reduced amplitude of the melatonin rhythm — allows the sleep-wake system to fragment, because the circadian drive that consolidates sleep into a single nocturnal episode is insufficiently strong. The study tested whether bright light exposure during the daytime could strengthen the circadian signal sufficiently to consolidate nighttime sleep. Elderl

papers.title: Biophilic design in office environments improves wellbeing and productivity — a field study
papers.excerpt: Plants, daylight, natural materials, and nature views in offices are not just aesthetically pleasant — they measurably improve worker wellbeing and productivity. This field study measured the effects of biophilic design elements in real office environments, comparing workspace conditions and worker outcomes in offices with high and low biophilic design quality. The study assessed biophilic design using a systematic evaluation tool that rated the presence and quality of natural elements: indoor plants, natural materials (wood, stone), daylight access, nature views, water features, natural colors, and organic forms. Worker outcomes were measured using validated wellbeing instruments (perceived stress, mood, job satisfaction) and productivity indicators (self-reported productivity, absenteeism). Workers in offices with higher biophilic design scores reported significantly lower stress, higher job satisfaction, and higher self-rated productivity than workers in offices with lower biophilic scores. The effects were consistent across demographic subgroups (gender, age, job type) and persisted after controlling for office size, location, and organizational factors. The specific biophilic

papers.title: PDF-0889
papers.excerpt: Cover the walls of a room with wood and people feel warm and relaxed. Strip the wood away and they feel cold and clinical. But how much wood is enough? Jing Li, Jianmei Wu, Frank Lam, Chao Zhang, Jian Kang, and Hongpeng Xu showed in this 2021 study in Wood Science and Technology that the degree of wood coverage in an interior space follows an inverted-U: moderate wood coverage draws the most visual attention and produces the strongest positive psychological impression. Urban stress and indoor lifestyles have pushed designers to bring natural materials back into interiors. Wood stands out because humans perceive it as warm, organic, and alive. Previous eye-tracking studies confirmed that wood surfaces capture gaze. But no study had varied wood coverage systematically to find the optimal amount. The team created digitally rendered interior scenes with varying degrees of wood use: no wood, low wood (floor only), medium wood (floor and one wall), high wood (floor and two walls), and full wood (all surfaces). They also varied the type and position of wood application. Participants viewed these scenes while an eye-tracker measured fixation count, fixation duration, and gaze distribution.

papers.title: PDF-0891
papers.excerpt: People think rooms with wood walls feel warmer than rooms with white-painted walls, even when the thermostat reads exactly the same. Denise Blankenberger, Kevin Van Den Wymelenberg, and Jason Stenson at the University of Oregon showed in this 2019 ARCC conference paper that exposed wood surfaces shift perceived thermal comfort toward the warm end of the scale by a meaningful amount — a hue-heat effect driven by the wood's visual warmth. Thermal comfort depends on six physical parameters: air temperature, radiant temperature, humidity, air speed, clothing, and metabolic rate. But a growing body of work shows that non-thermal factors — especially visual perception — also shift how warm or cool people feel. The hue-heat hypothesis (Mogensen, 1926) holds that warm-colored surfaces make people feel warmer than cool-colored surfaces at the same temperature. Wood, which humans perceive as yellow-brown and warm-toned, might trigger this cross-modal effect. The team placed 48 participants in a climate chamber set to 81.5 degrees Fahrenheit (27.5 degrees Celsius), 40% relative humidity, and a PMV of +0.5 (slightly warm). One group (n=24) saw wooden wall panels. The other group (n=24) saw whi

papers.title: PDF-0893
papers.excerpt: Your brain treats a curved room differently than a rectangular one, and it does so before you form any conscious opinion about the space. Alex Coburn, Oshin Vartanian, and Anjan Chatterjee wrote this 2017 perspective article in the Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience to argue that the next step for neuroarchitecture is not applying neuroscience findings to architecture, but making architectural experience itself the target of brain research. The paper reviews three domains of evidence. First, aesthetic response to architectural form: how buildings activate reward, emotion, and evaluation circuits. Vartanian et al. (2013, 2015) scanned people with fMRI while they viewed curved versus rectangular rooms. Curved rooms activated the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), a region linked to aesthetic pleasure and approach motivation. People who chose to enter the curved rooms also activated the anterior midcingulate cortex (aMCC), which supports decisions about whether to approach or avoid a stimulus. Beauty judgments about architecture activated the frontopolar cortex and superior frontal gyrus, regions tied to conscious reasoning, plus the parahippocampus, where memory and past experience resi

papers.title: Evening blue-enriched light delays sleep onset and impairs next-morning alertness
papers.excerpt: The color of your evening light determines when you fall asleep and how alert you feel the next morning. Sarah Chellappa, Roland Steiner, and colleagues demonstrated this in a controlled study showing that exposure to blue-enriched polychromatic white light (6500 K) in the evening — two hours before habitual bedtime — delayed melatonin onset, increased sleep latency, reduced slow-wave sleep in the first cycle, and impaired subjective alertness the following morning, compared to non-blue-enriched light (2500 K) at matched photopic illuminance. The experimental design was rigorous. Participants experienced both lighting conditions in a counterbalanced within-subjects protocol. The evening light exposure occurred during a two-hour period before bedtime, at a constant photopic illuminance of approximately 40 lux — a level comparable to dim room lighting, well below typical office illumination. Even at this low intensity, the spectral composition mattered: the 6500 K condition contained sufficient short-wavelength (blue) energy to engage the melanopsin-containing intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs), while the 2500 K condition did not. Salivary melatonin was meas

headings: PDF-0899: Heschong (1999, Technical Report for Pacific Gas & Electric) presents landmark investigation into th
papers.excerpt: # PDF-0899: Heschong (1999, Technical Report for Pacific Gas & Electric) presents landmark investigation into th ## Paper Type empirical_research (between_groups) ## Core Finding Heschong (1999, Technical Report for Pacific Gas & Electric) presents landmark investigation into the relationship between daylighting and human performance in schools. 226 citations, 14,656 reads. Large-scale study of daylit schools finding that students in classrooms with the most daylighting (skylights and views) showed 7-18% higher test scores compared to those with the least daylighting. One of the most influential studies establishing evidence for daylighting's positive impact on academic achievement and cognitive performance in built environments. papers.title: Evening light exposure from electronic devices delays sleep onset and impairs morning alertness
papers.excerpt: The screen you stare at before bedtime is pushing your sleep later. This 2014 study in the *Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine* examined the effects of evening light exposure from electronic devices on sleep parameters, finding that even modest light exposure from tablets and phones during the pre-sleep period delayed melatonin onset, increased sleep onset latency, and reduced next-morning alertness. The study measured sleep-relevant physiological and behavioral parameters in participants who used light-emitting electronic devices (tablets, phones) during the two hours before bedtime, compared to those who read printed material under dim light. Salivary melatonin was measured to track circadian timing. Sleep quality was assessed through polysomnography or actigraphy. Morning alertness and cognitive function were measured the following day. Evening device use delayed melatonin onset — the hormonal signal that prepares the body for sleep — by approximately 30–60 minutes. This circadian delay translated into increased sleep onset latency (it took longer to fall asleep), reduced total sleep time (the alarm clock does not respect delayed circadian phase), and impaired morning alertness

papers.title: Place attachment and pro-environmental behavior are linked — people protect places they love
papers.excerpt: People who feel emotionally attached to their neighborhood are more likely to behave in environmentally responsible ways within it. This 2012 study in *SAGE Open* examined the relationship between place attachment and pro-environmental behavior, finding that emotional bonds to place predict a range of environmentally constructive behaviors — from recycling and energy conservation to neighborhood stewardship and political advocacy for environmental protection. The study measured place attachment using multi-dimensional scales (place identity, place dependence, social bonding) and pro-environmental behavior using comprehensive behavior inventories (household conservation, community participation, environmental activism). The sample included residents from varied neighborhoods, allowing comparison across environmental conditions. Place identity — the sense that the neighborhood is part of one's self-concept — was the strongest predictor of pro-environmental behavior. Residents who said "this place is part of who I am" were significantly more likely to engage in behaviors that protected the local environment. The relationship held after controlling for general environmental attitudes,

papers.title: Lighting control increases satisfaction and reduces energy use — people choose less light than the standard prescribes
papers.excerpt: Give office workers a dimmer switch and they will make themselves both happier and greener. Jennifer Veitch and Guy Newsham demonstrated in a 2000 simulation experiment that when 47 office workers were given control over their overhead and task lighting, they chose illuminance levels substantially below the recommended standard — and reported higher satisfaction with their lighting than workers under fixed conditions. The experiment placed participants in a realistic open-plan office and allowed them to adjust overhead lighting and task lighting independently. After working for an extended period on office-representative tasks, participants settled into self-selected illuminance levels and rated their lighting satisfaction, visual comfort, and overall environmental quality. The headline finding: most participants chose desktop illuminance levels of 300–400 lux, roughly 20–40% below the 500 lux recommended by North American workspace lighting standards. Despite using less light, they reported higher satisfaction than comparison groups working under standard fixed conditions. The counterintuitive pattern — less light, more satisfaction — is explained by the control variable: satisfac

papers.title: Ceiling height and perceived enclosure independently affect beauty judgments — an fMRI study of architectural experience
papers.excerpt: Two architectural variables — ceiling height and perceived enclosure — independently predict how beautiful people judge a room to be, and each activates different brain regions. This fMRI study examined how ceiling height and the open-versus-enclosed character of architectural spaces affect beauty judgments and the neural responses associated with them. Participants viewed images of architectural interiors that varied factorially in ceiling height (high versus low) and enclosure (open or enclosed). They rated each space for beauty and approach-avoidance (would you want to enter or leave this space?). Brain activity was recorded throughout using functional magnetic resonance imaging. The behavioral results: high ceilings and open spaces were rated as more beautiful and more approach-worthy than low ceilings and enclosed spaces. Critically, the two variables contributed independently — a high-ceiling, open room received the highest beauty ratings, but a high-ceiling enclosed room was rated more beautiful than a low-ceiling open one, meaning that ceiling height had a larger effect than enclosure on beauty. The neural data revealed dissociable pathways. Ceiling height activated regions

papers.title: PDF-0915
papers.excerpt: Change the color of a room's light and watch what happens to mood, memory, and the way people judge the room itself. Igor Knez at the University of Gavle showed in this 2001 study in the Journal of Environmental Psychology that the color temperature of indoor lighting affects cognitive performance and room perception — and the effects differ by gender. Lighting research has long focused on how much light reaches the desk. But light carries color information too. Warm light (low color temperature, yellowish) and cool light (high color temperature, bluish-white) activate different photoreceptor pathways and may influence mood and cognition through non-visual channels. Previous studies found mixed results, partly because they did not test multiple cognitive tasks or control for gender. Knez tested subjects under three lighting conditions: warm white, cool white, and artificial daylight. He measured self-reported mood (positive and negative affect), short-term memory, long-term memory, problem-solving, and room light estimation. The study used a between-subjects design. No direct effect on mood emerged. But cognitive performance told a different story. Subjects performed better on shor

papers.title: PDF-0916
papers.excerpt: Stand on a straight street in Phoenix and point toward the library three blocks north. You will aim within a few degrees of the correct direction. Now walk to a diagonal street and try again. Your pointing error will roughly double. Daniel Montello showed in this 1991 study in Environment and Behavior that the angularity of urban route structure — whether streets run along a grid or at oblique angles — significantly distorts how accurately pedestrians orient themselves toward unseen landmarks. Cognitive maps are not perfect copies of the physical world. People regularize angles, straighten curves, and align features with cardinal directions. Tversky (1981) showed these systematic distortions in sketch maps. But nobody had tested whether the angular structure of a real street network — grid versus oblique — affects the accuracy of spatial orientation on the ground, while people actually stand in the environment. Montello stopped 60 pedestrians at three locations in the same neighborhood. One location sat on a street aligned with the local grid. Two locations sat on streets that ran obliquely to the grid. At each stop, participants pointed toward five non-visible targets: both local

papers.title: Sensory material architectures: CNC-knitted textile structures as responsive sensory environments for autism
papers.excerpt: Can a knitted wall filter the world for a child with autism? Sean Ahlquist at the University of Michigan developed sensory material architectures — textile structures that combine touch, movement, and spatial enclosure — in this 2016 paper in the International Journal of Architectural Computing. The work proposes that the building itself can become a sensory filter, using its material structure to modulate the sensory inputs that overwhelm children with autism spectrum disorder. Children with ASD often struggle with sensory overload. Standard rooms deliver visual, auditory, and tactile stimuli simultaneously, with no mechanism for the occupant to regulate the intensity. Fluorescent lights flicker at frequencies that neurotypical occupants do not notice but that ASD-sensitive visual systems detect. Hard surfaces reflect sound that blends into an undifferentiated acoustic wash. Smooth walls and plastic furniture offer no tactile engagement, leaving the proprioceptive and vestibular systems unstimulated. The architectural environment — designed for neurotypical perception — becomes a source of distress for the occupant it is supposed to shelter. Ahlquist developed CNC-knitted textile

papers.title: PDF-0924
papers.excerpt: Does the color temperature of your office light shape your mood? Igor Knez and Ingela Enmarker at the Royal Institute of Technology in Gävle showed in this 1998 study in Environment and Behavior that lighting color temperature did not significantly affect cognitive performance — but it did interact with gender to influence mood. Office lighting standards focus on illuminance (how bright) and glare (how blinding). Color temperature — whether the light looks warm (reddish, 3000 K) or cool (bluish, 4000 K) — is often treated as a matter of taste. Knez and Enmarker tested whether it matters for cognitive work and emotional state. Participants worked on cognitive tasks for two hours under warm (3000 K) or cool (4000 K) white lighting. Cognitive performance showed no significant difference between conditions. But mood data revealed an interaction: warm light and cool light communicated different emotional meanings to men and women. The cognitive workload itself — two hours of sustained intellectual effort — diminished positive mood and increased negative mood regardless of lighting. For workplace designers, the study suggests that color temperature may be a subtler lever than illuminance

papers.title: PDF-0925
papers.excerpt: Do younger and older workers respond to the same office lighting differently? Igor Knez and Christina Kers at the University of Gävle showed in this 2000 study in Environment and Behavior that they do: warm white light preserved mood better in younger adults (~23 years), while cool white light did so in older adults (~65 years). Age changes the visual system. Older adults need more light to achieve the same retinal illuminance because aging lenses absorb more short-wavelength light. But does age also change the emotional meaning of lighting color temperature? The study used a between-subjects design. Younger and older participants completed cognitive tasks under warm (3000 K) or cool (4000 K) white lighting. Cognitive performance showed a main effect of age (younger outperformed older) but no significant lighting effect. Mood data revealed a two-way interaction between lamp type and age: warm light protected younger adults' mood during demanding work, while cool light did the same for older adults. Younger females preserved both positive and negative mood better than younger males. For designers of workplaces with age-diverse populations (hospitals, universities, intergenerational

papers.title: PDF-0927
papers.excerpt: How much window does a room need for good daylight? Christoph Reinhart at Harvard and Valerio Roberto Maria LoVerso at Politecnico di Torino proposed and validated a four-step daylighting design sequence in this 2010 paper in Lighting Research and Technology — a practical toolkit for architects working in the earliest design stages. Daylighting design usually happens late, when floor plans are already fixed. Reinhart and LoVerso's goal was to give architects rules of thumb that work at the programming and massing stage, before simulation software is practical. The sequence has four steps. First, calculate effective sky angles and set target daylight factors for each zone of the building. Second, run a daylight feasibility study to identify zones with high daylighting potential. Third, determine suitable room dimensions and surface reflectances using limiting-depth and window-head-height rules. Fourth, estimate the required glazing area using the Lynes daylight factor formula. Each step was validated against Radiance simulations of over 2,300 sidelit spaces. External obstructions were accounted for throughout. For architects and building planners, the sequence provides a structured

papers.title: PDF-0935
papers.excerpt: Can a building's acoustics change how you feel inside it? Alaa Algargoosh, Babak Soleimani, Sile O'Modhrain, and Mojtaba Navvab at the University of Michigan showed in this 2022 study in Building Acoustics that the acoustic environment of worship spaces shapes emotional responses — and that the match between sound and visual setting amplifies the effect. Architectural acoustics research typically focuses on speech clarity or music quality. Algargoosh's team asked a different question: does the acoustic character of a space (its reverberation, warmth, brightness) change the occupant's emotional state? Using VR with 3D spatial audio and 360° visuals, participants experienced worship spaces of different acoustic character. Self-report and physiological measures (heart rate, skin conductance) tracked emotional responses. Audiovisual congruency — when the acoustics matched the visual space — amplified the emotional intensity of the experience. Familiarity with the sound (e.g., culturally familiar chanting) further boosted the effect. For architects designing spaces intended to evoke specific emotional states (worship, meditation, performance), the study argues: acoustic design is not ab

papers.title: Soundscape versus environmental noise management: Complementary approaches to urban acoustic design
papers.excerpt: Two fields study the same phenomenon — urban sound — and reach opposite conclusions about what to do with it. Environmental noise management treats sound as a waste product: something to reduce, attenuate, and regulate through barriers, insulation, and decibel limits. Soundscape research treats sound as a resource: something to design, curate, and protect through careful attention to what people hear and how it makes them feel. This review clarified the conceptual relationship between these two traditions, arguing that soundscape and environmental noise management are complementary, not competing approaches — and that cities need both. The environmental noise management perspective has dominated regulatory and engineering practice for decades. From this viewpoint, sound is conceived as a waste product requiring reduction and management. The approach uses physical metrics — A-weighted equivalent continuous sound pressure level (LAeq), maximum sound levels (LAmax), noise exposure contours — to quantify the problem and specify the solution. Success means reducing the number on the meter. The tools are acoustic barriers, setback distances, building envelope specifications, traffic mana

papers.title: Sound pressure level is the dominant acoustic feature shaping spiritual experience in mosque architecture — EEG evidence reveals a narrow optimal range
papers.excerpt: The acoustic signature of a mosque shapes how worshippers experience spiritual presence. This 2023 study combined expert judgment with electroencephalography (EEG) to measure how specific sound characteristics — pressure level, amplitude, source location, quality, concept, and type — affect both expert perception of spiritual efficacy and direct brain responses in visitors to the Nasir al-Mulk Mosque in Isfahan, Iran. Architects have long intuited that sound matters in religious spaces, but the evidence has been mostly qualitative. This work quantifies it. Sixty-four experts in Islamic architecture and acoustics ranked the importance of six sound characteristics for creating or enhancing spiritual experience in mosque design. Sound pressure level — the intensity or loudness of acoustic waves — ranked first, followed by sound concept, amplitude, quality, source, and type. The experts showed high inter-rater reliability (Cronbach's alpha > 0.71 for all dimensions), indicating genuine agreement about sound's role. To test whether these expert judgments mapped to neural responses, the researchers recorded EEG brain activity from 23 visitors while they heard the Adhan (Islamic call to p
papers.authors: Alessandra De Cesaris, Ali Sadeghi habababad

papers.title: PDF-0944
papers.excerpt: The soundscape of a room shapes how you work in it. Md Robiul Hossain, Manish Manohare, and Eoin King at the University of Galway reviewed 22 papers on indoor psychoacoustics in this 2024 systematic review in Building Acoustics, asking which sound quality metrics best predict how comfortable occupants feel across different types of rooms. Indoor noise standards focus on decibel levels. But the same 45 dB can feel acceptable in a library and intolerable in a private office. Psychoacoustic metrics — loudness, sharpness, roughness, how much a sound fluctuates — capture the perceptual qualities that decibels miss. The review found that psychoacoustic metrics predicted perceived annoyance better than A-weighted levels alone, especially in open-plan offices and classrooms. Loudness and sharpness were the most consistent predictors. But the field is young: few studies linked these metrics to specific work tasks (focus, collaboration, rest), and the tools to design indoor soundscapes around such criteria remain underdeveloped. For acoustics engineers and workplace designers, the review argues: move beyond dB targets. Specify psychoacoustic criteria — loudness floors and ceilings, sharpness

papers.title: Soundscape and natural landscape as a design construct for improving psycho-physiological health in cities: a semi-systematic literature review
papers.excerpt: Birds singing in the park calm your nervous system in measurable ways — and that calming effect is stronger when you can also see trees. Timothy Iyendo, David Welch, and Patrick Uwajeh synthesized 98 peer-reviewed studies in this 2024 semi-systematic literature review in Cities & Health to show how urban designers can harness the combined power of natural soundscapes and landscapes to reduce stress and restore attention in cities, where neither resource is abundant. The review's most potent finding concerns audio-visual integration: when natural sounds are paired with natural visual landscapes, the restorative effect exceeds the sum of the auditory and visual components alone. A three-minute exposure to birdsong paired with a tree-filled urban park view produced greater stress recovery than either modality presented separately. The brain appears to treat combined sensory input from natural environments as a coherent "sensescape" — a multisensory pattern that the autonomic stress response recognizes as a safety signal. This superadditive effect has direct design implications: a park with both vegetation and wildlife habitat will outperform one with vegetation alone, not by the margi
papers.authors: Iyendo, T.O., Welch, D. & Uwajeh, P.C.

papers.title: Tranquillity by design — architectural and landscape interventions to improve the soundscape for health
papers.excerpt: Tranquillity is not silence — it is a specific combination of natural sounds, visual greenery, and the absence of mechanical and traffic noise. This design framework monograph proposes architectural and landscape interventions to create tranquil environments, defining tranquillity in measurable terms and specifying the design strategies that produce it. The Tranquillity Rating method, developed through extensive field research, combines objective acoustic measurement with perceived tranquillity ratings to derive a predictive model. The model shows that perceived tranquillity is positively predicted by the presence of natural sounds (birdsong, water, wind) and visual natural features (vegetation, sky, water), and negatively predicted by mechanical and traffic sounds and visible built/industrial features. A Tranquillity Rating above a threshold predicts restorative health benefits — reduction in stress markers, improved mood, and attention recovery. The design interventions are organized by scale. At the landscape scale: vegetated barriers and earth moundings attenuate traffic noise while providing visual screening. Water features provide pleasant masking and increase the natural sou

papers.title: PDF-0954
papers.excerpt: Noise in classrooms harms children — and the damage is measurable. Bridget Shield at South Bank University and Julie Dockrell at the Institute of Education reviewed the evidence in this 2003 paper in Building Acoustics, covering speech clarity, cognitive tasks, academic scores, and annoyance. Children hear differently from adults. Their brains are still learning to separate speech from noise. In classrooms with poor acoustics — high background noise and long reverberation — this task becomes harder. The review found consistent effects across studies. Background noise above 35 dB(A) reduced speech clarity for children, especially younger children and those with hearing difficulties. Chronic noise exposure was linked to lower reading scores and poorer attention. Children reported annoyance at noise levels that adults tolerated. Reverberation times above 0.6 seconds degraded speech in typical classrooms. The authors noted that many schools exceeded these thresholds. For school architects and acoustics engineers, the review sets clear targets: keep background noise below 35 dB(A) and reverberation below 0.6 seconds for primary classrooms. These are not aspirational — they are the minim

papers.title: Classroom acoustic renovation: Reverberation time, speech clarity, and the measurable impact of absorptive treatment
papers.excerpt: A classroom that sounds reverberant is a classroom where children cannot learn. This study measured the acoustic performance of classrooms before and after renovation with sound-absorbing materials — ceiling fiberglass panels, wall-mounted absorptive panels, and carpet flooring — providing quantitative evidence that targeted acoustic treatment produces dramatic improvements in the room acoustic parameters that determine speech intelligibility. The pre-renovation classrooms were acoustically hostile. Hard plaster ceilings, painted masonry walls, linoleum floors, and large glass windows created a reverberant environment where the teacher's voice bounced repeatedly off every surface before reaching the student's ear. Each reflection added delay, and the accumulated delayed energy blurred the temporal envelope of speech — the pattern of consonant-vowel transitions that carries linguistic information. The result was reduced speech clarity: students could hear the teacher talking but could not always distinguish what was being said, particularly at the back of the room, particularly for students with even mild hearing loss, and particularly when competing noise from adjacent classrooms o

papers.title: PDF-0956
papers.excerpt: How a room is laid out determines how private and territorial people feel within it — and these feelings have measurable consequences for behaviour. Natheer Abu-Obeid and Majd Al-Homoud demonstrated this in a 2000 study in the *Architectural Science Review*, examining how the spatial layout of university public spaces affects occupants' sense of privacy and territoriality. The study draws on Altman's (1975) privacy regulation theory, which proposes that privacy is not simply the absence of others but a dynamic process of controlling the level of social interaction — opening or closing access to the self depending on circumstances. Spatial layout provides the physical infrastructure that enables or constrains this regulation. Abu-Obeid and Al-Homoud studied multiple public spaces within a university campus — lobbies, corridors, courtyards, and communal study areas — that varied in their spatial configuration: degree of enclosure, number and placement of entry/exit points, visual exposure to passing traffic, seating arrangement, and adjacency to private (offices) versus public (corridors) zones. The findings confirmed two key relationships. First, *sense of privacy* was highest in sp

papers.title: PDF-0958
papers.excerpt: Some people navigate effortlessly through new cities; others get lost in their own neighborhood. Aiden Arnold, Andrea Protzner, Signe Bray, Richard Levy, and Giuseppe Iaria at the University of Calgary showed in this 2013 study in the Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience that these individual differences trace to measurable variations in brain network architecture. Spatial orientation requires integrating information from many brain regions — the hippocampus (place cells), the parietal cortex (spatial reference frames), the prefrontal cortex (planning), and the supramarginal gyrus (body-centered coordinates). Previous work focused on single regions; Arnold's team asked how the entire network's wiring relates to navigation skill. Participants completed a spatial orientation task while undergoing fMRI. The researchers measured two properties of their brain networks: network configuration (which regions are functionally connected during the task) and network efficiency (how well information flows through the resting-state network). People who oriented more accurately showed greater activity in the right supramarginal gyrus, right precentral cortex, and left hippocampus. They also showed

papers.title: Environmental design and psychiatric ward safety: Crowding, privacy, and assault rates in inpatient settings
papers.excerpt: Psychiatric wards are among the most dangerous workplaces in healthcare — and the architecture of those wards may be making them more dangerous. This review examined the relationship between environmental design and safety in inpatient psychiatric facilities, documenting how spatial factors like crowding, privacy deprivation, and ward layout contribute to the assault rates, self-harm incidents, and patient distress that characterize acute psychiatric care. The numbers are stark. People in ward environments occupy 70 percent less personal space than their counterparts in community settings, producing increased crowding and loss of privacy as risk factors for safety incidents including verbal and physical aggression. Assault rates in psychiatric beds run dramatically higher than in other healthcare settings: 2.54 assaults per bed yearly in psychiatric wards compared to 0.37 in general medical settings — a nearly seven-fold difference that cannot be attributed solely to patient psychopathology. Assault rates increased by 32.9 percent from 1978 to 1980 during a period of deinstitutionalization in which patient populations became more acute, ward populations became more compressed, and

papers.title: PDF-0966
papers.excerpt: Place attachment is political. Andrés Di Masso at the Universitat de Barcelona, John Dixon at the Open University, and Bernardo Hernández at the Universidad de La Laguna argued in this 2017 chapter in the Handbook of Environmental Psychology and Quality of Life Research that how people bond with places reflects — and reinforces — social power. Most research on place attachment treats it as an individual feeling: a warm connection to your home or neighborhood. Di Masso and colleagues argued this misses the politics. Whose places get protected? Who gets displaced? Inequality, exclusion, and contested belonging shape place bonds. The chapter reviews three dimensions. Place attachment: the emotional bond to a location. Place identity: how a location becomes part of who you are. Place belonging: the felt right to occupy a space. The authors connected these to urban development, gentrification, and immigrant integration. When developers reshape a neighborhood, they do not just remove housing — they sever the psychological and political ties that existing residents built over decades. For urban planners and community designers, the chapter argues: treat place bonds as social capital. Disp

papers.title: Residential crowding impairs children s psychological health — housing density matters for development
papers.excerpt: Children who grow up in crowded homes show elevated psychological distress — and the relationship persists after controlling for poverty, family dysfunction, and neighborhood quality. Gary Evans, Susan Saegert, and Rebecca Harris demonstrated this in a 2001 study in *Environment and Behavior*, establishing that residential crowding — too many people per room — is an independent risk factor for children's psychological health. The study measured residential density (persons per room), crowding perception (subjective experience of insufficient space), and children's psychological health using standardized assessments of behavioral problems, emotional distress, social withdrawal, and attention difficulties. The sample included children from low-income families, allowing the researchers to examine whether crowding effects were separable from the effects of poverty. The results were clear: higher residential density predicted more behavioral problems and emotional distress in children, independent of income, parental education, and family structure. The effect was mediated by elevated chronic stress — children in crowded homes showed higher cortisol levels and more physiological markers

papers.title: Culture extends cognition — what architects can learn from cognitive integration theory
papers.excerpt: Your mind doesn't end at your skull. This counterintuitive proposition — that human cognition is not a solitary process unfolding inside the head but rather an extended system that leverages cultural tools, social learning, and environmental structure — carries direct implications for how architects design spaces where thinking happens. Menary's framework, called cognitive integration, challenges the assumption that our core cognitive capacities are fixed biological givens. Instead, it argues that phylogenetically ancient sensorimotor systems — the same neural circuitry humans share with other primates — have been progressively transformed through cultural evolution. Writing systems, number notation, diagrams, and normative cognitive practices reshape what our minds can do. The evidence comes from unexpected overlaps. Neural regions that map finger position (finger gnosis, crucial for grasping objects) overlap with neural substrates for numerical reasoning. This is not accident. Human cultures developed finger-counting systems thousands of years ago; the neural systems evolved to support it. The sensorimotor capacity — knowing where your fingers are in space — became a scaffold for

papers.title: Culture and the evolution of human cooperation: Cumulative cultural adaptation explains human ecological dominance
papers.excerpt: In the last 60,000 years, humans have occupied virtually every terrestrial habitat on Earth — from Arctic tundra to tropical rainforest, from Himalayan plateaux to Pacific atolls, from Saharan desert to Patagonian steppe. Homo sapiens occupies a larger geographic range than any other terrestrial vertebrate species. No other land mammal comes close. Robert Boyd, Peter Richerson, and Joseph Henrich argue in this 2011 paper in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences that this extraordinary ecological dominance cannot be explained by individual cognitive ability alone. It was made possible by cumulative cultural adaptation — the uniquely human capacity to accumulate adaptive information across generations through social learning, producing a body of knowledge that no individual could discover in a lifetime. The conventional explanation — that humans are simply smarter, with bigger brains and more cognitive power — is wrong, the authors argue. Individual human cognitive ability is not sufficient to acquire all the information necessary to survive in any single habitat, let alone the dozens of radically different environments humans occupy. Even the simplest foraging societies de
papers.authors: Boyd, R., Richerson, P. & Henrich, J.

papers.title: PDF-0979
papers.excerpt: Pick up a rake and use it to pull a food pellet toward you. After a few minutes of practice, your brain has changed: the neural map of your hand now extends to the tip of the tool. Angelo Maravita and Atsushi Iriki review the evidence in this 2004 paper in *Trends in Cognitive Sciences* that tool use literally reshapes the brain's representation of the body — and that this has profound implications for how we understand the human relationship with the built environment. The key evidence comes from single-neuron recordings in macaque monkeys trained to use a rake. In the intraparietal cortex, bimodal neurons respond to both somatosensory stimuli on the hand and visual stimuli near the hand. Crucially, some of these neurons have a visual receptive field (vRF) that remains anchored to the body surface and extends into nearby space. After the monkey uses a rake for several minutes, the vRFs of these bimodal neurons expand to include the entire length of the tool — as if the tip of the rake has become the new fingertip. When the tool is set down, the vRFs shrink back. This is not a metaphor. The expansion is specific, measurable, and activity-dependent: it occurs only after active tool

papers.title: PDF-0980
papers.excerpt: A ventriloquist's puppet appears to speak because your brain resolves a spatial conflict between eyes and ears by trusting the eyes. Patrick Bruns reviews in this 2019 mini review in *Frontiers in Integrative Neuroscience* how this ancient parlour trick has become one of the most productive experimental tools for studying how the brain integrates information from multiple senses. The ventriloquism effect (VE) occurs when a sound source and a visual stimulus are presented at slightly different locations: the perceived location of the sound shifts toward the visual stimulus. This visual bias reflects the brain's general strategy of weighting more reliable sensory inputs more heavily — and for spatial localization, vision is usually more precise than audition. The strength of the bias scales with two factors: the relative reliability of each modality (degrading visual precision increases auditory influence) and prior expectations about whether the signals share a common source (the "unity assumption"). The ventriloquism aftereffect (VAE) is more surprising. After repeated exposure to spatially discrepant audiovisual stimuli, the perceived location of subsequent sound-only stimuli is p

papers.title: Architectural atmosphere: Affective meaning, situation, and the design of felt space
papers.excerpt: You walk into a room and know immediately how it makes you feel. Before you notice the furniture, before you read the signage, before you assess the proportions — you feel the atmosphere. This paper examines architectural atmosphere as a phenomenon, arguing that it is composed of affective meanings identifying a situation or place that allows us to understand the character of our environment before we analytically understand its properties. Atmosphere is the emotional first impression of a space — the pre-cognitive, pre-verbal sense of whether this place is welcoming or forbidding, calm or agitated, sacred or profane, intimate or institutional. This impression forms within seconds, before the occupant has consciously processed the room's dimensions, materials, colors, or lighting. It is the felt quality of being-in-a-place, not the known quality of a-place-assessed. The concept comes primarily from the philosophical tradition of Gernot Böhme, who positioned atmosphere as a third entity between subject and object — neither a property of the environment (the room does not "contain" atmosphere as it contains air) nor a projection of the observer (atmosphere is not merely mood imposed

papers.title: The Content of Imagined Sounds Changes Visual Motion Perception in the Cross-Bounce Illusion
papers.excerpt: Two black discs glide toward each other on a white screen. At the moment they overlap, you imagine the sharp crack of billiard balls colliding. Even though no sound played, you now see the discs bounce apart — a perception that would not have occurred if you had imagined a softer, rising tone instead. Christopher Berger and Henrik Ehrsson at the Karolinska Institutet demonstrated that the specific content of imagined sounds — not merely the act of imagining — alters visual motion perception. When participants imagined a collision-like 'damped' sound at the moment two discs overlapped, they perceived significantly more bounces than when they imagined the same waveform played in reverse, establishing that mental imagery and sensory perception share representational resources that interact across modalities. The investigators exploited the cross-bounce illusion, a well-validated multisensory paradigm in which two moving discs can be perceived as either passing through or bouncing off each other. Previous research established that real collision-like sounds increase bounce percepts, but whether imagined sounds produce the same effect had never been tested. In Experiment 1a (N = 24 heal

papers.title: PDF-0986
papers.excerpt: How do you describe the structure of a Gothic cathedral window in a way that a computer can understand — and generate new designs from? George Stiny's 1980 paper in *Environment and Planning B* introduces the formal machinery of *shape grammars*, a computational framework for defining the spatial languages of two- and three-dimensional designs. The paper begins with a rigorous definition. A *shape* is a limited arrangement of straight lines in a Cartesian coordinate system with an associated Euclidean metric. Lines have endpoints, and two lines sharing an endpoint combine into a single line. Each shape is specified by its *maximal lines* — the longest lines that cannot be extended further by combining with adjacent colinear lines. The result: every shape has a unique canonical representation. A *shape rule* transforms one shape into another. Formally, a rule u → v says: whenever you find a shape geometrically similar to u within a design, replace it with v (after the same geometric transformation — translation, rotation, scaling, reflection). The replacement is exact: the system finds u within the current shape, subtracts u, adds v, and computes the result. This formalism enables t

papers.title: PDF-0987: The predictive processing framework naturally leads to the discovery and use of extended processing
headings: PDF-0987: The predictive processing framework naturally leads to the discovery and use of extended processing
papers.excerpt: # PDF-0987: The predictive processing framework naturally leads to the discovery and use of extended processing ## Paper Type theoretical ## Core Theoretical Contribution The predictive processing framework naturally leads to the discovery and use of extended processing regimes defined across heterogeneous mixtures of biological and non-biological resources. This solves the long-standing puzzle of how brains recruit the right non-neural processing resources at the right time, revealing how human brains spawn and maintain extended minds. ## Illustrations & Diagrams - Figures: 5 | Diagrams: 0 | Models: 0 ## Key References - No potential conﬂict of interest was reported by the author(s). - This work was carried out with the support of European Research Council Synergy Grant (XSCAPE) - Barrett, L.F. 2017. papers.title: Isovist field analysis: How city block geometry shapes visual perception and why architects should measure visibility
papers.excerpt: The geometry of a street block determines what you can see from any vantage point within it — and that pattern of visibility varies predictably enough to become a design metric. This 2001 paper in Environment and Planning B introduces isovist field analysis: a mathematical method for mapping the visual envelope from every point in a space, revealing how urban morphology shapes human perception at the scale of individual blocks, street networks, and entire neighborhoods. An isovist is the set of all points visible from a single vantage point in a space — the visual polygon you would draw if you traced every sightline from where you stand. An isovist field is the ensemble of all such polygons computed across a grid of vantage points within the space. By measuring properties of these polygons — their area, perimeter, compactness, maximum visual distance, minimum visual distance, and average distance — Batty creates a quantitative language for describing how open or constrained a space feels across its entire footprint. The method transforms the subjective experience of spatial openness into a measurable, mappable, computationally tractable field. The core demonstration uses a T-shaped

papers.title: PDF-0997: There is a significant difference in rate of travel between each of the five settings, F(4, 54) = 12
headings: PDF-0997: There is a significant difference in rate of travel between each of the five settings, F(4, 54) = 12

Rate of travel was lowest in Setting 5 (most complex) but was highest in Setting 3, which has the only symmetrical plan layout, possibly easier to understand despite higher topological complexity. ## Methods & Sample - **N** = 55 - **Design**: between_subjects - **Stimuli/Setting**: See page images ## Key Statistics - **p-value**: < 0.001 (simplicity-legibility correlation r=.91); ns (experience differences between groups) - **Effect size**: Spearman's Rho r=.91 (simplicity-legibility). ICD range: 1.00-1.75 across 5 settings (objective complexity measure) (correlation) - **Test**: N=55 (21 females, 34 males), primarily undergraduate students. 5×3 between-subjects factorial: 5 floor plan configurations (ICD range 1.00-1.75) × 3 signage types (none, textual, graphic). DV: wayfinding accuracy (rate of travel and number of errors). Card-sorting pr

headings: PDF-1000: Studies of wayfinding and spatial learning can benefit from a more rigorous analytic description of
papers.excerpt: # PDF-1000: Studies of wayfinding and spatial learning can benefit from a more rigorous analytic description of ## Paper Type methodological ## Overview Studies of wayfinding and spatial learning can benefit from a more rigorous analytic description of building layout and exploration paths that exhibit their own pattern. Search patterns are strongly shaped according to the degree of integration of each space and choice node of the circulation system within the overall layout, suggesting a search structure whereby the intelligible properties of layouts interact with navigation rules to produce characteristic patterns of exploration. ## Key Statistics - No inferential statistics - Test: Methodological paper: Studies of wayfinding and spatial learning can benefit from a more rigorous analytic description of building layout and exploration paths that exhibit their own pattern.
