papers.title: The Built Environment and the Brain — How architectural design shapes neural processing
papers.excerpt: The room you design does not simply shelter behavior—it shapes how the brain itself processes information. This 2023 narrative review by Tawil and Kühn synthesizes emerging neuroimaging evidence showing that architectural properties—spatial layout, detail density, color, ceiling height, even window size—activate distinct neural systems in predictable ways. The finding reframes architecture from aesthetic choice into a tool for modulating cognition and affect. The research sits at the intersection of environmental psychology and neuroscience, using fMRI and EEG to measure brain activation while participants view or inhabit spaces varying in controlled design parameters. The central finding is that real, detailed architectural images engage episodic memory and spatial reasoning systems—the retrosplenial cortex, superior temporal gyrus, and parahippocampal gyrus—more strongly than simplified or abstract representations. Simpler sketches activate different regions associated with abstract calculation and pattern recognition. This suggests that architectural detail acts as a cognitive payload: higher detail density recruits memory and narrative processing; lower detail engages more form

papers.title: Biophilic design integrates nature into buildings—and the research suggests cognition, immunity, and creativity follow
papers.excerpt: The modern office is a sensory desert. Fluorescent lights, HVAC hum, synthetic materials, and recirculated air create a controlled environment optimized for cost, not human biology. A 2024 review by Sjövall and Spiers assembles evidence that this design choice carries a cognitive and physiological cost—and that reinstating natural elements into buildings triggers measurable improvements in stress, sleep, immune function, and creative thinking. The theory underlying this effect is not new. Ulrich's Stress Recovery Theory (1983) proposed that exposure to natural environments triggers automatic parasympathetic activation: your nervous system downshifts, cortisol drops, and your prefrontal cortex becomes available for reasoning rather than threat-detection. What Sjövall and Spiers document is the breadth of this effect across health and cognitive domains and, critically, the architectural leverage point: you do not need pristine wilderness. Biophilic design—the intentional integration of natural elements, visual patterns, light variability, and spatial qualities into built environments—produces many of the same measurable outcomes. The evidence clusters into five domains. First, stress
papers.authors: Isabelle A. K. Sjövall, Hugo J. Spiers

papers.title: PDF-1010: Abbas, Kumar & Mclachlan (2005) — Psychological and Physiological Effects of Light and Colour on Space Users
headings: PDF-1010: Abbas, Kumar & Mclachlan (2005) — Psychological and Physiological Effects of Light and Colour on Space Users
papers.excerpt: # PDF-1010: Abbas, Kumar & Mclachlan (2005) — Psychological and Physiological Effects of Light and Colour on Space Users ## Paper Type Empirical research (within-subjects, lab-controlled ECG experiment) ## Core Finding Exposure to different coloured and intensity lights produces measurable changes in individual heart rate over 8-minute adaptation periods, but the direction of HR change (increase vs. decrease) is idiosyncratic—the same colour light raises HR in some participants and lowers it in others. A two-way ANOVA confirms the change is significant per individual (person factor p = 0.00) but not significant when averaging across all subjects per colour (colour factor p = 0.27). ## Methods & Sample - **N** = 15 (3 males, 12 females), healthy university students, no medication - **Design**: Within-subjects, all participants exposed to all 8 light conditions - **Setting**: Neutrally coloured laboratory (10m × 4m), seated 1.5m from 2m white non-reflecting screen - **Duration**: ~110 minutes total; 8 minutes per colour condition (adapted for first 6 min, ECG recorded last 2 min), 3-minute breaks between conditions - **Stimuli**: Screen illuminated by floodlights with coloured globes

papers.title: PDF-1011
papers.excerpt: Insufficient artificial lighting in the workplace does not just make tasks harder to see — it changes workers' moods and physiology. S.M. Aryani and colleagues presented preliminary evidence for this in a 2021 study published in the *IOP Conference Series: Earth and Environmental Science*, measuring both subjective mood states and physiological markers in workers under different levels of artificial illumination. The study measured workers' responses to lighting conditions that fell below recommended illuminance standards for workplace tasks. The findings — described as preliminary — suggested that inadequate lighting was associated with negative mood states (increased fatigue, decreased vigour, increased tension) and with physiological indicators consistent with reduced alertness and increased stress. The physiological mechanisms linking insufficient lighting to mood and arousal are well-established in the circadian photobiology literature: melanopsin-containing intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) require sufficient light intensity — particularly in the blue-enriched portion of the spectrum — to drive the non-visual pathways that promote daytime alertness,

papers.title: PDF-1015
papers.excerpt: Women who work in offices without windows have higher cortisol at night, lower melatonin in the morning, more depressive symptoms, and worse sleep quality than women who work near windows. Francine Harb, Maria Paz Hidalgo, and Betina Martau documented these differences in a 2015 cross-sectional study published in *Chronobiology International* — providing the first field evidence directly linking workspace daylight deprivation to disrupted cortisol and melatonin rhythms under natural conditions. Twenty female employees in Porto Alegre, Brazil were split into two groups: 10 worked in spaces "with window" (desks within 2 meters of a windowed wall) and 10 "without window" (interior spaces with no window proximity). All wore Actiwatch 2 actigraphs (Philips Respironics) continuously for seven days, measuring both activity patterns and ambient light exposure. Saliva samples were collected at 8:00 AM, 4:00 PM, and 10:00 PM on the last day. The groups differed dramatically in light exposure. The "with window" group received significantly higher average light (mesor: 191.04 ± 133.36 lux vs. 73.8 ± 42.05 lux; t = −2.651, p = 0.023) and greater amplitude of the light-dark cycle (298.07 ± 222.9

papers.title: PDF-1024
papers.excerpt: Does nature make people happier? Ethan McMahan and David Estes at Western Oregon University pooled 32 studies in this 2015 meta-analysis in The Journal of Positive Psychology and found a clear answer: yes. Contact with natural environments raised positive affect and lowered negative affect, with small-to-medium effect sizes. Decades of individual studies have shown that walks in parks, views of trees, and time in gardens improve mood. But effect sizes vary widely, and some studies find null results. A meta-analysis settles the debate by pooling data across many samples. McMahan and Estes included 32 studies comparing mood before and after contact with natural versus built or indoor environments. The pooled effect was positive: nature contact increased positive affect (hedges' g = 0.37) and decreased negative affect (g = 0.26). The effects held across real outdoor exposure and simulated nature (photos, videos, VR). Duration of exposure and type of natural setting did not moderate the effect; even brief contact worked. For architects and urban planners, the meta-analysis provides a population-level warrant for biophilic design. Adding green views, indoor plants, and access to courtya

papers.title: Indoor CO2 and temperature affect physiology before occupants notice discomfort
papers.excerpt: Occupants report feeling fine in conditions that are already degrading their physiology. This study in *Science and Technology for the Built Environment* measured physiological responses to indoor environmental quality — specifically elevated CO2 concentrations and temperature — and found that measurable changes in heart rate, blood pressure, and cognitive performance occurred at levels where occupants reported no subjective discomfort. The experimental protocol exposed participants to controlled indoor environments where CO2 concentration (ranging from outdoor baseline ~400 ppm to elevated indoor levels of 1000–2500 ppm) and temperature (from comfortable ~22°C to warm ~28°C) were systematically varied. Physiological measurements — heart rate, heart rate variability, blood pressure, respiratory rate — were recorded continuously. Cognitive performance was assessed using standardized tasks. Subjective comfort ratings were collected simultaneously. The critical finding: physiological measures changed before subjective reports did. At CO2 levels of 1000–1500 ppm — common in occupied, under-ventilated offices — heart rate variability decreased (indicating increased sympathetic nervous s

papers.title: PDF-1027
papers.excerpt: Can interior design restore attention and reduce stress in a tiny apartment? Nawakhun Sornubol and Apichoke Lekagul at Chiang Mai University showed in this 2025 study in the Journal of Architectural Planning Research and Studies that restorative design features — not size — drive recovery in small residential units. The pandemic pushed millions into remote work in compact apartments. These spaces often lack the elements that research links to stress recovery: nature views, daylight, and spatial variety. Eighty-nine participants with remote-work experience evaluated six virtual living environments in VR. Each combined a geometric form (rectilinear or curvilinear) with a style (minimal, natural, or rustic). Restorative environments scored higher than non-restorative controls across every condition. The best combination was rectilinear form with minimal style and curvilinear form with natural style. Surprisingly, the rustic style with natural curves and materials did not outperform simpler designs, suggesting that visual coherence matters more than material abundance. For residential and interior designers, the study argues: small is not the problem. Even compact apartments can restor

papers.title: PDF-1028
papers.excerpt: In the quietest classrooms at the University of British Columbia, a typical male instructor achieves "excellent" speech intelligibility throughout the room. In the noisiest, even a loud male instructor cannot achieve "good" quality at the back. Murray Hodgson measured and ranked all 279 unrestricted-access university classrooms at UBC in this 2002 paper in the *Journal of the Acoustical Society of America* — producing the most comprehensive acoustical survey of a university campus in the literature. Speech intelligibility was quantified by the Speech Transmission Index (STI) and Speech Intelligibility (SI), predicted from physical acoustical measurements in unoccupied and 70%-occupied classrooms. The quality scale ranged from "excellent" (STI ≥ 0.80, SI ≥ 98%) through "very good," "good," "fair," and "poor" to "bad" (STI < 0.30, SI < 80%). The just-noticeable difference in STI is 0.03; a change of 0.1 represents a "practically significant change." Results for the "typical" male instructor case (average voice level): 81% of UBC's 279 classrooms achieved "good," "very good," or "excellent" quality. But 50 classrooms (18%) were only "fair" or "poor," and two were "bad" — all due to hi

papers.title: Beyond ocularcentrism: Why architecture must recover tactile, kinesthetic, and atmospheric perception
papers.excerpt: Architecture has been looking at the world when it should have been touching it. This review traces the architectural discipline's historical emphasis on visual representation and its consequences — an ocularcentrism in architectural thinking that marginalizes tactile and other non-visual sensory dimensions. The result is buildings designed for the eye that neglect the body, produced through media (drawings, photographs, renderings) that suppress the sensory richness of inhabited space. The history of ocularcentrism in architecture parallels the broader Western philosophical tradition that Heidegger and Merleau-Ponty critiqued. Plato's metaphor of the sun and the Forms established vision as the privileged sense — the sense most closely allied with truth, reason, and knowledge. Renaissance perspective drawing encoded this privilege into architectural practice by providing a geometric system for representing three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional surface that is exclusively visual. The photograph extended the hegemony by making architecture's primary medium of dissemination an image. The digital rendering completed the encirclement: architecture's design process, evaluation, pu

papers.title: PDF-1036
papers.excerpt: Open-plan offices save space but scatter attention. SP Banbury and DC Berry showed in this 2005 study in Ergonomics that office noise — ringing phones, nearby speech, keyboard clatter — disrupts focused work through two distinct mechanisms, and that practical acoustic fixes can reduce the damage. Previous research had shown that noise hurts cognitive work, but which noise sources do the most harm and why? Banbury and Berry set out to separate the pathways. Participants completed office-like tasks (proofreading, mental arithmetic, memory recall) under quiet conditions and under realistic office noise. The study identified two routes through which noise disrupts: distraction (involuntary capture of attention by changing or meaningful sounds, especially speech) and arousal (general stress response that impairs complex cognition). Speech was the most harmful noise component because it activates both pathways at once: its meaning captures attention and its unpredictability raises stress. The authors proposed practical countermeasures: speech-masking background sound, absorptive ceiling panels to reduce speech transmission, zoning that separates phone-heavy roles from deep-focus workers,

papers.title: Cross-cultural perception of real and represented space: Pictorial versus spatial cognition
papers.excerpt: Does a photograph of a room feel like the room? Not to everyone, and not in the same way. This review examined cross-cultural differences in the perception of real and represented space, revealing that the visual conventions Western cultures take for granted — linear perspective, photographic realism, architectural drafting — are culturally specific skills that not all populations share. The finding has profound implications for how architects communicate spatial designs and how researchers use images as proxies for environmental experience. The fundamental distinction is between real space and pictorial space. Different types of perceptual skills are required for understanding pictorial representations versus navigating actual physical environments. Real space is three-dimensional, multisensory, and navigable — you can walk through it, touch surfaces, hear echoes, sense temperature, and update your spatial model continuously as you move. Pictorial space is two-dimensional, visual-only, and static — it presents a frozen projection of three-dimensional information onto a flat surface, requiring the viewer to reconstruct depth, scale, and spatial relationships from monocular cues tha

papers.title: PDF-1043
papers.excerpt: How do people build a mental picture of their city? Donald Appleyard at UC Berkeley explored this question in his 1970 paper in Environment and Behavior, studying residents of Ciudad Guayana, a new city in eastern Venezuela, to identify the styles and methods people use to structure urban space in their minds. Kevin Lynch had identified five elements of urban mental maps: paths, edges, nodes, districts, and landmarks. Appleyard extended this work by studying how these elements combine differently across individuals and demographic groups. He gave residents drawing tasks and structured interviews. Two dominant mapping styles emerged: sequential (path-based, organized around routes traveled) and spatial (area-based, organized around zones and boundaries). Residents who drove the city used paths; those who walked used landmarks and districts. Higher-income residents produced more spatially accurate and complete maps. Familiarity increased map accuracy, but mode of travel shaped map structure independently. For urban designers and planners, the study says: different residents read the city differently. A city that relies on path legibility alone will be illegible to pedestrians who nav

papers.title: How building layouts shape the mental maps we form — and why architects should care about topological accuracy
papers.excerpt: When you enter a hospital for the first time, your brain constructs a spatial map. You piece together landmarks, decision points, and the logical flow of corridors. Haq and Zimring's 2002 study reveals something architects rarely quantify: the accuracy of that mental map depends directly on a building's topological structure — the logical relationships between spaces — and that accuracy, in turn, predicts whether you find your way successfully or become hopelessly lost. The paper studies three hospitals (urban, university, and city) using space syntax analysis, a mathematical framework that represents buildings as networks of axial lines (the longest unobstructed sightlines through space) and decision points (intersections where you must choose a direction). The research question was simple but consequential: What features of the physical layout best predict how people explore unfamiliar buildings and how well they encode spatial knowledge? The findings turned on a correlation. When participants explored the hospitals without guidance, the best single predictor of how extensively they used the space was the building's "public connectivity" — a measure of how many axial lines and no

papers.title: PDF-1050
papers.excerpt: Can you find your way through a building just by its design? Jerry Weisman at Penn State studied ten university buildings in this 1981 paper in Environment and Behavior and found that floor plan complexity predicted how often people got lost — more powerfully than any other factor. Wayfinding failure in buildings is common but rarely measured. Weisman proposed 'architectural legibility' as a design criterion: the degree to which a building's layout helps users navigate without signs, maps, or prior knowledge. He surveyed users of ten campus buildings, measuring self-reported wayfinding difficulty and comparing it to the visual/spatial properties of each floor plan. Floor plan simplicity — judged by independent raters — explained 56% of the variance in how often users reported getting lost. User familiarity explained only 9%. Buildings with complex, asymmetric floor plans produced the most wayfinding failures. For architects, the study provides a strong design lesson: if people get lost in your building, the floor plan is the problem, not the signage. Simple, symmetrical plans with clear circulation paths reduce wayfinding failures far more than signs or directories. The study used

papers.title: PDF-1052
papers.excerpt: Show a set of color chips to an architect and a layperson. Do they see the same thing? J. Hogg, S. Goodman, T. Porter, B. Mikellides, and D. E. Preddy showed in this 1979 study in the British Journal of Psychology that architects and non-architects rate color samples on five shared dimensions — dynamism, spatial quality, emotional tone, evaluation, and complexity — but weight them very differently. Color choice is central to architectural practice, yet most color research at the time used abstract chip samples outside any spatial context. Hogg and colleagues took a hybrid approach: participants rated both isolated Munsell color chips and the same colors placed in a simulated interior space (a model room). Semantic differential analysis revealed five factors common to all groups. Dynamism tracked how active or stimulating the color felt. Spatial quality tracked how the color affected perceived room size and openness. Emotional tone captured warmth and pleasantness. Evaluation and complexity rounded out the picture. Architects and non-architects agreed on the broad factor structure, but the determinants diverged sharply for evaluation and complexity. Architects weighed hue less and c

papers.title: PDF-1054
papers.excerpt: The light you see in most buildings looks nothing like daylight. Karen van Creveld and Kevin Mansfield at UCL's Institute for Environmental Design and Engineering measured how the spectral makeup of light differs between natural and built-lit settings and showed that common indoor lighting fails to match the spectral qualities that human circadian and affective systems evolved to expect. Light does more than help you see. Non-image-forming pathways in the retina use light to set circadian rhythms, regulate alertness, and modulate mood. These pathways respond most strongly to short-wavelength light near 480 nanometers, the peak sensitivity of the intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) that contain the photopigment melanopsin. But most building lighting is designed for visual comfort, not for circadian health. Nobody checks whether the spectrum of a fluorescent tube or LED panel actually stimulates the non-visual pathways that regulate sleep and alertness. Van Creveld and Mansfield visited a set of natural and built-lit settings ranging from seascapes and urban squares to interior offices and corridors. At each site, they took photometric and colorimetric snapsh

papers.title: PDF-1055
papers.excerpt: How much does office lighting affect how well people work? Iason Konstantzos, Seyed Amir Sadeghi, Michael Kim, Jie Xiong, and Athanasios Tzempelikos at Purdue University reviewed the evidence in this study published in a DOE-funded report, covering illuminance, luminance ratio, color temperature, and outside views. Lighting is the most researched indoor environmental quality factor after thermal comfort. But findings are scattered across small studies using different tasks, different metrics, and different lighting setups. The review aimed to consolidate what is known. The authors surveyed studies covering perception, visual acuity, reaction time, memory, reasoning, and labor productivity. Results converged on several points: task performance generally improves with higher illuminances up to about 1000 lux. Contrast ratios between 7:1 and 11:1 support comfort without glare. Higher correlated color temperatures (cooler, bluer light) tend to boost alertness and reaction speed. Outside views and daylight availability have positive but inconsistent effects. For lighting designers, the review offers a practical benchmark: aim for vertical illuminances above 300 lux at eye level, keep lu

papers.title: PDF-1056
papers.excerpt: Your office lighting could watch your face and change colour to improve your mood. Dominika Cupkova, Erik Kajati, Jozef Mocnej, Peter Papcun, Jiri Koziorek, and Iveta Zolotova describe in this 2019 paper in the *International Journal of Distributed Sensor Networks* a prototype intelligent lighting system that uses a convolutional neural network (CNN) to detect facial expressions from an IP camera, classify the user's emotional state, and automatically adjust the colour of ambient lighting to promote wellbeing. The system integrates three components. First, an IP camera captures video frames. Second, a CNN-based facial expression recognition model — trained on the FER2013 dataset (28,709 training images, 3,589 validation and test images each) — classifies the detected face into one of four emotional categories: angry, happy, sad, or neutral. (Surprise, fear, and disgust were excluded as too transient or too threatening to warrant lighting intervention.) The model architecture uses residual depth-wise separable convolutions with batch normalization, achieving 66% validation accuracy. Third, a decision-making module running on a Raspberry Pi applies colour psychology principles to sel

papers.title: PDF-1057
papers.excerpt: Close your eyes and run your hand along a brick wall. You can feel its roughness, temperature, and the rhythm of mortar joints. Open your eyes and you perceive the same qualities — texture, depth, pattern — through an entirely different sense. Paolo Papale, Leonardo Chiesi, Alessandra Rampinini, Pietro Pietrini, and Emiliano Ricciardi argue in this 2016 perspective article in *Frontiers in Psychology* that the bridge between these two experiences — the supramodal representation of material qualities — is what makes architectural surfaces neurologically meaningful. The paper synthesizes evidence from two converging fields: haptic perception research in cognitive neuroscience and the emerging 'hapticity' concept in architectural theory. In neuroscience, functional neuroimaging studies have established that certain brain regions process object properties (shape, texture, material) regardless of whether the information arrives through touch or vision. These supramodal representations — documented in the lateral occipital complex and intraparietal sulcus — mean the brain encodes 'roughness' or 'warmth' as abstract properties, not as modality-specific sensations. papers.title: PDF-1063
papers.excerpt: Walking into an office with indoor plants, natural wood surfaces, and daylight views lowers your blood pressure by nearly nine points. Jie Yin, Shihao Zhu, Piers MacNaughton, Joseph Allen, and John Spengler at Harvard's T.H. Chan School of Public Health showed in this 2014 study that a biophilic office outperformed both a standard non-biophilic office and a virtual-reality replica of the same biophilic space on every physiological and cognitive measure tested. The built environment affects health through pathways that most people never notice. Previous work linked access to nature with reduced stress, improved mood, and faster recovery from surgery. But most of that work compared outdoor settings or window views. This study asked a more specific question: do indoor biophilic elements, plants, wood, water features, daylight, change measurable physiological states in a controlled office setting? Thirty participants spent time in three office conditions in a within-subjects crossover design: a non-biophilic office (standard corporate furniture, fluorescent light, no plants), a real biophilic office (live plants, natural wood, water feature, daylight access), and a virtual-reality vers

papers.title: PDF-1065
papers.excerpt: Turn up the thermostat three degrees in summer. Do office workers notice? Fan Zhang, Shamila Haddad, Bahareh Nakisa, Mohammad Naim Rastgoo, Christhina Candido, Dian Tjondronegoro, and Richard de Dear at the University of Sydney showed in this 2017 study in Building and Environment that raising the temperature setpoint from 22°C to 25°C did not significantly harm cognitive performance — but did reduce cognitive load. Australian commercial leases mandate indoor temperatures of 22.5 ± 1.5°C in summer. Meeting this requires heavy air conditioning, which consumes energy and money. The question: could a higher setpoint (25°C) save energy without hurting workers? Twenty-six office workers performed cognitive tests (Cambridge Brain Sciences battery, NASA Task Load Index, Paced Auditory Serial Addition Test) at 22°C and 25°C in a controlled climate chamber. EEG and heart rate were recorded during the tasks. CBS test scores showed no significant difference between temperatures. NASA-TLX scores indicated lower perceived cognitive load at 25°C, though this may reflect a learning effect. EEG and heart rate showed no significant temperature-related changes. Thermal comfort was not significantly

papers.title: PDF-1068
papers.excerpt: Dim warm light calms. Bright cool light energizes. But does it change your facial expression? Xian Zhang, Yidan Qiao, Hanyu Wang, Jingluan Wang, and Dengkai Chen at Northwestern Polytechnical University showed in this 2023 study in Science of the Total Environment that lighting settings in enclosed spaces create measurable emotional responses — and that facial expression analysis captures them as reliably as self-report questionnaires. Enclosed spaces (control rooms, submarines, underground facilities) lack natural light and windows. Workers in these settings are at risk for mood disturbance and fatigue. Lighting is the primary environmental lever available to designers. The team tested six lighting conditions combining two color temperatures (3100 K warm, 6500 K cool) with three illuminance levels (250, 600, 1000 lux) in a windowless laboratory. Participants rated their emotions using two subjective methods — Check-All-That-Apply (CATA) and Subjective Coordinate Scale (SCS). Their facial expressions were also recorded and analyzed by FaceReader software. Results converged across methods. Warm light at moderate brightness (3100 K × 600 lux) produced relaxed, pleasant emotions. Cool

papers.title: PDF-1072
papers.excerpt: The Bedouin of the Sahara wear *black* robes in the desert. This seems counterintuitive — black absorbs more solar radiation than white. But as Shkolnik et al. demonstrated, the black robe absorbs two to three times as much radiant heat, which creates enhanced convection beneath the fabric, carrying heat away from the skin before it reaches the body. Antonella Mazzone and Radhika Khosla open this 2021 review in *Energy Research & Social Science* with this example to frame their argument: cooling needs are not purely physiological facts — they are socially constructed, culturally mediated, and materially grounded. Space cooling is expected to become the dominant energy service worldwide by 2050, with demand tripling. The dominant research paradigm frames cooling as a technical problem: building physics, heat-exchange models, and temperature-based comfort standards (ASHRAE 55, EN 15251). Mazzone and Khosla argue this framing is dangerously incomplete. By focusing exclusively on the physics of heat exchange, it ignores the social, cultural, and psychological dimensions that determine what people actually experience as thermal comfort — and how they respond to thermal discomfort. The p

papers.title: PDF-1078
papers.excerpt: Rural English villages are losing their young people. House prices rise beyond local incomes, second-home buyers consume the housing stock, and villages become commuter suburbs or retirement communities. Tom Moore documents in this 2021 *Journal of Rural Studies* paper how a particular form of community response — the community land trust (CLT) — is driven not primarily by the practical need for affordable housing but by something deeper: *place attachment*, the affective bond between people and the physical and social fabric of their community. Using qualitative interviews with founding members of 8 rural CLTs across England, Moore explores why local volunteers — who will never personally benefit from the housing they build — invest years of effort in forming, funding, and managing these organizations. The answer, consistently, is place attachment. Three forms of attachment motivate CLT founders. *Social attachment*: the desire to maintain intergenerational social networks, community bonds, and the "character" or "spirit" of the village. Founders spoke of keeping young families in the village, preserving the pub and the school, and maintaining the social mix that gives a village i

papers.title: PDF-1083
papers.excerpt: Do desk plants make you more productive? Marlon Nieuwenhuis, Craig Knight, Tom Postmes, and S. Alexander Haslam showed in three field experiments, published in this 2014 paper in the Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, that enriching offices with plants boosted productivity, air quality ratings, and workplace satisfaction — and lean offices performed worst. The 'lean office' movement strips workspaces of personal items and decorations to minimize distraction. But the evidence for lean productivity is thin. The authors tested the opposite: does adding plants (a simple, low-cost biophilic intervention) improve work output? Three field experiments ran in large commercial offices in the Netherlands and the UK. In each, sections of open-plan offices were either kept lean or enriched with large green plants visible from every desk. In all three studies, workers in green offices reported higher concentration, better air quality, and greater workplace satisfaction. Objective productivity measures (data entry speed, error rates) improved by 15% in the green condition. The gains persisted over months. papers.title: PDF-1087
papers.excerpt: Smart offices — workplaces embedded with sensors, actuators, and artificial intelligence — could simultaneously boost productivity and improve employee wellbeing, but the empirical evidence is almost entirely absent. Savvas Papagiannidis and Davit Marikyan argue in this 2020 opinion paper in the *International Journal of Information Management* that the research community has focused on smart homes and smart cities while ignoring the distinct type of space where most knowledge workers spend their productive hours: the office. The paper distinguishes smart offices from smart homes and smart manufacturing in several ways. First, offices are *collaborative* spaces where multiple people work simultaneously, creating multi-user optimisation challenges that do not arise in single-family homes. Second, offices require *cognitive* environments — conditions that support sustained attention, creative thinking, and complex decision-making — not merely physical comfort. Third, offices have *organisational* contexts: the relationship between spatial design, technology, and productivity is mediated by management practices, organisational culture, and employment relationships. papers.title: PDF-1088
papers.excerpt: Why do some people feel deeply rooted in their town while others feel like outsiders? Grace Pretty, Heather Chipuer, and Paul Bramston at the University of Southern Queensland surveyed 246 adults and 365 adolescents in two remote rural Australian towns in this 2003 study in the Journal of Environmental Psychology, teasing apart the dimensions that separate stayers from leavers. Sense of place is a composite: attachment (emotional bond), community (belonging and shared identity), and dependence (practical reliance on local resources). The study asked which of these dimensions best predicts whether a resident identifies with the town or wants to leave. For adults, emotional bonding, belonging, and behavioral commitment loaded strongly on a single factor that correctly classified 76% of residents into identifiers versus non-identifiers. For adolescents, dependence and belonging dominated. The age difference matters: young people weigh practicality (what does this town offer me?), while adults weigh sentiment (do I feel at home?). For architects and planners working in declining rural communities, the finding suggests tailored strategies: for adults, preserve the familiar landmarks and

papers.title: Architectural stimulus reshapes physiology — light wavelength, intensity, and timing are direct levers on human function
papers.excerpt: The light in your office is not merely illuminating a page or screen — it is actively altering your heart rate, suppressing or promoting melatonin production, and shifting your body's sense of when night actually is. This 2022 comprehensive review in *Building and Environment* demonstrates that architectural stimuli, particularly light properties, function as direct physiological levers with effects measurable at the level of circadian rhythm, cardiovascular function, and occupational health outcomes. Architects and building systems engineers typically treat light as a visual amenity; the evidence reviewed here treats it as a hormone delivery system. The review's central finding is elegantly simple: light is not a binary (present or absent) but a multidimensional set of inputs — wavelength, intensity, timing, and duration — each with distinct physiological effects. Monochromatic light at 550 nanometers (yellow-green) produced wavelength-dependent suppression of salivary melatonin in controlled exposure studies. Blue-enriched light (6500 Kelvin), commonly installed in office environments for perceived visibility, produced a 19% increase in subjective alertness compared to warm light
papers.authors: Philippe St-Jean, Osborne Grant Clark, Michael Jemtrud

papers.title: PDF-1105
papers.excerpt: Can artificial light affect your stress hormones? G. Tonello, N. Hernández de Borsetti, H. Borsetti, L. Tereschuk, and S. López Zigarán at the Universidad Nacional de Tucumán showed in this 2019 study in Lighting Research and Technology that correlated color temperature at ~4000 K produced the strongest alignment between melatonin, cortisol, and perceived well-being. Office lighting standards specify lux levels but rarely address hormonal effects. Light's spectral composition triggers biological responses — melatonin for sleep, cortisol for alertness — that interact with mood and perceived stress. Fifty-six evaluations were collected in a controlled laboratory where participants experienced two lighting conditions that differed in color temperature and intensity. Melatonin and cortisol were measured alongside subjective mood, somnolence, and visual comfort. At ~4000 K, the spectral blue component aligned cortisol and melatonin responses with self-reported alertness and comfort. Psychological profiles of individuals mediated the strength of the relationship: participants with higher perceived stress showed stronger hormonal responses to light conditions. papers.title: Natural environments reverse workplace stress faster than urban scenes — a design lever for recovery spaces
papers.excerpt: Your building's break room may be limiting recovery from occupational stress in measurable, physiological ways. This 1991 study by Ulrich and colleagues measured the speed at which stressed participants recovered when exposed to either natural environments or urban scenes, demonstrating that a view of trees recovers the human stress response faster than a view of streets and buildings. The mechanism begins with acute stress. Researchers exposed 120 participants to a stressor film showing simulated workplace accidents—graphic scenes of injury and blood designed to activate the body's threat response. The results were predictable and significant: skin conductance increased sharply (p < 0.001), systolic blood pressure rose (measured as shortened pulse transit time, p < 0.001), muscle tension elevated (p < 0.001), and heart rate decelerated in the involuntary attention pattern that marks high cognitive intake. These are not minor fluctuations; they are the full autonomic cascade of stress mobilization—elevated sympathetic activation, parasympathetic withdrawal, and the physiological allocation of resources toward threat appraisal. Then came the critical test: recovery. After the stress

headings: PDF-1107: van Bommel (2006) — Non-Visual Biological Effect of Lighting and the Practical Meaning for Lighting for Work
papers.excerpt: # PDF-1107: van Bommel (2006) — Non-Visual Biological Effect of Lighting and the Practical Meaning for Lighting for Work ## Paper Type Narrative review (Applied Ergonomics, Philips Lighting) ## Core Argument Light affects the human body through two pathways: the familiar visual pathway (rods and cones → visual cortex) and a recently discovered non-visual biological pathway (intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells/ipRGCs → suprachiasmatic nucleus → pineal gland). This second pathway regulates circadian timing, hormone production (melatonin, cortisol), body temperature, alertness, and sleep quality. The practical consequence is that workplace lighting must move beyond illuminance on the task plane to consider the spectral, temporal, and intensity characteristics of light entering the eye for health and productivity. ## Key Evidence Synthesized ### Three Photoreceptor Systems (Section 1) - Rods and cones (visual), plus ipRGCs containing melanopsin (Berson et al., 2002) - Visual system peaks at green-yellow (~555 nm); biological action spectrum peaks in blue (~460 nm) - Figure 2 compares Vλ (photopic sensitivity) with the "biological action curve" — they diverge significant

papers.title: Green roofs cut façade noise from road traffic by diffracting sound over building edges
papers.excerpt: Noise from road traffic is the most pervasive environmental pollutant in urbanized regions — more people are chronically exposed to harmful sound levels from roads than from any other source. Timothy Van Renterghem and Dick Botteldooren, working at Ghent University, asked a deceptively simple question: can a green roof reduce the noise that reaches a building's own façade? The answer, published in 2009, is yes — and the mechanism is not absorption but diffraction. The physics is counterintuitive. A green roof sits on top of a building, far from the road. How can vegetation overhead reduce noise hitting a vertical wall? The answer lies in the geometry of sound propagation in urban canyons. When sound from a road source travels toward a building façade on the opposite side of a street, much of the energy reaches the façade not directly but via diffraction over the roof edge of the source-side building. The sound bends around the roofline. A substrate of soil and vegetation on that roofline changes the diffraction conditions — the porous, irregular surface scatters and absorbs high-frequency sound at exactly the point where diffraction is most efficient. papers.title: PDF-1110
papers.excerpt: Double the fresh air in an office. Do workers notice? Pawel Wargocki, David Wyon, Jan Sundell, Geo Clausen, and P. Ole Fanger at the Technical University of Denmark showed in this 2000 study in Indoor Air that they do: increasing outdoor air supply from 3 to 10 L/s per person reduced sick building syndrome symptoms, improved perceived air quality, and boosted office task performance by about 1.7% per twofold increase in ventilation rate. Building codes set minimum ventilation rates, but most offices hover near those minima to save energy. Wargocki's team asked what happens when you push well above the minimum. Thirty women worked 4.6-hour sessions in a furnished 108-m³ office at three outdoor air rates (3, 10, and 30 L/s per person; about 0.6, 2, and 6 air changes per hour). Temperature, humidity, and other variables stayed fixed. Workers did not know the ventilation rate was changing. Higher ventilation reduced odor intensity, increased perceived air freshness, relieved dry eyes and throat, and improved performance on four simulated office tasks (text typing, logical thinking, proofreading, calculation). For HVAC engineers and building owners, the study provides a cost-benefit lev

papers.title: PDF-1117
papers.excerpt: Why do people get lost when a building's hallways shift direction? Steffen Werner and Laura Schindler at the University of Idaho showed in this 2004 study in Environment and Behavior that misaligned spatial reference frames — sections of a building rotated relative to each other — impair wayfinding and corrupt spatial memory. Architects frequently rotate wings, offset corridors, or angle connections between parts of a building. These design choices break the geometric alignment that the brain uses to stitch together a coherent mental map. Fifty-six participants navigated four virtual buildings that varied systematically in how aligned their sections were. When building sections shared a common axis, participants navigated faster and pointed more accurately to unseen locations. When sections were misaligned (rotated relative to each other), both wayfinding speed and spatial memory declined. The effect was independent of familiarity and path complexity. For architects, the finding has direct design implications: keep building wings aligned to a shared grid. If rotation is necessary — for views, site constraints, or aesthetics — provide strong visual cues (landmarks, sightlines, atria

papers.title: PDF-1118: Wood, Rea, Plitnick & Figueiro (2013) — Light Level and Duration of Exposure Determine the Impact of Self-Luminous Tablets on Melatonin Suppression
headings: PDF-1118: Wood, Rea, Plitnick & Figueiro (2013) — Light Level and Duration of Exposure Determine the Impact of Self-Luminous Tablets on Melatonin Suppression

papers.title: Soundscape Evaluation and ANN Modelling in Urban Open Spaces
papers.excerpt: The sound you don't notice might be making your space worse. This mixed-methods study across 19 urban open spaces examines how sound pressure levels and the varied acoustic characteristics of different sites shape how people experience and prefer those environments—not through discomfort complaints, but through systematic measurement of their actual subjective evaluations. Urban open spaces are typically designed around visual and functional criteria: sightlines, seating, shelter, connectivity to adjacent uses. Acoustics—the sound environment that people encounter the moment they enter—receives far less attention from architects and landscape designers, despite evidence that soundscape quality is a first-order driver of place preference. This study operationalizes that relationship by coupling acoustic measurement (objective sound pressure levels at different frequencies and times) with psychometric evaluation (how people actually rate what they hear), then using artificial neural network modeling to identify which acoustic features predict subjective response. The core finding is straightforward: across all seven case study sites, the correlation between measured sound pressure le

papers.title: PDF-1138
papers.excerpt: Close your eyes and walk through a building. What do you notice? The echo of your footsteps, the texture of the handrail, the warmth of sunlight on your skin. Dina Bakir, Yasser Mansour, Shaimaa Kamel, Yasser Moustafa, and Marwa Khalil at Ain Shams University showed in this 2022 study in Civil Engineering and Architecture that visually impaired and blind people extract rich spatial knowledge from exactly these non-visual cues. Architecture is designed for the eye. Floor plans, elevations, and renders assume that space is understood through sight. But one in every 200 people worldwide has severe visual loss. How do they experience buildings? The researchers used a phenomenological approach. They analyzed the autobiography of Taha Hussein (a blind Egyptian writer) and conducted guided walking tours with visually impaired participants, asking them to describe how they mapped and navigated spaces. Key findings: touch was the dominant channel for close-range spatial knowledge (material texture, handrail shape, floor changes). Sound served as the main distance cue (echo gives room size, traffic noise signals an exit). Thermal gradients marked transitions between indoor and outdoor zones.

papers.title: PDF-1143
papers.excerpt: Ceiling fans keep classrooms cool. They also drown out the teacher. Nikhil Bhandari, Srinivas Tadepalli, and Padmanaban Gopalakrishnan at the National Institute of Technology Tiruchirappalli showed in this 2024 study in Building and Environment that background noise from ceiling fans degrades acoustic comfort, student engagement, and self-reported productivity in naturally ventilated Indian classrooms. In warm-humid climates, ceiling fans are the primary cooling strategy in schools. But fans at high speed generate noise levels that compete with the teacher's voice. How much damage does this noise do? The team measured sound pressure levels (SPL), background noise (BGN), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and reverberation time (RT) in 11 naturally ventilated classrooms over four months, collecting 828 student survey responses. BGN ranged from 58 to 65 dBA. Students with high noise sensitivity reported acoustic discomfort when BGN exceeded 62 dBA, RT exceeded 1.7 seconds, and SNR fell below 3.7 dBA. Acoustic comfort, engagement, and productivity all dropped as BGN rose. For school architects in warm climates, the study sets practical limits: keep BGN below 61 dBA, RT below 0.7 seconds, an

papers.title: Sound in museums: From enforced silence to designed acoustic experience
papers.excerpt: Museums have traditionally maintained a cultural policy of silence — hushing visitors, policing conversation, and treating sound as noise that impedes the functional forms of cultural appreciation they were designed to foster. A reverberant gallery where footsteps echo and whispers reverberate is not a failure of acoustic design but a deliberate (if often unreflective) expression of institutional authority: the museum demands quiet contemplation, and the architecture enforces it through hard surfaces that amplify any violation of the silence norm. This paper argues that the relationship between museums and sound deserves a fundamental reorientation — from suppression to design. The silence policy reflects an assumption about how cultural experience works: that visual attention to art requires acoustic emptiness, that cognitive processing of exhibition content benefits from the absence of auditory stimulation, and that the museum's cultural mission is best served by the sensory deprivation of all channels except vision. This assumption is challenged by the emerging evidence on multisensory integration: human cognition does not process visual and auditory information in separate chan

papers.title: PDF-1149
papers.excerpt: Hospitals are too loud — and the noise keeps rising. Ilene Busch-Vishniac, James West, Colin Barnhill, Tyrone Hunter, Douglas Orellana, and Ram Chivukula measured noise levels across Johns Hopkins Hospital in this 2005 study in the Journal of the Acoustical Society of America and found that every area exceeded recommended guidelines. The World Health Organization recommends background noise below 35 dB(A) in hospital patient rooms. Busch-Vishniac's team placed calibrated monitors throughout the hospital — in patient rooms, hallways, operating rooms, and the emergency department. Average daytime levels in patient rooms reached 52-57 dB(A), roughly 20 dB above WHO guidelines. Nighttime levels dropped only slightly. Peak levels regularly exceeded 80 dB(A). The team also analyzed historical data from other hospitals and found a consistent upward trend: hospital noise levels have risen by an average of 0.38 dB per year since 1960, driven by more equipment, more alarms, and harder surfaces. For hospital architects and facility managers, the data make a stark case: current hospital design produces noise environments that impair sleep, slow recovery, and stress both patients and staff. Abs

papers.title: PDF-1154
papers.excerpt: Can a wall covered in living plants reduce urban noise? Lan-Ting Chang and Feng-Cheng Chang measured the sound absorption performance of Living Wall Systems (LWS) with acoustic Support Systems (SS) in this 2022 study in *Building and Environment* — providing the first systematic investigation of how the design parameters of green walls interact with acoustic support structures to affect noise reduction. The study used wood-plastic composites (WPC) made from *Jacobaea maritima* and recycled plastic as the substrate material for the LWS panels, combined with various acoustic support systems. Sound absorption coefficients (αs) were measured in a reverberation room following standard protocols, testing multiple configurations that varied in: LWS panel design (weight, perforated board design, surface pattern), SS structure (additional panel absorbers, air gaps), plant variety, planting density, substrate depth, and greenery coverage percentage. Key findings on design parameters: (1) *Weight* emerged as the most important factor determining the sound absorption capacity of LWS panels — heavier panels absorbed more sound, suggesting that substrate mass is the primary absorptive mechanism.

papers.title: Your brain judges rooms on three dimensions — beauty, coziness, and fascination — using different neural circuits
papers.excerpt: When you look at a room, your brain makes three distinct judgments — and each fires in a different neural circuit. Alexander Coburn, Oshin Vartanian, Yoed Kenett, Marcos Nadal, and Anjan Chatterjee demonstrated this in a 2020 study published in *Cortex*, combining behavioral ratings with fMRI to map the neural architecture of how people respond to interior spaces. Participants viewed images of real architectural interiors — not stylized renderings but photographs of actual rooms — and rated them along multiple dimensions. Factor analysis revealed three primary dimensions of response: beauty (aesthetic pleasure), coziness (warmth, comfort, homeliness), and fascination (interest, engagement, desire to explore). These three factors accounted for the majority of variance in how people evaluated architectural spaces. The critical finding was neural dissociation. Beauty judgments activated the ventral striatum and medial orbitofrontal cortex — the brain's reward circuitry, the same regions that respond to beautiful faces, music, and art. Coziness activated regions associated with self-referential processing, autobiographical memory, and social warmth — the default mode network, including
papers.authors: Coburn, Vartanian, Kenett, Nadal, Hartung, Hayn-Leichsenring, Navarrete, Gonzalez-Mora, Chatterjee

papers.title: PDF-1172
papers.excerpt: Does a fake wood floor fool anyone? Yaqi Zhang, Yao Song, and Jing Luo at Hong Kong Polytechnic University and Shenzhen University showed in this 2023 study in Buildings that people can tell natural from imitation textures at a glance, and that perceived naturalness drives both aesthetic preference and judgments of environmental friendliness. The construction industry increasingly uses synthetic materials that mimic natural surfaces — vinyl that looks like wood, porcelain that looks like stone. Manufacturers assume that realistic imitation will satisfy users. But does it? Zhang, Song, and Luo tested whether visual appearance alone is enough or whether something subtler — perceived authenticity — matters more. They recruited 112 participants (professional architects and non-professionals) and showed them sustainable and non-sustainable building materials with natural or artificial surface textures. Participants rated each material on naturalness, aesthetics, and eco-friendliness on 7-point scales. Materials with natural-looking surface textures received higher ratings on all three scales. Critically, non-professionals were nearly as accurate as professionals at distinguishing authen

papers.title: PDF-1173
papers.excerpt: Can biophilic design make workplaces work for neurodiverse employees? James Hutson and Piper Hutson at Lindenwood University argued in this 2023 perspective in Global Health Economics and Sustainability that integrating nature elements, sensory regulation, and AI-supported environmental controls can create offices that serve employees with ADHD, autism, and learning disabilities. An estimated 15-20% of the global population is neurodiverse. Standard open-plan offices overwhelm many of these individuals with noise, visual clutter, and unpredictable sensory inputs. Biophilic design — plants, natural light, organic forms, water features — reduces amygdala activity and promotes stress recovery. The authors proposed combining three strategies: biophilic design for baseline stress reduction, extended reality (XR) for personalized onboarding and workspace previews, and AI-driven environmental controls that adapt lighting, sound, and temperature to individual needs. The vision is a workplace that detects and responds to occupant stress, adjusting the sensory environment in real time. For workplace architects and HR leaders, the paper argues: neuroinclusive design is not accommodation — it

papers.title: PDF-1180
papers.excerpt: Can hearing water help your body recover from urban stress? Jin Yong Jeon, Hyun In Jo, and Kounseok Lee at Hanyang University showed in this 2023 study in Sustainable Cities and Society that specific audio-visual combinations in natural environments drive psycho-physiological restoration — and that the sound matters as much as the view. Urban environments stress residents through noise, crowding, and visual clutter. Nature exposure reverses this. But which elements of nature — what you see, what you hear, or how the two combine — do the restorative work? Sixty participants evaluated nine environments (three each of urban, waterfront, and green settings) delivered through VR with spatial audio. The team measured perceived restoration (psychological) and autonomic responses (physiological). Waterfront and green environments produced the strongest restoration, and audio-visual congruence amplified the effect. Ten prediction models built from audio-visual design elements achieved correlations up to 0.80, explaining 22% of variance in urban psycho-physiological responses. For urban designers, the study argues: design soundscapes alongside landscapes. A park with traffic noise will not r

papers.title: PDF-1182
papers.excerpt: What people do in a park shapes what they hear — and what they hear shapes whether they stay. Hyun In Jo and Jin Yong Jeon at Hanyang University showed in this 2020 study in Landscape and Urban Planning that human behavior in urban parks (social interaction, transit, lingering) drives soundscape quality as much as physical acoustics. Soundscape research typically measures sound pressure levels and spectral content. Jo and Jeon asked: how do the behaviors of park users shape the perceived quality of the soundscape? Using VR with 360° visuals and 3D audio recorded in three Parisian parks, participants evaluated each environment on audio-visual indicators, emotional qualities, and overall soundscape quality. The researchers coded the human behavioral characteristics visible in each scene. Sounds from other people decreased perceived tranquility and peacefulness, while scenes perceived as dynamic and lively received higher overall quality ratings. The primary driver of park preference was not the type of activity but the presence or absence of other people. For park designers and urban planners, the study shows: manage human activity patterns, not just physical acoustic conditions. A q

papers.title: PDF-1183
papers.excerpt: Walk through a city square and listen. Traffic hums, a fountain splashes, pigeons coo, a busker plays guitar. What words best capture how this soundscape feels? Jian Kang and Mei Zhang at the University of Sheffield showed in this 2010 study in Building and Environment that semantic differential analysis can extract four stable factors that describe how people perceive urban soundscapes: relaxation, communication, spatiality, and dynamics. Most noise research measures decibels and treats all sound as unwanted. Soundscape research takes a different view: it asks how people interpret the full sonic environment, including sounds they enjoy. But the field lacked a standardized vocabulary for describing urban soundscapes. Kang and Zhang ran three stages. First, 48 students took a soundscape walk through four urban open spaces in Sheffield and described what they heard using free-form language. Second, 491 members of the public rated the soundscapes at two of the sites on semantic differential scales. Third, 223 architectural design students rated the same sites. Factor analysis of the ratings consistently extracted four factors across all groups. Relaxation captured whether the soundsca

papers.title: PDF-1185
papers.excerpt: Pick up a leather wallet. It feels heavy, warm, slightly rough. Ask someone what it means, and they might say "nostalgic" or "professional." Now pick up a smooth, glossy plastic phone case — and the word "sexy" comes to mind. Elvin Karana, Paul Hekkert, and Prabhu Kandachar wanted to know exactly which sensorial properties of materials drive these meaning attributions. Their 2009 study in *Materials and Design* asked 25 participants — 7 PhD researchers and 18 students from Delft University of Technology's Faculty of Industrial Design Engineering — to select products expressing five given meanings: aggressive, nostalgic, professional, sexy, and toy-like. Participants selected one product per meaning, focusing specifically on the material of the product. In total, 125 products were collected and analyzed. For each product, participants (1) verbally described the material through interviews, and (2) rated the material's sensorial properties and manufacturing processes using five-point scales with visual representations. The property list comprised 13 sensorial properties — colour intensity, colourfulness, ductility, elasticity, glossiness/matteness, hardness/softness, odour, reflectiv

papers.title: PDF-1188
papers.excerpt: Does a window change how warm you feel? Won Hee Ko, Stefano Schiavon, Hui Zhang, Lindsay Graham, Gail Brager, Iris Mauss, and Yu-Wen Lin at UC Berkeley showed in this 2020 study in Building and Environment that it does: a window with a nature view made a slightly warm room feel 0.3 thermal sensation votes cooler and boosted positive emotions — without changing the actual air temperature. Window access is a luxury in modern offices. But building codes and LEED points increasingly reward it. Does this make physiological sense, or is it just aesthetic preference? Eighty-six participants worked in a climate-controlled laboratory set to 28°C (slightly warm) with and without a window providing a nature view, in a randomized crossover design. The room with the window produced cooler thermal sensations, higher thermal comfort, and 12% more 'comfortable' votes. Positive emotions (happy, satisfied) rose; negative emotions (sad, drowsy) fell. Working memory and focus improved, though short-term memory, planning, and creativity did not differ between conditions. For architects, the result provides a concrete design lever: a window with a view can compensate for a 0.74°C increase in air tempera

papers.title: Psychological and physiological responses to soundscapes: A systematic review
papers.excerpt: Do soundscapes affect your body or just your mood? That distinction matters enormously for design: if natural sounds only change self-reported feelings, their value is subjective and dispensable. If they change heart rate, cortisol, and brain wave patterns, their value is physiological and non-negotiable. Pei-Rou Kong and Ke-Tsung Han at National Chin-Yi University of Technology conducted a systematic review in this 2024 paper in Science of the Total Environment, analyzing 25 experiments with 1,950 total participants that measured both psychological and physiological responses to soundscapes. Their answer: the two types of evidence converge — but the physiological evidence base has serious methodological problems. The review's central finding is that audio-visual stimuli produced more positive psychological and physiological responses than auditory or visual stimuli presented alone. When participants heard natural sounds paired with nature imagery, stress recovery was greater and mood improvement was larger than when either channel was delivered in isolation. This superadditive audio-visual effect appeared consistently across studies using different sound types, visual stimuli, and

papers.title: PDF-1195
papers.excerpt: Forty years of research on place attachment — and the field still emphasizes the wrong component. Maria Lewicka at the University of Warsaw argued in this 2011 review in the Journal of Environmental Psychology that the 'Person' component has dominated research while the 'Place' and 'Process' components remain underexplored. Place attachment — the emotional bond between people and meaningful locations — has generated hundreds of empirical and theoretical papers since the 1970s. Lewicka reviewed this entire body of work, organizing it around a tripartite model: Person (who attaches), Place (what they attach to), and Process (how the bond forms). The review found that most studies focus on individual differences: age, length of residence, home ownership, social ties. Far fewer investigate what physical qualities of a place foster attachment, or how the psychological process of bonding actually works. Lewicka called for stronger attention to environmental aesthetics, phenomenology from geography, and the concept of place as a meaning-making process. For architects and urban planners, the review argues: if you want to create places people bond with, study the places — their physical for

papers.title: PDF-1202
papers.excerpt: A quiet classroom helps students learn. But how quiet is quiet enough? Chao Liu, Qiuyun Zang, Jiaxin Li, Xue Pan, Haoru Dai, and Weijun Gao at Qingdao University of Technology reviewed 67 studies in this 2023 review in the Journal of Building Engineering, mapping how sound sources, pressure levels, and reverberation time affect learning. Students spend most of their waking hours in classrooms and study rooms. Acoustic quality in these spaces shapes speech clarity, cognitive load, concentration, and even emotional well-being. Yet acoustic design is often an afterthought in school construction. The review covered studies from school and home-based learning environments since 2000 and classified findings by sound type (traffic, speech, music, mechanical noise), sound level, and reverberation. Key findings: non-noise backgrounds (silence, some natural sounds) had no negative effect. Speech noise was the most damaging, especially for tasks requiring verbal processing. High SPL consistently paired with lower learning scores. Studies on reverberation time often overlooked the interaction between RT and background noise type. For school architects, the review recommends: position classroom

papers.title: Daylight's invisible clock: How melanopic intensity reshapes our physiology
papers.excerpt: You know daylight through your eyes. But your body knows it through a different sensor entirely — one that has nothing to do with vision. This 2024 study examined how nonvisual pathways of daylight — specifically the melanopic intensity that triggers our circadian system — reshape heart rate, autonomic balance, and stress physiology in building occupants, with implications that current lighting standards dangerously underestimate. The problem is architectural blind spot. Lighting standards for buildings have historically been optimized for *visual* function: illumination levels, color rendering, glare control. These standards arose from photopic measures of light intensity that weight wavelengths according to what the human eye sees. But the human body contains a parallel sensory system that *does not care* what the eye sees. Intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) contain melanopsin — a photopigment maximally sensitive to short-wavelength blue light around 460–480 nanometers. These cells project directly to the suprachiasmatic nucleus, the circadian clock, and the autonomic nervous system. They mediate no conscious perception of light. Yet they regulate sleep,

papers.title: PDF-1208
papers.excerpt: Can virtual reality capture how all of your senses respond to a building at once? Kun Lyu, Arianna Brambilla, Anastasia Globa, and Richard de Dear at the University of Sydney developed a method for doing exactly that in this 2023 methods paper in Automation in Construction. Most lab studies of buildings test one sense at a time — visual comfort, thermal comfort, acoustic comfort. But real buildings deliver all three at once, and the senses interact: a warm room looks warmer, a noisy room feels smaller. Field studies capture these interactions but sacrifice control. The method bridges the gap. Lyu's team built an immersive VR system that combines visual, auditory, and thermal stimuli. Participants wore a headset and headphones while fans, radiant panels, and heat lamps controlled air and radiant temperature. The system let researchers vary all three modalities independently or together, measuring subjective comfort, behavior, skin temperature, and emotional response. A proof-of-concept study in a semi-outdoor context showed that the method could simultaneously capture visual appeal, thermal comfort, and acoustic satisfaction along with their interactions. Participants reported exper

papers.title: Multisensory virtual reality offers architects a new lens for testing how buildings affect occupants
papers.excerpt: How do you test whether a room with sloped ceilings makes people feel more creative? Or whether thermal discomfort in a corner office measurably degrades decision-making? For decades, architects have relied on intuition, post-occupancy surveys, and the occasional lab study. But there's a fundamental problem: most behavioral research happens in sterile, featureless lab rooms. Real buildings are multisensory environments. The hum of HVAC systems, the glare from tall windows, the temperature gradient between sun and shadow — these elements interact in ways that no 2D diagram can capture. A new approach suggests that immersive virtual reality, deployed as a controlled laboratory tool, can bridge this gap. The challenge is what researchers call *ecological validity*. When you study human response to architecture in isolation — testing visual stimuli on a computer monitor, or thermal comfort in a climate chamber — you lose the integrated sensory experience that actually shapes how people respond to buildings. The moment a person enters a space, their brain is processing visual depth cues, thermal sensations, acoustic properties, proprioceptive feedback from spatial scale, and smell all a

papers.title: Fractal light patterns hold human attention steady — and warm color temperatures amplify the effect
papers.excerpt: The walls of your waiting room may be quietly sustaining your attention. A 2018 study in *Journal of Environmental Psychology* reveals that fractal light patterns — the kind found in nature, from tree bark to clouds — capture and hold visual interest when projected onto interior surfaces, with warm color temperatures amplifying the effect on emotional response. This matters because attention is exhaustible. Directed attention, the deliberate focus required for reading, problem-solving, and deep work, depletes over hours. Environments that fail to engage visual attention passively force the brain to work harder; environments that naturally capture attention periodically restore fatigue without conscious effort. The gap between a restorative environment and a fatiguing one is a design decision, not an accident. The study tested 300 participants exposed to projected light patterns varying in fractal complexity and color temperature. Fractal complexity itself — the self-similar mathematical structure that appears throughout nature — produced measurable increases in sustained visual attention compared to non-fractal controls. The effect emerged reliably at p-values in the 0.03-0.04 rang

papers.title: How spatial layout shapes academic collaboration — and why architects should care about network topology
papers.excerpt: The floor plan of a university building contains hidden information about who will collaborate with whom. This study demonstrates that the spatial configuration of academic departments—how hallways branch, where offices cluster, which common spaces exist—measurably predicts changes in collaboration networks and organizational behavior over time. Architects often treat space as a backdrop. Students and faculty occupy offices, attend meetings, and conduct research; the building is assumed to be a neutral container for these activities. But this study, using Space Syntax (a quantitative method for analyzing spatial configuration) integrated with Social Network Analysis, reveals that space is not neutral—it is a structured predictor of social organization. The relationship flows from geometry to behavior: how easily people encounter each other shapes who collaborates, which affects how academic departments reorganize themselves. The mechanism operates at the level of encounter. Space Syntax quantifies how much a given location is topologically "central" within a building's layout. Offices in high-centrality zones—those that many pathways pass through or funnel into—generate more freque

papers.title: Design elements that promote wellbeing — from light and privacy to biophilic integration
papers.excerpt: Your office was not designed with your wellbeing in mind. Most buildings are optimized for cost, occupancy density, and construction simplicity — not for the psychological and physiological health of the people inside them. A 2019 narrative review in the *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health* synthesizes evidence from across environmental psychology, neuroscience, and architectural research to identify fifteen design stimuli that demonstrably improve occupant wellbeing. The central finding is simple: designed environments that support perceived control and boundary regulation — giving occupants agency over their space — produce measurable improvements in stress, mood, focus, and overall psychological wellbeing. The mechanism operates through what environmental psychologists call perceived control. When you cannot control what you see, hear, or experience in your environment, your autonomic nervous system interprets this as a threat. You become vigilant. Your cortisol levels rise. Attention narrows. Cognitive resources divert toward monitoring threats rather than productive thought. Conversely, when a space provides meaningful control — over privacy, lig

papers.title: PDF-1225: Radicchi (2013) — Emotional Geography & Soundscape Studies: Beyond the Cognitive Approach in (Sound)Mapping Urban Spaces
headings: PDF-1225: Radicchi (2013) — Emotional Geography & Soundscape Studies: Beyond the Cognitive Approach in (Sound)Mapping Urban Spaces
papers.excerpt: # PDF-1225: Radicchi (2013) — Emotional Geography & Soundscape Studies: Beyond the Cognitive Approach in (Sound)Mapping Urban Spaces ## Paper Type Theoretical/methodological paper (EAEA-11 Conference, Track 2: Experiential Simulation) ## Core Argument Traditional sound mapping (acoustic cartography) relies on a "cognitive" paradigm that privileges the distant observer's eye—measuring decibel levels, frequency spectra, and noise contours from above. Radicchi argues this approach misses the embodied, emotional, and culturally specific dimensions of how people actually experience sound in cities. Drawing on her doctoral research in "Sensuous Urbanism" (Radicchi, 2013), she proposes the "tender soundmap" of Florence as an alternative: a qualitative, emotionally-grounded tool that maps not just what sounds exist in a place but how they feel to those who inhabit it. ## Key Concepts ### Emotional Geography (after Bruno, 2002) - Origins in the 1654 "Carte du pays de Tendre" by Madeleine de Scudéry — a map of emotions rather than topography, tracing a landscape of "tenderness" through sites like the "curve of tenderness," the "road of confidence," the "mountain of diligence" - This traditio

papers.title: PDF-1233: Namazian & Mehdipour (2013) — Psychological Demands of the Built Environment: Privacy, Personal Space and Territory in Architecture
headings: PDF-1233: Namazian & Mehdipour (2013) — Psychological Demands of the Built Environment: Privacy, Personal Space and Territory in Architecture
papers.excerpt: # PDF-1233: Namazian & Mehdipour (2013) — Psychological Demands of the Built Environment: Privacy, Personal Space and Territory in Architecture ## Paper Type Theoretical review (narrative synthesis of environmental psychology concepts for architectural practice) ## Core Argument Privacy, personal space, territory, and crowding are four interrelated psychological constructs that architects must understand and translate into physical design. The authors argue that these are not static needs but dialectic, dynamic processes—people constantly shift between wanting contact and wanting separation. Successful environments must therefore be responsive and flexible, allowing occupants to regulate their own level of social interaction. ## Key Concepts ### Privacy (Section 2) - Privacy is a self/other boundary regulation process—not simply "being alone" but achieving a desired level of contact (Davis & Palladino, 1997) - Dialectic quality: isolation (too much separation) and crowding (too much contact) are both undesirable extremes - **Design principle**: Spaces should be flexible enough to shift between states of togetherness and separateness. papers.title: PDF-1238
papers.excerpt: Nature never wastes a material. Termite mounds ventilate without fans. Lotus leaves repel water without coatings. Abeer Makram at Delta University for Science and Technology argued in this 2019 paper in Architecture Research that sustainable architectural design should learn from these biological systems through two complementary strategies: biomimetic design, which copies nature's engineering, and biophilic design, which integrates nature into buildings to benefit human well-being. Biomimetic design treats nature as a source of structural and material solutions. Termite-mound ventilation inspired the Eastgate Centre in Harare, which maintains comfortable temperatures without air conditioning. Lotus-leaf micro-textures inspired self-cleaning building surfaces. Sharkskin structures inspired drag-reducing facade materials. Makram catalogs these examples and argues that architects should systematically scan biological organisms for design principles rather than inventing solutions from scratch. Biophilic design treats nature as a direct presence in occupied spaces. Green walls, indoor planting, water features, daylight access, and natural materials all bring elements of nature into th

papers.title: Natural light in residential spaces improves mental health — and window design is the architectural lever
papers.excerpt: The bedroom window of an active duty soldier may be more important to their mental health than their medication. This study, surveying military personnel and veterans, found a measurable positive relationship between natural light access in residential spaces and psychological well-being—a finding that reframes window placement from an aesthetic choice into a clinical intervention. Mental health prevalence in military populations exceeds civilian rates by 40–60%. Existing interventions focus on counseling, pharmacology, and peer support. But the built environment has been largely invisible in military medicine. This paper asks whether residential daylighting—the quantity and quality of natural light reaching occupied rooms—operates as an uncontrolled variable that either amplifies or mitigates the mental health burden that service members carry home. The study measured two primary findings across populations of active duty and veteran respondents. First, self-reported natural light rating in residential spaces showed a positive association with general health and emotional well-being in 439 surveyed individuals. As reported light exposure increased, so did self-assessed health and
papers.authors: Nathanael T. Kohl, Andrew J. Hoisington
