papers.title: PDF-1268
papers.excerpt: Home is not just a roof and four walls. It is a bond between a person and a place. Navid Khaleghimoghaddam, Sara Arzhangi, and Narges Rajaeipour showed in this 2023 study in PLANARCH that social life drives place attachment more strongly than built form does — though both play a role. Place attachment theory asks why people feel tied to certain spots. The question matters for architects because buildings and streets form the physical stage on which attachment grows. But which matters more — the quality of the built fabric or the quality of social life it supports? The team surveyed 180 residents across four neighborhoods in Karaj, Iran. They measured place attachment, physical traits (building quality, maintenance, street layout), and social factors (neighbor contact, access to local shops, community events). Social factors — above all, frequent neighbor contact and easy access to amenities — raised attachment scores directly. Physical factors worked through an indirect path: tidy streets and well-kept buildings boosted social life, which then raised attachment. For urban designers, the finding reframes the brief. papers.title: Place attachment is not decoration — it's a psychological system rooted in survival and security
papers.excerpt: The spaces we inhabit are not neutral containers for activity. A comprehensive review of place attachment theory reveals that our emotional bonds to specific locations operate as a psychological system as fundamental as the attachment relationships we form with people — and the mechanisms are rooted in evolutionary survival needs, not aesthetic preference. Place attachment describes a consistent, affective bond between individuals and specific environments. When you feel "at home" in a place, you are not experiencing a cosmetic reaction to decor. You are exhibiting an evolved behavioral system that has roots in your early experiences with caregiving and security, and that manifests across emotional, cognitive, and social dimensions simultaneously. The attachment begins in infancy. Attachment theory, developed from observational research on parent-child relationships, reveals that children develop internal working models — cognitive schemas of how relationships function, what to expect from others, and how to regulate their own emotions. These models are not confined to people. They extend to places and objects. A child whose caregiver consistently provided safety and stability in a

papers.title: Maps and Architecture Work Together—Why Signage Alone Cannot Fix a Confusing Building
papers.excerpt: You are lost in a hospital. The signs are clear—arrows point left, room numbers are legible, directions say "Surgery, Third Floor"—yet you still backtrack. You still miss the turn. You still walk farther than necessary. This is the central finding of a new review of wayfinding research: the built environment itself, not just the information overlaid on it, determines whether you find your way or wander. The problem architects and facility managers face is deceptively simple. A person navigating an unfamiliar building relies on two systems: the environmental architecture (which visual landmarks are visible, how many decision points exist, whether the floor plan is regular and legible) and the information system (signs, maps, numbering, directional cues). The review demonstrates that these two systems interact, and that signage alone—no matter how clear—cannot rescue a building with poor spatial legibility. The evidence supports this through a progression of findings. Visual differentiation matters: areas that look distinct from one another aid wayfinding. Interior and exterior views matter: people navigate more efficiently when they can see landmarks and reference points. Regular fl
papers.authors: Cheng, Karen; Pérez-Kriz, Sarah; Walton, Ashley; Fontaine, Lisa; Cuée, Patricia; Schwanbeck, Andrew T.

papers.title: From sketch to simulation: How computational tools and virtual reality are reshaping architectural design
papers.excerpt: The hand-to-brain connection that defines traditional architectural sketching has dominated design practice for centuries. Yet in the past two decades, computational tools have begun competing with—and sometimes replacing—the pencil as architects' primary design medium, raising a question architects have grappled with: Does this shift enhance or diminish design thinking? This 2019 narrative review synthesizes research on how computer-aided design (CAD), digital modeling, and virtual reality (VR) are fundamentally altering architectural education and professional practice. Unlike traditional media that privilege intuitive, embodied design exploration, computational tools enforce precision, control, and measurable efficiency—shifting the cognitive demands on designers in ways that remain poorly understood. The evidence suggests a genuine tradeoff. Computational design enables what the literature calls "semantic fluency"—the ability to rapidly generate, iterate, and evaluate design options through simulation. CAD and modeling software provide architects with quantifiable feedback on structural efficiency, spatial relationships, and construction feasibility that manual sketching cannot

papers.title: Why children in care homes need their own territory — and what architects can do about it
papers.excerpt: A child in a residential care facility stands in a hallway, uncertain which corner is theirs. This architectural gap—the absence of clearly defined personal territory—may be costing them more than comfort. It may be costing them a sense of belonging. A narrative review of the environmental psychology literature reveals a simple but overlooked fact: territory matters more to child development than most institutional design standards acknowledge. Not territory as luxury, but as a fundamental psychological need woven into how children construct identity, regulate their social relationships, and experience safety in spaces where they lack parental bonds. Territory begins with what environmental psychologists call *primary territory*—a place a person controls and identifies with as their own. For children living with parents, this is typically the bedroom: a space they personalize, retreat into, defend from siblings. For the estimated 3.5 million children in formal care globally, many facilities treat the bedroom as interchangeable functional space, not as a child's primary territory. The institutional logic is efficient: rooms are standardized, cleared of personal objects, maintained i

papers.title: Acoustic chaos in open offices — speech intelligibility is the enemy, not just decibels
papers.excerpt: The hum of an open office masks more than we think. This review of acoustic design in office buildings isolates a counterintuitive finding: the decibel level matters less than what occupants *understand* at those decibels. Intelligible speech — coworkers' recognizable words and conversations — emerges as the dominant source of distraction, not just background noise, and this distinction reshapes how architects should approach acoustic control. The problem is increasingly visible. Surveys of 237 workers in open-plan offices reported noise levels exceeding acceptable thresholds, with widespread distraction during focused work. But the mechanism is not mechanical. Elevated noise causes stress, impairs attention, erodes job satisfaction, and creates measurable productivity loss. This is Mehrabian's environment-behavior principle in action: the sensory environment shapes psychological state, which determines performance. What makes the mechanism treacherous is auditory masking — the process by which one sound obscures others. In offices, this is often inverted: background machinery provides steady masking, which is tolerable. But when intelligible speech overlays that baseline, the cogn

papers.title: Smart Materials Are Making Virtual Touch Feel Real — And Designers Need to Know Why
papers.excerpt: The next generation of virtual and augmented reality relies on a technical problem most people don't notice until it's absent: how to make digital objects feel like they're actually there when you touch them. For decades, the answer was brute force — bulky electromechanical motors that could vibrate your hand or provide resistance. But this approach carries a fundamental architectural flaw: the systems that generate haptic feedback are too stiff, too heavy, and too conspicuous for seamless wearable use. A 2021 review in *Advanced Functional Materials* catalogs an emerging alternative that changes the design equation entirely: active materials — substances that generate motion or force in response to electrical, thermal, or magnetic stimuli — are replacing mechanical actuators, enabling haptic systems lightweight enough to wear imperceptibly. The shift from motors to smart materials solves a cascading set of design constraints. Traditional electromechanical actuators excel at generating force but fail on three dimensions simultaneously: they require rigid mechanical housings, they're heavy relative to the feedback they produce, and they resist integration into flexible surfaces like
papers.authors: Tae-Heon Yang, Jin Ryong Kim, Hanbit Jin, Hyunjae Gil, Jeong-Hoi Koo, Hye Jin Kim

papers.title: PDF-1303
papers.excerpt: Trees line the sidewalk. But what about flowers? Assenna Todorova, Shoichiro Asakawa, and Tetsuya Aikoh at Hokkaido University showed in this 2004 study in Landscape and Urban Planning that street flowers are the most preferred element of urban vegetation — more than trees alone — but only when planted in low, ordered compositions beneath tree canopies. Street trees have been studied for decades. Flowers have been largely ignored in urban design research, despite being a common and beloved planting element. Todorova's team asked which planting models residents actually prefer. Eighty-one residents of Sapporo, Japan, rated 59 photomontage simulations showing streets with different vegetation combinations: trees alone, flowers alone, and various tree-plus-flower compositions. They also answered a questionnaire about attitudes toward street flowers. Results: flowers beneath trees were the most preferred combination. Low, brightly colored, ordered flower beds scored highest. Tall flowers were not preferred — they were seen as neither attractive nor appropriate. Factor analysis revealed five attitude clusters: aesthetic and psychological benefits, natural-environmental value, practical

papers.title: PDF-1306
papers.excerpt: Do people really prefer environments that are both coherent and mysterious? Arthur Stamps III at the Institute of Environmental Quality tested Rachel and Stephen Kaplan's informational model in this 2004 meta-analysis in the Journal of Environmental Psychology, covering 61 articles, 6,288 participants, and 3,125 scenes. The Kaplans proposed that four variables predict whether people prefer an environment: coherence (immediate understanding), complexity (immediate exploration), legibility (inferred understanding), and mystery (inferred exploration). The theory has guided decades of design research. Stamps found that the correlations between these variables and preference were real but weaker than commonly assumed. There was considerable variation across studies. Studies using expert panels to rate coherence and complexity produced smaller effects than those using the general public. Natural environments showed different patterns from built ones, and results were not reliably reproducible. For designers, the meta-analysis offers a caution: the informational model works, but its predictions are modest and context-dependent. Do not assume that a coherent building will always be preferr

papers.title: PDF-1309
papers.excerpt: Walk down Orchard Road in Singapore during rush hour. Thousands of pedestrians stream past you. The buildings are beautiful — glossy facades, curated shopfronts, carefully designed public spaces. But the aesthetic qualities of the architecture are inseparable from the social density of the crowd. Panagiotis Mavros, Agnieszka Olszewska-Guizzo, and Dominique Makowski argue in this 2023 book chapter that urban design research has failed to disentangle these two variables. The chapter, published in *Location, Location, Location*, reviews the intersection of environmental neuroscience, neuroaesthetics, and social psychology as applied to urban space. The core problem: most research on how urban environments affect well-being confounds the physical design of a space with the social conditions within it. A park may feel restorative not because of its greenery but because it is empty; a high street may feel stressful not because of bad design but because it is crowded. The authors review three established theories of environmental restoration. The Biophilia Hypothesis (Kellert & Wilson 1993) posits innate human affiliation with natural elements and fractal patterns. Attention Restoration T

papers.title: PDF-1311
papers.excerpt: A window is not merely a hole in a wall — it is an optical instrument. Martin Kischkoweit-Lopin's 2002 overview in *Solar Energy* catalogues the full landscape of daylighting systems, dividing them into a systematic matrix that building practitioners have needed for decades. The matrix classifies systems along two axes: the physical principle (shading, optical without shading, or scattering) and the system's capabilities (glare protection, view outside, light guiding, homogeneous illumination, energy savings potential, tracking requirements, and market availability). Shading systems that use diffuse skylight include prismatic panels, prisms and venetian blinds, sun-protecting mirror elements, anidolic zenithal openings, directional selective shading with holographic optical elements (HOEs), and transparent shading with HOEs on total reflection. Those using direct sunlight include light guiding shades, louvers and blinds, lightshelves, and glazing with reflecting profiles (Okasolar). Optical systems without shading fall into three subcategories. Diffuse light guiding systems — lightshelves, anidolic integrated systems, anidolic ceilings, fish systems, and zenith light guiding with H

papers.title: PDF-1314
papers.excerpt: Why do people flock to the busiest part of a city and avoid the quiet block around the corner? Colin Ellard at the University of Waterloo argued in this 2020 review in Psychological Research on Urban Society that the answer lies in a deep neural preference for vitality — scenes that combine complexity, movement, and social activity. Urban designers have long debated whether busy streets are good or stressful. Jane Jacobs championed the lively sidewalk; others warned of overstimulation. Ellard synthesized neuroscience evidence to settle the debate on Jacobs's side — with caveats. The review drew on EEG, skin conductance, and behavioral studies conducted during urban walks. Results converged: people showed higher physiological arousal and more positive affect near active storefronts, mixed-use facades, and streets with varied architectural detail. They showed lower arousal and more negative affect near blank walls, parking garages, and monotonous facades. The sweet spot was moderate complexity — enough visual variety to engage attention without overwhelming it. Ellard linked these responses to predictive processing: the brain continuously forecasts sensory input, and scenes that prov

papers.title: The neuroscience of architecture is ready to move from description to experiment
papers.excerpt: People in developed countries spend over 90% of their time inside buildings. Yet we know almost nothing about how those buildings shape our mental states and brain activity. Anjan Chatterjee, Alex Coburn, and Adam Weinberger argue in this 2021 keynote paper in *Cognitive Processing* that the neuroscience of architecture is ready to move from anecdote and intuition to rigorous experiment — and they lay out the framework for doing so. Their argument rests on three converging developments. First, neuroaesthetics — the study of how the brain processes beauty and aesthetic experience — has matured enough to apply to built environments. The field can now ask not just "Do people prefer this building?" but "What neural systems are engaged when people judge architectural spaces, and do those systems differ for beauty, comfort, safety, and wayfinding?" Second, advances in mobile neuroscience (EEG, fNIRS) make it feasible to record brain activity as people move through real buildings, not just view photographs in scanners. Third, virtual reality now allows systematic manipulation of architectural parameters (ceiling height, material texture, spatial openness, lighting) while maintaining ecolo

papers.title: PDF-1320
papers.excerpt: Can you read a city with brainwaves? Panagiotis Mavros, Martin Zaltz Austwick, and Andrew Hudson Smith at the Bartlett, UCL, reviewed the emerging field of 'Geo-EEG' in this 2016 paper in Applied Spatial Analysis and Policy — the use of mobile EEG to study how urban environments affect cognition and emotion in real time. Traditional environmental psychology relies on surveys, interviews, and lab simulations. Mobile EEG lets researchers measure brain activity while participants actually walk through cities, crossing streets, entering parks, and navigating crowded plazas. The review covers the technical requirements (portable EEG headsets, GPS synchronization, artifact removal during walking), the relevant studies to date (emotional responses to green versus built environments, wayfinding and spatial cognition during navigation), and the methodological challenges (motion artifacts, limited spatial resolution, small sample sizes). The authors argued that mobile EEG has unique value: it captures the moment-by-moment neural response to specific places. For urban designers, the paper suggests a powerful tool: walk a proposed street design in VR with EEG and see which elements trigger str

papers.title: The color of calm: Red disrupts stress recovery while white and yellow compete for attention in the brain
papers.excerpt: The walls in your recovery space may be working against you. A 2025 study using neuroimaging and anxiety measurements in virtual reality environments reveals that wall color shapes the neural dynamics of stress recovery in ways that contradict the common assumption that neutral environments work best. The researchers confronted a foundational problem in environmental design: we lack quantitative neural evidence about how color choices during recovery actually affect the brain. Previous work relied on subjective ratings and behavioral outcomes, but the intermediate mechanism—what happens in the cortex when you sit in a red room versus a white one—remained opaque. This study exposed 38 participants to virtual rooms with different wall colors during three successive 90-second recovery phases following an induced stressor, while simultaneously measuring dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) activation using functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) and subjective anxiety using the STAI-5 scale. The results reveal a color-dependent choreography of neural states. Red wall exposure during early recovery elicited heightened DLPFC activation (M = 0.06) and significantly elevated subjec
papers.authors: Dehan Jia, Wenan Li, Xingkai Wang, Yuyang Jiang, Guobin Xia, Luwen Yu

papers.title: PDF-1323
papers.excerpt: Does a wooden room help you think? Sieun Kim at Yonsei University showed in this 2025 study in Scientific Reports that nature-inspired indoor environments — especially wooden interiors — enhanced EEG markers of relaxed attention and improved cognitive performance in VR. People spend 80-90% of their time indoors. If indoor design elements drawn from nature can reduce mental fatigue and sharpen focus, the practical value for offices, schools, and healthcare spaces is enormous. Thirty-six participants experienced four VR conditions: a control room, a room with curvilinear forms, a room with nature views, and a room with wooden walls. EEG recorded throughout. The wooden condition produced the strongest alpha-to-theta and alpha-to-beta ratios — neural signatures of relaxed but engaged attention. Participants in the wooden room also scored highest on standardized cognitive tasks. The curvilinear and nature-view conditions increased physiological arousal but did not consistently boost task performance. For interior designers, the study argues: natural materials may matter more than natural shapes or views for cognitive work. A wooden ceiling or wall panel may do more for focus than a wind

papers.title: PDF-1324
papers.excerpt: A woman sits in a laboratory, sensors taped to her forehead, while a projected image fills the wall in front of her. The wall shows vertical planks of Japanese cedar — pale, warm, and textured with grain. As she views it for ninety seconds, the oxygen concentration in her prefrontal cortex drops measurably below where it started. Masashi Nakamura, Harumi Ikei, and Yoshifumi Miyazaki found that viewing full-scale images of wooden wall surfaces reduced prefrontal brain activity and improved subjective mood — providing the first direct neurophysiological evidence that the visual appearance of wood as an interior material calms the brain in measurable, immediate ways. The question behind this study emerges from architecture and biophilic design: wood has been used as a building material for thousands of years, yet rigorous evidence for claims that 'natural materials are calming' has remained sparse. Previous studies by this group at Chiba University examined physiological responses to wood odor and touch, but the visual channel — the dominant sense in architectural experience — had not been isolated with neuroimaging. papers.title: PDF-1325
papers.excerpt: Prospect-refuge theory says people prefer spaces that offer a wide view (prospect) and a sheltered position (refuge). But how strong is the evidence? Annemarie Dosen and Michael Ostwald at the University of Newcastle reviewed quantitative studies in this 2016 meta-analysis in City, Territory and Architecture and found that the evidence is real but uneven. Proposed by Jay Appleton in the 1970s, prospect-refuge theory has become a cornerstone of biophilic design. Architects cite it when placing windows with panoramic views or creating alcove seating areas. But the theory was built on evolutionary argument and anecdote, not data. How well does it hold up under quantitative scrutiny? Dosen and Ostwald reviewed studies across natural, urban, and interior environments. For natural settings, both prospect and refuge were supported as drivers of preference. In urban contexts, prospect was consistently supported, but evidence for refuge was more neutral. In interiors, some degree of visual complexity was preferred, but mystery (the sense that more information lies ahead) showed mostly null results. The strongest support came from natural landscapes — exactly where most studies were conducte

papers.title: PDF-1326
papers.excerpt: Some buildings catch your eye and stick in memory. Others blur into the streetscape. James Rounds, Jesus Gabriel Cruz-Garza, and Saleh Kalantari at Cornell University showed in this 2020 study in Frontiers in Human Neuroscience that the brain's theta rhythm reveals which architectural features make a building a landmark. Urban navigation relies on landmarks — distinctive buildings that anchor mental maps. But what makes one building a landmark and another forgettable? Previous research used surveys and recall tests; Rounds's team went directly to the brain. Participants walked a virtual city via immersive VR while wearing EEG caps. The researchers varied architectural features of target buildings (facade pattern, height, twist, natural elements, color contrast) and measured posterior theta-band (4-7 Hz) synchronization when participants viewed each building. Highly salient buildings — those that contrasted sharply with their surroundings — produced stronger theta responses, longer gaze times, and better location recall. The features most associated with landmark status were rotational twist designs and natural elements (trees, gardens). Unusual facade patterns and extreme building

papers.title: PDF-1327
papers.excerpt: Human brains evolved on African savannas. Do the grid-form cities we build today fit the neural hardware that evolved in open landscapes? Gordon Brown and Charles Lee at Space Analytics and Louisiana State University asked this in a 2016 perspective article in Frontiers in Psychology. They argued that urban settlement patterns — especially the grid — may reflect evolved cognitive preferences rooted in hippocampal place cells, grid cells, and prospect-refuge heuristics. Cognitive neuroscience has mapped the brain systems that support navigation: hippocampal place cells fire at specific locations, entorhinal grid cells tile space in hexagonal patterns, and head-direction cells track orientation. These systems evolved in natural environments — savannas, forests, coastlines. Brown and Lee ask: do urban grids succeed because they align with grid-cell geometry? Do prospect-refuge landscapes (open views with nearby shelter) attract people because they match evolved survival heuristics? The paper proposes that grid-form settlements recur across cultures and centuries not by coincidence but because right-angle intersections simplify the cognitive map. papers.title: PDF-1328
papers.excerpt: Can a garden change brain activity in depressed people? Agnieszka Olszewska-Guizzo, Anna Fogel, Nicolas Escoffier, Angelia Sia, Kenta Nakazawa, Akihiro Kumagai, Ippeita Dan, and Roger Ho showed in this 2022 study in Frontiers in Psychiatry that it can: a therapeutic garden with contemplative features reduced mood disturbance and altered EEG and fNIRS patterns in both healthy and clinically depressed adults. Therapeutic gardens are designed to calm and restore. Contemplative landscape features — layered plantings, water, meandering paths, enclosed seating areas — are thought to promote reflection and reduce rumination. But does the effect show up in the brain? Ninety-two participants (24 clinically depressed, 68 healthy) visited three urban sites: a Therapeutic Garden (TG) with high contemplative landscape scores, a Residential Green space (RG), and a Busy Downtown (BD). EEG and fNIRS were recorded during passive exposure. The TG produced the lowest Total Mood Disturbance scores (p = 0.032). fNIRS showed lower frontal oxy-hemoglobin at the TG compared to BD, consistent with reduced rumination. papers.title: PDF-1329
papers.excerpt: When does office sound help and when does it hurt? Dan Chong, Wangling Zhu, Tao Liu, Huiying Hou, Ying Zhang, and Yuqiao Su showed in this 2026 study in Buildings that the effect of sound on work depends on both intensity and type — and that EEG reveals the neural pathways through which sound alters performance. Office workers face a constant acoustic environment: HVAC hum, keyboard clicks, conversation, music. Most guidelines set a single noise limit, but different sounds have different effects at different levels. The team tested three sound types (office noise, dialog, music) at three intensities (45, 65, 85 dBA) while participants completed standardized work tasks. EEG recorded brain activity throughout. Office noise and music impaired accuracy at moderate-to-high intensities, but dialog at moderate levels shortened reaction times. Tension-anxiety fully mediated the negative effect of noise on performance. For music, moderate intensity improved performance through reduced mental workload. EEG confirmed these behavioral pathways with distinct neural signatures. For workplace designers and facility managers, the study says: don't just limit noise — manage the type. Mask speech at

papers.title: People prefer fractals at mid-range complexity — and physical complexity drives the preference, not just scaling
papers.excerpt: Why do people find some fractal patterns beautiful and others not? Alexander Bies, Daryn Blanc-Goldhammer, Cooper Boydston, Richard Taylor, and Margaret Sereno at the University of Oregon addressed this question in a 2016 study in *Frontiers in Human Neuroscience*. The answer challenges the prevailing assumption in neuroaesthetics: people do not simply prefer mid-range fractal dimension. They prefer mid-range physical complexity — and fractal dimension is only one component of that complexity. The study used exact (mathematical) fractals rather than statistical (natural) fractals, allowing precise control over the fractal properties of each stimulus. Participants rated the aesthetic appeal of patterns that varied systematically in fractal dimension (D), recursion (the number of levels of self-similar repetition), and the number of distinct elements. The researchers measured which of these parameters best predicted aesthetic preference. The key finding: physical complexity — defined as the total number of elements in the pattern, which depends on both fractal dimension and recursion — predicted aesthetic preferences more strongly than fractal dimension alone. papers.title: PDF-1332
papers.excerpt: Can ten minutes in a room full of plants rewire a nurse's brain? ChoHye Youn, Minji Kang, and Juyoung Lee showed in this 2025 study in the International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health that brief exposure to a biophilic room reduced prefrontal cortical activity and shifted psychological state toward recovery in hospital nurses. Nurses face chronic occupational stress: long shifts, high emotional demands, and sensory overload. Break rooms are typically bare walls and fluorescent lights. Can biophilic design — vegetation-covered walls — deliver genuine neural and psychological recovery in just ten minutes? Twenty-one nurses spent ten minutes in each of two conditions: a plain white room and a room whose walls were entirely covered with living vegetation. Functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) measured blood oxygenation in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex throughout. The biophilic room reduced oxy-hemoglobin concentration in the DLPFC, a neural signature of reduced cognitive-emotional overload. Nurses also reported lower fatigue, depression, and anxiety; higher vigor, attentiveness, and perceived restoration. For hospital architects and facility managers,

papers.title: Aesthetic Complexity and Design Preference: Why Simplicity Satisfies—Up to a Point
papers.excerpt: What separates a room that feels alive from one that feels lifeless? The answer lies in complexity—but not in the way architects have traditionally assumed. A 2000 narrative review examining the aesthetic implications of complexity theory suggests that human preference for architectural and environmental form follows a curvilinear pattern: we like neither maximum simplicity nor overwhelming elaboration, but something in the middle. The mechanism turns out to hinge on how easily our minds process what we see. The core principle is known as semantic fluency—the ease with which we recognize and mentally represent visual information. When you enter a space, your perceptual system classifies what it encounters (symmetry, repeating elements, spatial hierarchy, color relationships) and organizes it into a coherent mental model. This fluency comes in degrees. Spaces organized around simple grids and clear geometric relationships are processed quickly and easily. Spaces crammed with visual noise, contradictory scales, and unresolved complexity demand more cognitive effort and feel uncomfortable. The key finding, synthesized across cited studies, is that preference peaks at moderate complexi

papers.title: PDF-1336
papers.excerpt: The lighting, the table setting, the background music — these atmospheric cues do not just set the mood in a restaurant. They generate positive emotions that mediate the relationship between perceived quality and behavioural intentions. SooCheong (Shawn) Jang and Young Namkung demonstrated this in a 2009 study in the *Journal of Business Research* using structural equation modelling to extend Mehrabian and Russell's stimulus-organism-response (S-O-R) framework to the restaurant context. The Mehrabian-Russell model (1974) proposes that environmental stimuli (S) produce emotional responses in the organism (O) — specifically pleasure, arousal, and dominance — which then drive approach or avoidance behaviours (R). Previous research had applied this model primarily to retail environments, showing that atmospheric variables like music, colour, scent, and lighting influence how long customers stay, how much they spend, and whether they return. But these studies focused narrowly on atmospheric stimuli and used generic emotion measures. Jang and Namkung extended the framework in two ways. First, they distinguished three types of perceived quality in restaurants: *product quality* (food attr

papers.title: PDF-1337
papers.excerpt: Walking into a mosque changes your brainwaves. Vickram Thevar Vijayan and Mohamed Rashid Embi used portable EEG to record brainwave signals from four participants at the Tuanku Mizan Zainal Abidin Mosque in Malaysia in this 2019 study in the *International Journal of Built Environment and Sustainability* — one of the first attempts to provide neurophysiological evidence for the phenomenological experience of sacred architectural space. The study draws on the philosophical tradition of phenomenology — Husserl, Merleau-Ponty, Pallasmaa — which argues that the quality of architectural experience cannot be adequately captured by verbal reports or questionnaires alone. The authors' ambition is to bridge the gap between phenomenological description (qualitative, subjective) and neuroscientific measurement (quantitative, objective) by using EEG to detect changes in brain states as participants move through an architectural space. Four participants wore portable Emotiv EPOC EEG headsets across three phases: *Phase 1* (before entering the mosque — exterior approach), *Phase 2* (inside the main prayer area), and *Phase 3* (after leaving the mosque — returning to the exterior). papers.title: Room size magnifies the stress of proximity — a proxemics principle for architects
papers.excerpt: The conference room is too small, the partner sits too close, and the meeting becomes stressful. This 1977 thesis by Marie-Louise Blanchet demonstrates a principle that Edward T. Hall's proxemics theory predicted but few architects have operationalized: room size and interpersonal distance interact to modulate psychological stress in dyadic interaction. The study measured how these two architectural variables—one fixed (room dimensions) and one behavioral (seating distance)—jointly produce the intensification effects of crowding that Blanchet terms "perceived spatial compression." Proxemics, Hall's framework for understanding how humans organize space during social interaction, posits that there are comfortable ranges of interpersonal distance for different relationship types and interaction contexts. The intimate zone operates at 0–18 inches; the personal zone from 18 inches to 4 feet; the social zone from 4 to 12 feet. But Hall's zones assume a normal-sized room. What happens when you compress those zones further by putting the dyad into a small space? papers.title: PDF-1339
papers.excerpt: Every environment produces an emotional response — and that response can be measured with three numbers. Albert Mehrabian and James Russell at UCLA proposed in this 1974 monograph in Perceptual and Motor Skills that all human reactions to environments reduce to three dimensions: pleasure, arousal, and dominance (PAD). How do you describe the emotional effect of a room, a park, or a street? Before Mehrabian and Russell, there was no standard framework. The authors drew on studies of synesthesia, physiological responses, and semantic differentials to argue that emotional reactions to any stimulus — regardless of sensory modality — can be captured by three independent axes. Pleasure: how pleasant or unpleasant the setting feels. Arousal: how stimulating or calming it is. Dominance: how much control you feel you have in the space. They developed self-report scales for each dimension and validated them across a range of environmental settings. The framework became the foundation for decades of environmental psychology research. For architects and designers, the PAD model offers a diagnostic tool: if a space fails, ask which dimension is off. A hospital waiting room that is unpleasant an

papers.title: Visual perception of pictorial space — how the brain constructs three dimensions from two-dimensional images
papers.excerpt: Why do pictures of rooms feel like rooms? The brain's ability to construct a three-dimensional spatial experience from a two-dimensional image is so effortless that we rarely notice it happening — yet it involves sophisticated visual processing that is directly relevant to how architectural representations (photographs, renderings, drawings) communicate spatial experience. This review examines the perceptual mechanisms by which observers extract depth, scale, and spatial layout from pictures. The mechanisms include monocular depth cues (perspective convergence, texture gradients, occlusion, relative size, atmospheric perspective) and pictorial composition cues (horizon position, ground plane extent, vertical position in the picture frame). Each cue provides partial information about the depicted spatial layout, and the visual system integrates them to construct a coherent spatial interpretation. The critical finding for architecture: perception of pictorial space is systematically different from perception of real space. Depth in pictures is compressed relative to real viewing — distances look shorter, rooms look shallower, and volumes look smaller. This compression varies with vie

papers.title: Depth perception from multiple sources: Occlusion dominates, but parallax, shading, and texture contribute independently
papers.excerpt: How does the brain decide which object is in front? Not from a single cue but from a hierarchy of depth sources — and this study mapped that hierarchy, finding that occlusion is the most effective source overall, exceeding all other depth cues in strength and reliability. The finding has direct implications for how architects design spatial layering, visual depth, and the perception of three-dimensional space in buildings. Depth perception is the visual system's solution to a fundamental problem: the retinal image is two-dimensional, but the world is three-dimensional. The brain must infer depth — which surfaces are nearer, which are farther, how far apart they are — from information sources embedded in the flat retinal projection. These sources include occlusion (one surface partially blocking another), motion parallax (nearer objects moving faster than farther objects during observer movement), binocular disparity (slight differences between left and right eye images), shading (luminance gradients indicating surface orientation), texture gradients (surface texture becoming finer with distance), and familiar size (known objects appearing smaller with distance).
